03 July 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing active threat today is the exploitation of a Microsoft SharePoint RCE vulnerability, flagged by CISA as now actively exploited in the wild, demanding immediate patching attention from FinServ firms running SharePoint. A new infostealer dubbed 'Djinn' is actively targeting cloud and AI credentials via a critical SimpleHelp authentication bypass, posing a direct risk to AI/ML infrastructure. The FCA has also published landmark crypto regulatory rules, setting binding standards for capital resilience, market integrity, and stablecoins. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CACEIS UK £31.7m Censure
— A major asset servicing bank was penalised for failing to act on financial crime red flags, highlighting custodian due diligence obligations relevant to all UK FinServ firms
🔗 View source
FCA Landmark Crypto Rules
— New binding rules on capital adequacy, stress testing, insider trading, market manipulation, and stablecoin standards will require compliance action from any UK firm offering crypto services
🔗 View source
CISA: Microsoft SharePoint RCE Actively Exploited
— An actively exploited high-severity RCE vulnerability in SharePoint represents a direct risk to FinServ firms using it for internal collaboration or document management
🔗 View source
Global Banks Back Open Source Resilience Alliance (OSERA)
— FINOS is forming a vendor-neutral coalition to secure open source components underpinning financial services supply chains, addressing a known sector-wide risk
🔗 View source
Visa Threat Intelligence Platform Launch
— Visa has launched a cyber threat intelligence platform for financial institutions, alongside live agentic commerce transactions in Europe, signalling growing operational AI risk in payments
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud and AI Credentials
— This infostealer, delivered via a critical SimpleHelp authentication bypass (CVE-2026-48558), specifically targets credentials used to access cloud services and AI/ML environments, threatening model integrity and pipeline security
🔗 View source
ATLAS: Data Exfiltration via MCP Server in Cursor
— A proof-of-concept attack showed that a malicious Model Context Protocol server can inject prompts into an AI coding assistant, causing it to exfiltrate agent configuration files and credentials via shell commands
🔗 View source
ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails through adversarial prompting or weight modification to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs, directly relevant to any FinServ deployment of customer-facing or internal LLMs
🔗 View source
ATLAS: AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
— Attackers acquiring publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can harvest API credentials, system prompts, and tool access details to escalate attacks against AI-integrated systems
🔗 View source
'Cordyceps' Malicious Pull Requests Threaten AI Developer Workflows
— A CI/CD pipeline weakness affecting Google's AI Agent Development Kit and other major projects allows malicious pull requests to compromise developer workflows, posing a supply chain risk to firms building or consuming AI tooling
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-54998 (CVSS 8.8)
— Incorrect authorisation in Microsoft Exchange Online allows an authenticated attacker to elevate privileges over a network, a high-priority risk for FinServ firms dependent on Exchange Online for communications
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-59093 (CVSS 8.8)
— A privilege escalation flaw in Weaviate (a vector database used in AI/ML pipelines) allows a low-privilege user to assign themselves the admin role, threatening any AI deployment using Weaviate for embeddings or retrieval
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-59095 (CVSS 7.7)
— A server-side request forgery vulnerability in LobeChat before 2.2.10 allows authenticated attackers to reach internal cloud metadata endpoints and steal cloud credentials, relevant to any firm running this AI chat platform internally
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-59092 (CVSS 7.7)
— An authentication bypass in JuiceFS exposes debug endpoints containing database credentials, granting attackers full read/write access to filesystem metadata without any authentication
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-59096 (CVSS 7.5)
— A host-header injection flaw in Dapr Sentry's OIDC discovery endpoint allows an unauthenticated attacker to poison JWT validation so that attacker-signed tokens are accepted, undermining zero-trust authentication in microservices environments. [https://
02 July 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML3 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threat today is the 'Djinn' stealer actively targeting cloud and AI credentials via a critical authentication bypass (CVE-2026-48558) in SimpleHelp, posing direct risk to financial services environments with AI/cloud integrations. MITRE ATLAS has published updated techniques covering LLM jailbreaks, agentic resource abuse, and AI agent configuration harvesting, reinforcing the breadth of the AI attack surface. On the regulatory front, the FCA has published landmark UK crypto rules and actioned CACEIS UK for weak financial crime controls, with a £31.7m client compensation. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CACEIS UK £31.7m censure for weak financial crime controls
A sub-custodian failed to act on clear warning signs about an unauthorised firm, resulting in client asset exposure — a direct reminder that third-party due diligence and Financial Services Register checks must be operationally enforced
🔗 View source
FCA landmark crypto rules
All UK crypto firms must now meet capital adequacy, stress testing, and market integrity standards including insider trading rules, raising the compliance bar for any FinServ firm with crypto or stablecoin exposure
🔗 View source
OSERA open-source supply chain alliance
Major global banks are backing a new coalition to secure the open-source components underpinning financial services, highlighting that supply chain risk in financial infrastructure remains a live and industry-wide concern
🔗 View source
Japanese firms (Aflac, Sapporo, KDDI) disclose cyber breaches
Multiple large Japanese financial and corporate entities have publicly disclosed data breaches, signalling an active threat actor campaign worth monitoring for TTPs that may spread to UK firms
🔗 View source
Biometric fraud and dark web identity cloning
Dark web access to fingerprints, IDs, and full biographies is undermining identity verification, directly threatening onboarding and KYC controls at UK financial institutions
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
'Djinn' Stealer targets cloud and AI credentials via CVE-2026-48558
This infostealer specifically harvests credentials linking AI development and admin environments to enterprise systems, making any firm with AI agent deployments an attractive target
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — MCP Server data exfiltration via indirect prompt injection (Cursor case study)
A proof-of-concept shows a malicious Model Context Protocol server can inject commands into an AI coding agent, causing it to exfiltrate agent configuration files and credentials — a realistic risk for any team using AI-assisted development tools
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — AI Agent Configuration harvesting
Adversaries are actively acquiring publicly exposed AI agent configuration files to extract credentials and understand tool capabilities, meaning any externally accessible agent config is an intelligence asset for attackers
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — LLM Jailbreak technique
Attackers can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification to trigger data leakage or malicious content generation, relevant to any firm deploying customer-facing or internal AI assistants
🔗 View source
'Cordyceps' malicious pull requests threatening CI/CD workflows
A campaign of malicious pull requests is exploiting CI/CD weaknesses across major open-source projects including Google's AI Agent Development Kit, meaning AI toolchain build pipelines are a live attack vector
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-48558 (SimpleHelp — critical authentication bypass)
Exploited in the wild by the Djinn stealer campaign to deliver credential-harvesting malware targeting cloud and AI environments; patch or isolate SimpleHelp instances immediately
🔗 View source
Multiple Adobe product vulnerabilities (arbitrary code execution)
Critical flaws in Adobe Campaign Classic and ColdFusion could allow full system compromise in the context of the logged-on user, affecting any firm using these platforms for marketing or web applications
🔗 View source
Multiple Mozilla Firefox and Thunderbird vulnerabilities (arbitrary code execution)
Exploitation could allow an attacker to install programmes, access or delete data, or create privileged accounts — a high-priority patch for any staff using Firefox or Thunderbird on corporate devices
🔗 View source
⚡ Analyst note

CVE-2026-48558 in SimpleHelp is being actively exploited to steal AI and cloud credentials, which in a financial services environment could directly compromise AI agent deployments or cloud-hosted trading and data systems — audit your estate today for any SimpleHelp installations and apply the vendor patch or enforce network isolation as an interim control. Separately, review whether any AI agent configuration files (system prompts, tool credentials, API keys) are stored in locations accessible from internet-facing systems or developer tooling, and rotate any credentials that may have been exposed. ---

01 July 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML3 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threat today is a cluster of critical SSRF vulnerabilities in IBM Langflow OSS (an AI workflow platform) that could expose internal cloud infrastructure and credentials, compounded by a new infostealer actively targeting AI and cloud credentials via a known authentication bypass. Aflac's disclosure of a breach affecting 4.38 million customers underscores continued risk to financial services from subsidiary-chain compromises. MITRE ATLAS has also published updated techniques and a case study highlighting how agentic AI systems and MCP servers are becoming active attack surfaces. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
Aflac Data Breach (Japan subsidiary)
— Attackers stole personal and bank account data of 4.38 million customers via a subsidiary hack, illustrating third-party and cross-border entity risk that is directly relevant to any UK insurer or financial group with overseas subsidiaries
🔗 View source
CACEIS UK Censured — £31.7m Payment for Weak Financial Crime Controls
— CACEIS UK failed to act on clear indicators that client assets were at risk, resulting in client losses; a reminder that sub-custodian due diligence and ongoing monitoring of third-party financial crime controls is a live regulatory expectation
🔗 View source
FCA Landmark Crypto Rules
— New FCA rules introduce capital requirements, stress testing, and market integrity obligations (covering insider trading and manipulation) for crypto firms, with specific stablecoin standards; UK FinServ firms offering or custodying crypto must assess compliance readiness
🔗 View source
FINOS Open Source Enterprise Resiliency Alliance (OSERA)
— Global banks are backing a coalition to secure open source software components underpinning financial infrastructure, reflecting growing recognition of supply chain risk in the sector
🔗 View source
Biometric Fraud and Dark Web Fingerprint Sales
— Dark web markets are selling biographies, IDs, and fingerprints, undermining biometric verification methods used in customer onboarding and identity checks at financial firms
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud and AI Credentials
— This infostealer, delivered via a critical authentication bypass (CVE-2026-48558 in SimpleHelp), specifically harvests credentials linking AI development and admin environments to wider enterprise systems, posing a direct risk to any firm running AI tooling
🔗 View source
ATLAS Case Study — MCP Server Prompt Injection Leading to Credential Exfiltration
— A proof-of-concept showed that a malicious webpage scraped by an MCP tool can inject prompts into an AI coding assistant (Cursor), causing it to execute shell commands and exfiltrate agent configuration files containing credentials
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-10564 (CVSS 8.2) — IBM Langflow SSRF via RSS and SearXNG Components
— The vulnerability can be triggered through prompt injection in agentic workflows, meaning an attacker who can influence AI agent inputs could pivot to internal cloud metadata services (AWS/Azure/GCP) and steal IAM credentials
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — LLM Jailbreak Technique (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries are documented as using jailbreaks to override LLM safety guardrails, enabling data leakage, harmful content generation, and discovery of system information; relevant to any firm deploying customer-facing or internal LLM tools
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
— Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose tool credentials, system prompts, and API keys, giving attackers a low-effort path to escalate access across connected systems
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-48307 (CVSS 8.8) — Adobe ColdFusion Reflected XSS
— A reflected XSS flaw in ColdFusion 2025.9 and 2023.20 and earlier can lead to arbitrary code execution in the victim's browser context; any firm running ColdFusion-based web applications should patch immediately
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-10129 (CVSS 8.5) — IBM Langflow SSRF Protection Bypass
— An authenticated low-privilege user can bypass SSRF controls using redirect-following to reach internal services and cloud metadata endpoints in IBM Langflow OSS 1.0.0–1.9.3
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-11714 (CVSS 8.5) — IBM WebSphere Liberty SSRF (apiDiscovery feature)
— WebSphere Application Server Liberty versions 17.0.0.3 through 26.0.0.7 are vulnerable to SSRF when the apiDiscovery-1.0 feature is enabled; widely used in enterprise Java environments including banking backends
🔗 View source
30 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threat today is active ransomware exploitation of the Windows BlueHammer privilege escalation flaw, confirmed by CISA, while the ShinyHunters group continues targeting Oracle PeopleSoft systems with demonstrated impact on insurance and automotive sectors. On the AI/ML front, a new infostealer ("Djinn") is actively harvesting cloud and AI credentials via a critical SimpleHelp authentication bypass, signalling that AI infrastructure is now a primary target for credential theft operations. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CACEIS UK / WealthTek Financial Crime Failure
— A UK asset servicing bank was censured and ordered to pay £31.7m after ignoring clear indicators that its sub-custody client was not authorised to hold client assets, a direct reminder that third-party due diligence and ongoing register checks are a regulatory obligation
🔗 View source
NAIC PeopleSoft Breach (ShinyHunters)
— The National Association of Insurance Commissioners confirmed ShinyHunters exploited a zero-day in Oracle PeopleSoft to steal configuration files and logs, a pattern directly applicable to any UK FinServ firm running PeopleSoft for HR or finance
🔗 View source
Nissan Employee Data Breach via Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day
— ShinyHunters used the same Oracle PeopleSoft vulnerability to exfiltrate current and former employee data from Nissan, demonstrating the threat actor is conducting broad campaigns against this platform
🔗 View source
FCA Landmark Crypto Rules
— The FCA has published new rules requiring crypto firms to meet capital adequacy, stress testing, and market integrity standards including anti-manipulation and insider trading provisions, creating direct compliance obligations for UK FinServ firms offering crypto services
🔗 View source
Windows BlueHammer Flaw Exploited by Ransomware
— CISA confirmed ransomware gangs are actively exploiting a Microsoft Defender privilege escalation vulnerability, posing a direct threat to Windows-based financial services infrastructure where endpoint protection tools are ubiquitous
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud and AI Credentials
— This infostealer, delivered via a critical authentication bypass in SimpleHelp (CVE-2026-48558), specifically harvests credentials for cloud and AI systems, threatening the API keys and service accounts underpinning FinServ AI deployments
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: Data Exfiltration via MCP Server in Cursor
— A proof-of-concept demonstrated that a malicious Model Context Protocol server can inject prompts into an AI coding assistant, causing it to silently exfiltrate agent configuration files containing credentials — a real risk for developers using AI tools in FinServ environments
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: AI Agent Configuration File Exposure
— Adversaries are acquiring publicly accessible AI agent configuration files to harvest credentials, tool access, and system prompts, making secure storage and access control of agent configs a critical defensive priority
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: Agentic Resource Consumption
— Attackers can use prompt injection or data poisoning to force AI agents into excessive, costly tool calls, draining API budgets and degrading availability of AI-driven services such as fraud detection or customer-facing automation
🔗 View source
Agentic AI Identity Problem
— AI agents operating across enterprise systems hold privileged access but typically lack the identity governance controls applied to human users, creating exploitable gaps that attackers are beginning to actively target
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-57999 (CVSS 8.8)
— Command injection in luci-app-tailscale-community allows authenticated users to execute arbitrary commands as root via improperly sanitised shell parameters
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-40521 (CVSS 8.8)
— Path traversal in FrontAccounting before 2.4.20 lets authenticated attackers upload PHP files outside the intended directory to achieve remote code execution
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-12240 (CVSS 8.0)
— The WordPress Export User Data plugin up to version 2.2.6 allows subscriber-level users to delete arbitrary server files, which can lead to remote code execution if wp-config.php is removed
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-40524 (CVSS 8.1)
— SQL injection in FrontAccounting before 2.4.20 allows users with analytics permissions to extract sensitive journal entry data through blind injection in the GL transactions function
🔗 View source

29 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threat today centres on AI agent security, with a demonstrated proof-of-concept showing that malicious MCP servers can be used to exfiltrate AI agent credentials via prompt injection in developer tools like Cursor. The FCA has censured CACEIS UK and secured a £31.7m payment for WealthTek clients, highlighting custodian-level financial crime control failures with direct relevance to UK FinServ firms. The MITRE ATLAS framework continues to document expanding adversarial techniques targeting LLMs and agentic AI systems, a growing concern as over 80% of financial services firms are reported to be adopting AI. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CACEIS UK FCA Censure — WealthTek Financial Crime Failures:
CACEIS UK failed to act on three separate checks showing WealthTek was unauthorised to hold client assets, resulting in a £31.7m voluntary client repayment and highlighting the risk of inadequate third-party due diligence in custodian relationships
🔗 View source
TrickBot — Banking Trojan (MITRE ATT&CK S0266):
TrickBot remains a well-documented C++ banking trojan, originally targeting financial institutions, and continues to underpin "big game hunting" ransomware campaigns across all sectors
🔗 View source
KDDI Data Breach — 14.2 Million Email Logins Exposed:
A breach of KDDI's email systems exposed credentials for up to 14.2 million accounts across six ISPs, posing credential-stuffing risks for financial services firms whose customers reuse passwords
🔗 View source
Biometric Fraud — Dark Web Access to Fingerprints and IDs:
Dark web markets are selling fingerprints, IDs, and biographic data, directly undermining biometric verification methods increasingly relied upon by financial services firms for onboarding and authentication
🔗 View source
OSERA — Open Source Supply Chain Resilience Initiative:
Global banks have backed a new vendor-neutral coalition (OSERA) to secure open source components underpinning financial services, reflecting growing awareness that supply chain risk in financial infrastructure requires coordinated industry action
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MCP Server Prompt Injection — Data Exfiltration via Cursor (MITRE ATLAS AML.CS0045):
A proof-of-concept showed that a malicious Model Context Protocol server can inject prompts into the Cursor IDE, causing it to execute shell commands that exfiltrate AI agent configuration files and credentials
🔗 View source
LLM Jailbreak (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054):
Adversaries can bypass LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or model weight modification, enabling data leakage or generation of harmful content from AI systems deployed in financial services
🔗 View source
AI Agent Configuration Exposure (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0002.002):
Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose tool access, credentials, and system prompts to adversaries, enabling further compromise of agentic AI deployments
🔗 View source
Agentic Resource Consumption (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0034.002):
Attackers can abuse agentic AI systems through prompt injection to trigger excessive API calls and tool use, exhausting budgets and degrading service availability — a relevant risk for AI-driven financial operations
🔗 View source
'Cordyceps' — Malicious Pull Requests Targeting CI/CD Pipelines:
A campaign of malicious pull requests is exploiting CI/CD workflow weaknesses affecting major projects including Google's AI Agent Development Kit and Microsoft's Azure Sentinel, threatening the integrity of AI development supply chains
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-13515 (CVSS 8.8):
A remotely exploitable stack-based buffer overflow in Tenda JD12L router firmware could allow attackers to take control of network devices; the exploit is publicly available
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-13516 (CVSS 8.8):
A second remotely exploitable stack-based buffer overflow in Tenda JD12L affects the Wi-Fi guest configuration function, with a public exploit already released
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-13487 (CVSS 7.3):
A publicly available SQL injection exploit targets SourceCodester Class and Exam Timetabling System 1.0, exploitable remotely without authentication
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-13551 (CVSS 7.3):
A remotely exploitable SQL injection flaw in itsourcecode Baptism Information Management System 1.0 has a public exploit and allows manipulation via the ID parameter
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-13552 (CVSS 7.3):
A remotely exploitable SQL injection vulnerability in itsourcecode
26 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML4 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threat today combines active account takeover campaigns exploiting a critical unverified password-change flaw in Flowise (CVSS 8.3) with multiple high-severity Vim vulnerabilities enabling arbitrary code execution. On the AI/ML front, a new MITRE ATLAS case study confirms that Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers can be weaponised for indirect prompt injection leading to credential exfiltration, a direct risk to firms deploying AI coding assistants or agentic workflows. The FCA has also signalled heightened expectations around AI governance in financial services, with its CEO calling for scale-up of responsible AI adoption. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CACEIS UK £31.7m FCA Censure
An asset servicing bank failed to act on red flags that WealthTek was unauthorised to hold client assets, resulting in a £31.7m voluntary payment — a reminder that sub-custodian due diligence is a live regulatory enforcement priority
🔗 View source
Account Takeover Webinar (BleepingComputer)
Attackers increasingly abuse legitimate sessions and trusted services to evade detection, making behavioural AI a growing component of ATO defences relevant to online banking and trading platforms
🔗 View source
Fingerprints for a Fee — Biometric Fraud
Dark-web access to cloned biometrics, IDs, and fingerprints is undermining biometric verification schemes used in financial onboarding, and human-layer controls are described as underfunded relative to tech spend
🔗 View source
Three Cybercrime-as-a-Service Operations Disrupted (Microsoft/Europol)
StealC, Amadey, and SocGholish malware infrastructure — commonly used for credential theft targeting financial sector employees — had over 300 servers taken down in a coordinated action
🔗 View source
FCA Speech — Rethinking Regulation for AI
The FCA CEO confirmed that over 80% of financial services firms are already adopting AI and that the regulator will treat AI governance as a core supervisory concern, signalling upcoming compliance obligations
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS Case Study — MCP Server Prompt Injection (Cursor)
A proof-of-concept showed that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into an AI coding assistant (Cursor) via a scraped webpage, causing it to execute shell commands and exfiltrate agent configuration files containing credentials
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
Adversaries can use adversarial prompting or model weight modification to bypass safety guardrails, enabling data leakage or generation of harmful content from LLMs deployed in customer-facing or internal financial services tooling
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
Publicly accessible agent configuration files can expose tool credentials, system prompts, and API keys, giving attackers a low-effort path to lateral movement within agentic AI deployments
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
Attackers can use prompt injection to coerce AI agents into excessive API calls, wasting compute budgets and potentially disrupting automated financial workflows dependent on those agents
🔗 View source
'Cordyceps' — Malicious Pull Requests in CI/CD Pipelines
A CI/CD workflow weakness has been found affecting Google's AI Agent Development Kit and Microsoft's Azure Sentinel among others, meaning AI tooling under active development could be poisoned at the build stage
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2025-71328 (CVSS 8.3)
Flowise before 3.0.10 allows any authenticated user to change their account password without supplying the current password, enabling full account takeover if a session is compromised — critical for any firm using Flowise to build AI workflows
🔗 View source
CVE-2025-71324 (CVSS 7.5)
Flowise before 3.0.6 allows unauthenticated path traversal via the `chatId` parameter, exposing the entire SQLite database including credentials to any external attacker with network access
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-57456 (CVSS 7.8)
Vim before 9.2.0699 executes attacker-controlled Python code during omni-completion if the buffer contains a crafted docstring, affecting any developer or analyst using Vim to edit untrusted files
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-57453 (CVSS 6.5)
Vim before 9.2.0678 allows arbitrary PowerShell command execution when opening a crafted ZIP archive, relevant to Windows-based developer and analyst workstations
🔗 View source
25 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML4 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing active threat today is the Cisco SD-WAN zero-day (CVE-2026-20245) being exploited in the wild to create rogue root accounts on network devices — a critical risk for any firm running that infrastructure. Microsoft and Europol have disrupted three major cybercrime-as-a-service operations (Stealc, Amadey, SocGholish), reducing but not eliminating those threats. On the AI/ML front, a newly documented MITRE ATLAS case study demonstrates how Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers can be weaponised to exfiltrate AI agent credentials via prompt injection, a direct concern for UK financial firms deploying agentic AI tools. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day (CVE-2026-20245):
Mandiant has detailed how attackers exploited this flaw to gain root access and create rogue accounts — any FinServ firm using Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN for branch or trading connectivity should treat this as an immediate priority
🔗 View source
Stealc/Amadey/SocGholish Takedown:
Microsoft and Europol disrupted over 300 servers across three cybercrime-as-a-service platforms commonly used to deliver banking malware and ransomware, though infrastructure remnants may still pose a residual threat to financial sector targets
🔗 View source
Mistic Backdoor Targeting Insurance and Professional Services:
A stealthy new backdoor linked to a ransomware access broker has been observed hitting the insurance sector, making it directly relevant to UK financial services and their third-party professional services suppliers
🔗 View source
FCA Warning on Misleading CMC Adverts:
The FCA has flagged a growing wave of fraudulent social media adverts by claims management companies impersonating independent advisors, posing reputational and consumer harm risks for financial firms whose branding may be misused
🔗 View source
Biometric Fraud and Verification Exploitation:
Dark-web access to cloned fingerprints and identity documents is undermining biometric verification systems used by financial institutions, with human-layer preparedness identified as a critical and underfunded control
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MCP Server Prompt Injection Leading to Credential Exfiltration:
A MITRE ATLAS case study shows a malicious MCP server scraping a webpage could inject prompts into Cursor (an AI coding assistant), causing it to run shell commands that exfiltrate AI agent configuration files and credentials
🔗 View source
LLM Jailbreak Techniques (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054):
Adversaries are actively using jailbreaks — via adversarial prompting or weight modification — to bypass LLM safety controls and extract sensitive data or generate harmful content, relevant wherever financial firms deploy customer-facing or internal LLMs
🔗 View source
Agentic Resource Consumption Attacks (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0034.002):
Prompt injection or data poisoning can coerce AI agents into making thousands of unnecessary API calls, burning API budgets and potentially causing service disruption — a tangible operational risk for firms using agentic workflows
🔗 View source
AI Agent Configuration Files as Attack Surface (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0002.002):
Publicly exposed AI agent configuration files can reveal tool credentials, system prompts, and access keys, giving attackers a low-effort route into wider infrastructure if agent deployments are not properly secured
🔗 View source
Generative AI Weaponised for Phishing and Malware (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0016.002):
Threat actors are using open-source LLMs hosted locally or in the cloud to generate phishing content, deepfakes, and malware, lowering the skill threshold for attacks against financial sector targets
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-56270 (CVSS 7.5):
A missing authentication flaw in Flowise before 3.1.0 allows unauthenticated attackers to retrieve full SSO configurations including OAuth client secrets in cleartext for Google, Microsoft, GitHub, and Auth0 — patch immediately if Flowise is used in any AI workflow
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56351 (CVSS 8.2):
SQL injection in n8n before 2.4.0 allows authenticated users with workflow creation rights to execute arbitrary database commands via MySQL, PostgreSQL, or MSSQL nodes — relevant to any firm using n8n for automated financial or data workflows
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56232 (CVSS 8.8):
A broken authorisation flaw in Capgo before 12.128.2 allows attackers to bypass API subkey scope restrictions, escalating to use of unrestricted parent keys — a significant privilege escalation risk in CI/CD or app delivery pipelines
🔗 View source

24 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threat today is the LastPass data breach via a supply chain attack on Klue, which exposed customer data from LastPass's Salesforce environment through stolen OAuth tokens — a direct risk for financial services firms using password management or CRM integrations. A MITRE ATLAS case study also highlights a live proof-of-concept where an MCP server was used to exfiltrate AI agent credentials via indirect prompt injection in the Cursor IDE. The FCA has published updated guidance on AI governance in financial services, reinforcing that existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR) apply to AI deployments now. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
LastPass/Klue Supply Chain Breach
Stolen OAuth tokens allowed attackers to access LastPass customer data from Salesforce, a risk pattern directly relevant to any FinServ firm using third-party SaaS integrations or password management tools
🔗 View source
FCA AI Governance Guidance
The FCA has clarified it will use existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR) to govern AI in financial services, meaning firms must evidence AI oversight and model monitoring under current obligations today
🔗 View source
Misleading Car Finance Claims Ads
The FCA is warning consumers about CMC-run social media ads impersonating impartial advice and misusing brand logos, a social engineering pattern that security teams should flag as a reputational and fraud risk
🔗 View source
Biometric Fraud / Dark Web ID Cloning

A proof-of-concept showed that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into Cursor IDE, causing it to execute shell commands and exfiltrate AI agent configuration files containing credentials

Adversaries can use adversarial prompting or model weight modification to bypass LLM safety guardrails, enabling data leakage or harmful content generation from AI systems used in FinServ

Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can reveal tool credentials, system prompts, and API keys, giving attackers a direct path into connected services

Researchers warn that self-adapting AI worms capable of seeking vulnerabilities and propagating through agentic AI environments are expected to emerge as an enterprise threat within a year

CVE-2026-7761 (CVSS 8.8)
Critical account takeover flaw in the Ultimate Member WordPress plugin (up to v2.11.4) via a password reset link disclosure chain, affecting any firm running WordPress-based portals
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-35018 (CVSS 8.8)
Authenticated remote code execution in NetComm NF20MESH routers (firmware R6B031 and earlier) via shell metacharacter injection, allowing full root access
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56785 (CVSS 8.2)
Stored XSS in FlatPress (pre-commit 10be83c) via comment and contact forms, allowing attackers to execute scripts in administrator browsers and potentially escalate access
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56784 (CVSS 8.1)
IDOR vulnerability in OpenRemote (before v1.25.0) allows authenticated users to delete alarm records belonging to other tenants, a cross-tenant data integrity risk relevant to shared-platform deployments
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-12095 (CVSS 7.2)
Unauthenticated SSRF in the Kargo Takip WordPress plugin (up to v1.2) leaks responses from internal services including cloud instance metadata endpoints directly to the attacker
🔗 View source

23 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The NCSC has issued a warning that AI is materially shifting the cyber risk landscape, urging leaders to act now, while researchers have demonstrated that AI agents using Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers can be exploited to exfiltrate credentials via indirect prompt injection. UK financial services firms should also note a critical hardcoded-credential flaw in IBM Storage Protect (CVSS 8.1) and two Picklescan bypasses (CVSS 8.1) that undermine ML model scanning pipelines. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA AI Governance Expectations
— The FCA has clarified it will use existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR) rather than new AI-specific rules to hold firms accountable for AI governance, model testing, and outcome monitoring

— Dark-web sale of fingerprints, IDs, and biographies is undermining biometric verification at financial institutions, with human vulnerability cited as a greater weak point than technology gaps
🔗 View source
Lloyds AI Agent Deployment for Scam Detection
— Lloyds Banking Group is deploying agentic AI for real-time payment scam detection across its 28 million customers, raising both capability and AI-system-integrity risks that peers should monitor
🔗 View source
Misleading Car Finance CMC Ads
— The FCA is warning consumers about fraudulent "money tips" adverts from claims management companies impersonating trusted brands, a social-engineering risk relevant to firms' customer-facing fraud controls
🔗 View source
NCSC AI Cyber Risk Alert
— The NCSC has published guidance stating that AI is actively shifting the cyber threat landscape and that leaders must act now to adapt their security posture
🔗 View source

MCP Server Prompt Injection & Credential Exfiltration (MITRE ATLAS AML.CS0045)
— A proof-of-concept showed that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into an AI coding agent (Cursor), causing it to execute shell commands that exfiltrate AI agent configuration files containing credentials
🔗 View source
LLM Jailbreak (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification to extract sensitive data or generate harmful content, a direct risk for any firm deploying customer-facing or internal LLMs

AI Agent Configuration File Exposure (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0002.002)
— Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose tool credentials, system prompts, and API keys, enabling attackers to pivot into connected services

— Attackers can use prompt injection to coerce agentic AI systems into excessive API calls, exhausting budgets and degrading service availability — a concern for AI-driven fraud or trading systems

🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-12628 (CVSS 8.1)
— IBM Storage Protect Client contains a hardcoded credential in its FlashCopy Manager authentication mechanism, allowing unauthenticated remote attackers to establish trusted sessions and access protected backup/recovery services
🔗 View source
CVE-2025-71339 / CVE-2025-71344 (CVSS 8.1 each)
— Two separate Picklescan bypasses allow attackers to craft malicious ML model files that execute arbitrary code when loaded, defeating a tool many teams rely on to validate third-party model safety
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56266 (CVSS 8.6)
— Crawl4AI before 0.8.7 has an SSRF vulnerability allowing unauthenticated attackers to bypass internal-address blocklists using IPv6-mapped IPv4 addresses to reach cloud metadata endpoints — critical if this tool is used in any internal AI/data pipeline
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56268 (CVSS 7.7)
— Flowise before 3.1.2 leaks full chatflow configurations, including system prompts and credential IDs, across workspaces to any holder of a valid API key, posing a serious data exposure risk for firms using this LLM orchestration platform
🔗 View source

22 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

A critical MCP server prompt injection attack demonstrated by Backslash Security shows AI development tools can be weaponised to exfiltrate credentials, posing direct risk to firms deploying agentic AI. Multiple privilege escalation and access control flaws in Capgo (a Supabase-based platform) could expose billing and organisational data if used in FinServ workflows. The FCA has clarified its AI governance expectations, relying on existing frameworks such as Consumer Duty and SM&CR rather than introducing new AI-specific regulation. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CVE-2026-56239 (CVSS 7.6) — Capgo Privilege Escalation via Billing RPC:
An authenticated user could invoke an unsecured Supabase RPC function to manipulate billing records or deplete credits for arbitrary organisations, a direct financial integrity risk for any FinServ platform using Capgo
🔗 View source
FCA: AI in Financial Services — Governance Expectations:
The FCA will apply existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR) to AI oversight, meaning UK FinServ firms must ensure AI models are documented, tested, and accountable under current senior manager responsibilities
🔗 View source
FCA: Misleading Car Finance Claims Ads:
Claims management companies are running deceptive social media ads misusing trusted brand logos, a fraud vector that could generate mis-selling liabilities or reputational damage for firms whose brands are spoofed
🔗 View source
Fingerprints for a Fee — Biometric Verification Fraud:
Dark-web availability of biometric data including fingerprints is undermining identity verification, a pressing concern for FinServ firms relying on biometric onboarding and KYC processes
🔗 View source
Ransomware Attack on Mackay Sugar (Gentlemen Group):
A highly active ransomware group shut down industrial operations at a major food producer, illustrating the operational disruption risk that similar groups pose to critical sector firms including financial market infrastructure
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
ATLAS Case Study — MCP Server Data Exfiltration via Cursor:
A proof-of-concept attack showed that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into Cursor to execute shell commands and steal AI agent configuration files containing credentials, a direct risk for any developer team using AI coding assistants with MCP integrations
🔗 View source
ATLAS Technique — LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054):
Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification to extract sensitive data or generate harmful content, relevant to FinServ firms deploying customer-facing or internal LLM tools
🔗 View source
ATLAS Technique — Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002):
Attackers can use prompt injection to force AI agents into excessive API calls, burning compute budgets and potentially disrupting automated fraud detection or compliance workflows
🔗 View source
ATLAS Technique — AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002):
Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, tool access, and system prompts, enabling attackers to pivot into connected services or harvest secrets
🔗 View source
Adaptive Agentic AI Worms — Emerging Enterprise Threat:
Researchers warn that self-propagating AI worms capable of exploiting vulnerabilities and adapting to new environments are likely to emerge within a year, with agentic AI deployments in FinServ being a high-value target
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2025-71348 / CVE-2025-71357 (CVSS 8.1):
Two separate picklescan bypasses allow malicious pickle files to evade detection and execute arbitrary code when loaded, directly threatening ML pipelines and model supply chains used in FinServ AI systems
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56242 (CVSS 7.5):
Capgo exposes an unauthenticated RPC function that allows attackers to confirm API key validity and map keys to user identities, enabling further lateral movement into organisational data
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56253 (CVSS 7.5):
Capgo's unauthenticated `get_org_members` RPC endpoint leaks member email addresses, user IDs, roles, and pending invitations using only a public key, posing a reconnaissance risk
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-12581 (CVSS 7.5):
EasyFlow .NET contains a session fixation flaw allowing unauthenticated remote attackers to hijack authenticated user sessions, risking unauthorised access to workflow systems used in operational processes
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-56382 (CVSS 7.2):
Craft CMS versions 5.5.0–5.9.13

Executive summary

A critical Splunk Enterprise vulnerability is under active exploitation with a CISA-mandated patch deadline of this Sunday, making it the most urgent action item today. A proof-of-concept attack demonstrated via a malicious MCP server shows AI coding assistants like Cursor can be weaponised to exfiltrate credentials through prompt injection, representing a notable AI/ML risk for firms adopting agentic development tools. The FCA has published guidance on AI governance expectations under existing frameworks, signalling increased regulatory scrutiny of how UK financial services firms oversee their AI deployments. ---

18 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The NCSC has confirmed that hostile states are responsible for three-quarters of cyber attacks on UK critical infrastructure, placing financial services firms at heightened risk. A zero-day in Check Point VPN is being actively exploited by a Qilin ransomware affiliate, requiring immediate attention from any firm running that product. On the AI front, a proof-of-concept attack via a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server demonstrates a credible new vector for credential theft from AI agent deployments. ---

5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln

Executive summary

The most pressing threat today is the unpatched Microsoft Defender "RoguePlanet" zero-day, which remains unresolved and poses immediate risk across enterprise environments including financial services. A cluster of high-severity vulnerabilities (CVSS 7.1–8.8) in the OpenClaw AI agent framework highlights serious risks for firms deploying agentic AI, including privilege escalation and authorisation bypass. The FCA's active engagement on AI governance in financial services underscores the regulatory pressure firms face to demonstrate controlled, well-monitored AI deployments. ---

Misleading Car Finance CMC Ads (FCA Warning):
Social media scam ads impersonating impartial advisors and misusing trusted brand logos could be used to harvest consumer data or facilitate fraud, a social-engineering risk relevant to firms handling motor finance complaints

Dark web availability of fingerprints, IDs and biographical data is undermining biometric verification controls that many UK financial firms rely on for onboarding and authentication

Cloud Security as a Strategic Priority for Financial Institutions:
Financial institutions expanding cloud infrastructure face growing attack surface risks that require security to be embedded at the strategic, not just operational, level

Microsoft Defender RoguePlanet Zero-Day (Unpatched):
With no patch yet available, endpoint detection on Windows systems used across financial services operations is potentially compromised, reducing visibility of active intrusions

IBM/Red Hat Open Source Security Clearinghouse (Major Banks as Early Adopters):
The $5 billion initiative to vet open source software security, backed by major banks, signals that supply chain risk in open source dependencies is now a board-level concern for financial institutions

🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054):
Adversaries can bypass LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification, enabling data leakage or generation of malicious content from AI systems deployed in financial services workflows
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: MCP Server Data Exfiltration via Cursor (AML.CS0045):
A proof-of-concept attack shows that a malicious webpage can inject prompts into an AI coding agent via an MCP server, causing it to exfiltrate credentials and configuration files via shell commands

MITRE ATLAS: Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002):
Adversaries can abuse prompt injection or tool data poisoning to force AI agents into excessive, costly API calls, threatening operational availability and API budget integrity

Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, system prompts, and tool access details, giving attackers a roadmap to compromise the entire agent deployment
🔗 View source
Lloyds Deploys Agentic AI for Real-Time Scam Detection:
Lloyds Banking Group's rollout of agentic AI for payment scam detection across 28 million customers illustrates both the opportunity and the expanded attack surface that agentic AI introduces into production financial systems
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-53843 (CVSS 8.8):
An authorisation bypass in OpenClaw before 2026.5.26 allows a revoked paired device to regain WebSocket node-level access, effectively making access revocation unreliable in affected AI agent deployments
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-53864 (CVSS 8.1):
Insufficient sanitisation in OpenClaw's host environment handler allows Node.js control variables from workspace `.env` files to bypass validation and influence child processes, enabling potential code execution
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-53849 (CVSS 8.1):
OpenClaw's Discord-based `allowFrom` access control uses mutable display names rather than immutable user IDs, allowing any attacker to impersonate an authorised identity by simply renaming their account
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-53840 (CVSS 7.1):
OpenClaw's MCP server forwards custom headers including API keys during cross-origin redirects, meaning a compromised MCP endpoint can silently exfiltrate authentication credentials to an attacker-controlled server
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-12398 (CVSS 7.5):
A command injection flaw in galaxy_ng's legacy role import API allows an authenticated user to achieve remote code execution on the Ansible Pulp worker

Executive summary

A MITRE ATLAS case study published this week confirms a real-world proof-of-concept attack where a malicious MCP server triggered data exfiltration of AI agent credentials via prompt injection in the Cursor IDE, representing an immediate risk for any firm deploying agentic AI tooling. The FCA has published updated guidance on AI governance in financial services, signalling increased regulatory scrutiny of how firms oversee and test AI models. Multiple publicly disclosed, high-CVSS buffer overflow vulnerabilities with public exploits were published today, requiring prompt patch assessment. ---

— The FCA has confirmed it will use existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR) rather than new AI-specific rules to hold firms accountable for AI oversight, model testing, and outcome monitoring — meaning gaps in AI governance are already a regulatory exposure

🔗 View source
CVE-2026-12218/12220/12221/12222 (CVSS 8.0)
— Multiple stack-based buffer overflow vulnerabilities with public exploits affect Yealink SIP-T46U VoIP phones across firmware, Bluetooth, and upgrade handler functions; vendors have not responded, so network segmentation of VoIP infrastructure is the immediate mitigation
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-12193 (CVSS 7.8)
— A heap-based buffer overflow in RevoUninstaller 2.5.x/2.6.x (RevoDetector.sys IOCTL handler) can be exploited locally with a public exploit available; upgrade to version 2.7.0 resolves the issue
🔗 View source
12 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

A critical Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day (CVE-2026-35273) enabling unauthenticated remote code execution is being actively exploited by the ShinyHunters group in data theft campaigns, making it the most urgent threat for organisations running this widely-used ERP platform. Multiple high-severity vulnerabilities in AI agent frameworks (OpenClaw) were published today, reinforcing a broader theme of insecure agentic AI deployments. The FCA has also published updated guidance on AI governance in financial services, signalling increased regulatory scrutiny of AI risk management. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CVE-2026-35273 — Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day RCE:
PeopleSoft is widely used for HR, payroll, and finance functions in UK financial institutions; active exploitation by ShinyHunters means unpatched systems face immediate risk of data theft
🔗 View source
Coupang $409M Data Breach Fine:
South Korea's record fine for a breach affecting 37 million customers is a sharp reminder that inadequate data protection controls carry severe regulatory and financial consequences for any firm handling large customer datasets
🔗 View source
FCA AI in Financial Services Guidance:
The FCA is actively engaging firms on AI governance, model testing, and outcome monitoring under existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR), meaning your AI deployments already face live regulatory expectations
🔗 View source
Misleading Car Finance CMC Ads — FCA Warning:
Claims management companies are misusing brand logos and posing as impartial advisers on social media, a social engineering vector that could be exploited to harvest consumer data or erode trust in legitimate firm communications
🔗 View source
Biometric Fraud and Identity Verification Headache:
Dark-web availability of fingerprints and identity documents is undermining biometric verification, a control many financial firms rely upon for onboarding and authentication
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054):
Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification, enabling data leakage or generation of malicious content from models your firm may rely on
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002):
Attackers can abuse prompt injection to force AI agents into excessive API calls, draining compute budgets and potentially destabilising automated financial workflows
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS Case Study — MCP Server Data Exfiltration via Cursor:
A proof-of-concept showed that a malicious webpage can inject prompts into an AI coding assistant, causing it to exfiltrate agent configuration files and credentials via shell commands
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002):
Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose tool credentials, system prompts, and API keys, giving attackers a ready-made foothold into connected systems
🔗 View source
Adaptive Agentic AI Worms Emerging as Enterprise Threat:
Researchers warn that self-propagating AI worms capable of adapting to new environments and exploiting vulnerabilities autonomously are expected to emerge within a year, raising the stakes for firms deploying agentic AI at scale
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-35273
(no CVSS published in articles): Oracle PeopleSoft unauthenticated RCE actively exploited in the wild; apply Oracle's mitigation immediately, particularly on internet-facing PeopleSoft instances
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-11774
(CVSS 7.6): Integer overflow in 389 Directory Server's SASL layer allows any authenticated domain user with a Kerberos ticket to trigger heap buffer overflow, leading to DoS or RCE in FreeIPA and Red Hat Identity Management environments
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-53814
(CVSS 8.3): OpenClaw privilege escalation allows a user with a valid hook token to invoke owner-only MCP tools, potentially enabling persistent system modifications in AI agent deployments
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-53811
(CVSS 8.8): OpenClaw privilege escalation via mutable Matrix display names allows authenticated users to hijack agent access permissions intended for other identities
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7870
(CVSS 8.8): IBM i (versions 7.3–7.6) unqualified library call allows a local user to run attacker-controlled code with administrator privileges, a high risk in mainframe-heavy financial services environments
🔗 View source
11 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML4 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threats today are actively exploited vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange Server (XSS zero-day) and the Langflow AI development platform (path traversal), both under live attack. A critical CVSS 8.8 RCE flaw in Splunk Enterprise via unsafe deserialization poses immediate risk to financial services firms using Splunk for SIEM/monitoring. On the AI/ML front, MITRE ATLAS has published new techniques covering agentic resource abuse and AI agent configuration harvesting, while Lloyds Banking Group's deployment of real-time agentic AI for fraud detection illustrates the sector's growing AI attack surface. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CVE-2026-20251 (CVSS 8.8) — Splunk Enterprise RCE via jsonpickle deserialization:
A low-privileged user can achieve remote code execution through the Splunk Secure Gateway app, directly threatening SIEM and log management infrastructure widely used in UK financial firms
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-20252 (CVSS 7.6) — Splunk SSRF via Dashboard Studio PDF export:
An attacker can bypass trusted-domain validation using subdomain spoofing to send server-side requests to internal destinations, risking exposure of internal network services in Splunk-monitored environments
🔗 View source
Microsoft Exchange Server zero-day (XSS, actively exploited):
Attackers can execute arbitrary JavaScript against Outlook Web Access users, a significant risk for firms relying on on-premises or hybrid Exchange for staff and client communications
🔗 View source
"The Gentlemen" ransomware group — rapid growth:
This group has become the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, with a 90% affiliate payout model accelerating recruitment, raising the likelihood of attacks against financial sector targets
🔗 View source
FCA warning — misleading car finance CMC ads:
The FCA has flagged a surge in deceptive social media adverts by claims management companies misusing logos of trusted bodies, a reputational and regulatory risk for firms whose branding may be impersonated
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
Lloyds Banking Group deploys real-time agentic AI for fraud detection:
Expanding agentic AI use across payment scam detection for 28 million customers increases the attack surface for prompt injection and model manipulation attacks described in MITRE ATLAS
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — AI Agent Configuration file harvesting (AML.T0002.002):
Adversaries can acquire publicly exposed AI agent configuration files to steal credentials and understand agent tool permissions, a direct risk for firms deploying LLM-based automation
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — Agentic Resource Consumption via prompt injection (AML.T0034.002):
Attackers can coerce AI agents into excessive API calls to drain budgets and degrade availability, relevant for any FinServ firm deploying cost-metered AI pipelines
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — MCP server used by Cursor exploited for data exfiltration (AML.CS0045):
A proof-of-concept shows that a malicious Model Context Protocol server can inject commands into an AI coding agent to exfiltrate credentials via shell execution, highlighting supply-chain risk in developer AI tooling
🔗 View source
Actively exploited path traversal in Langflow AI development platform (CVE-2026-5027):
Attackers are writing arbitrary files to servers running the Langflow AI platform, posing a direct risk to any development or production AI pipelines built on it
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-52751 (CVSS 8.8) — Ghidra unsafe deserialization, unauthenticated RCE:
Opening a maliciously crafted project file triggers a Jython gadget chain leading to arbitrary command execution, threatening any security or reverse-engineering team using Ghidra
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-10795 (CVSS 8.1) — UpdraftPlus WordPress plugin authentication bypass leading to RCE:
Unauthenticated attackers can forge RPC commands and activate malicious plugins, relevant for any firm-operated WordPress portals or client-facing sites
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-49498 (CVSS 8.8) — Ghidra SQL injection, privilege escalation to PostgreSQL superuser:
Authenticated users can escalate to database superuser via crafted usernames in password-change messages, risking full database compromise on shared Ghidra deployments
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-52754 (CVSS 8.8) — Ghidra PKI authentication bypass via null signature:
Any holder of a valid CA-signed certificate can impersonate other users and compromise shared reverse engineering repositories
🔗 View source
10 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML4 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday is the most pressing immediate concern, with three actively exploited zero-days (YellowKey, GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma) granting SYSTEM privileges or bypassing BitLocker, alongside a newly published RoguePlanet Defender zero-day released after patching. Critical RCE flaws in Veeam Backup & Replication, SAP NetWeaver, and Ivanti Sentry compound an already severe patching burden this cycle. On the AI/ML front, a real-world MCP prompt injection case study and a new authorisation bypass in the Mem0 AI memory platform highlight growing risks in agentic AI deployments relevant to financial services. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA Warning — Misleading Car Finance Claims Ads
CMCs and law firms are running deceptive social media promotions misusing logos of well-known bodies, which could expose customers of motor finance providers to fraud and create regulatory liability for firms whose branding is misused
🔗 View source
FCA AI Governance Engagement
The FCA is actively engaging firms on AI oversight, model testing, and outcome monitoring under existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR), signalling that AI governance will be assessed during supervision
🔗 View source
SAP NetWeaver & Commerce Cloud Critical Flaws
Four critical vulnerabilities patched in SAP's June 2026 update affect platforms widely used in financial services back-office and e-commerce operations, warranting urgent patching
🔗 View source
Veeam Backup & Replication RCE
A critical flaw in Veeam allows remote code execution on domain-joined backup servers, posing a direct risk to business continuity and data integrity at firms relying on Veeam for regulated data backup
🔗 View source
Check Point VPN Zero-Day Exploited by Qilin Ransomware
A critical Check Point VPN flaw has been under active exploitation since early May, with a Qilin ransomware affiliate linked to at least one confirmed incident — a direct threat to remote access infrastructure at financial institutions
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS — MCP Prompt Injection Leading to Credential Exfiltration (AML.CS0045)
A proof-of-concept showed that a malicious webpage can inject commands into an AI coding agent via an MCP server, causing it to execute shell commands and steal agent configuration files containing credentials
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-49948 — Mem0 Missing Authorisation (CVSS 8.1)
Any authenticated user of the Mem0 self-hosted AI memory platform can redirect all LLM and embedder traffic to an attacker-controlled server, with the malicious configuration persisting across restarts and affecting all users on the instance
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
Adversaries can use adversarial prompting or model weight modification to bypass LLM safety controls, enabling data leakage or generation of harmful content — a risk for any firm using LLMs in customer-facing or decision-making workflows
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
Attackers can manipulate AI agents into making excessive API calls or tool invocations via prompt injection, wasting compute budgets and potentially triggering denial-of-service conditions in production agentic systems
🔗 View source
Adaptive AI Worms Emerging as Enterprise Threat
Researchers warn that self-adapting AI worms capable of seeking out vulnerabilities autonomously are expected to become a live threat within a year, representing a novel and difficult-to-detect attack class for enterprise environments
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2025-YellowKey / GreenPlasma / MiniPlasma (Zero-days)
Microsoft patched two zero-days granting SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows and one enabling access to BitLocker-protected drives — all actively exploited prior to this month's Patch Tuesday
🔗 View source
CVE — Ivanti Sentry (Max Severity / CVSS 10.0 implied)
A maximum-severity flaw in Ivanti Sentry allows unauthenticated remote attackers to execute code as root, affecting a gateway product commonly deployed at the network perimeter
🔗 View source
CVE — Microsoft Defender RoguePlanet (Zero-day, post-Patch Tuesday)
A researcher published a new Defender zero-day granting SYSTEM privileges just hours after June Patch Tuesday, meaning fully patched systems remain exposed until a further fix is issued
🔗 View source
**CVE-2026-49948 (CVSS 8.1) —
09 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threat today is a critical Check Point Remote Access VPN zero-day (actively exploited since early May by Qilin ransomware affiliates) for which CISA has mandated federal patching within 3 days — UK financial services firms using the same technology should treat this as urgent. On the AI/ML front, new MITRE ATLAS case study evidence confirms that Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers can be weaponised for indirect prompt injection leading to credential exfiltration from AI agent environments. Multiple high-severity RCE vulnerabilities published today across widely-used infrastructure components add further pressure on patching teams. ---

— Claims management companies are running deceptive social media promotions misusing logos of well-known institutions, a tactic that could damage brand reputation and mislead customers of financial firms

— Financial institutions' accelerating cloud adoption is expanding their attack surface, making cloud misconfiguration and access control failures a growing operational risk

— CISA's emergency directive requiring federal agencies to patch within 72 hours underscores the severity of this vulnerability and sets a benchmark that regulated financial firms should match

MITRE ATLAS: MCP Server Data Exfiltration via Cursor (AML.CS0045)
— A proof-of-concept shows that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into an AI coding agent (Cursor) to execute shell commands and exfiltrate AI agent configuration files containing credentials, directly threatening firms deploying AI developer tooling

— Adversaries can bypass LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification to extract sensitive data or generate harmful content, relevant to any firm deploying customer-facing or internal LLMs

— Researchers warn that self-propagating AI worms capable of adapting to new environments and seeking out vulnerabilities could emerge as an enterprise threat within a year, with financial services' interconnected systems a likely target

— A Keycloak privilege escalation flaw allows a limited administrator to gain full realm admin rights by importing users with elevated role mappings, posing a serious identity and access management risk for firms using Keycloak

08 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML3 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing technical threat today is CVE-2026-49494, a CVSS 7.5 integer underflow in Comodo's firewall driver that allows unauthenticated remote exploitation via a single crafted IPv6 packet, bypassing firewall rules entirely. On the AI/ML front, a demonstrated proof-of-concept shows that Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers can be weaponised for indirect prompt injection to exfiltrate AI agent credentials — a direct and immediate risk for firms deploying agentic AI tooling. The FCA has also reiterated it will govern AI in financial services through existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR), signalling that accountability for AI governance sits firmly with senior managers today, not a future regulatory regime. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
CVE-2026-49494 — Comodo Inspect.sys Integer Underflow (CVSS 7.5):
Any endpoint within your firm running Comodo Internet Security is vulnerable to a pre-firewall remote exploit via a single crafted IPv6 packet, making this a critical priority for firms relying on Comodo for endpoint protection
🔗 View source
FCA AI Governance Guidance:
The FCA has confirmed it will use existing frameworks (Consumer Duty, SM&CR) to hold firms accountable for AI oversight, model testing, and outcome monitoring — meaning your firm's senior managers are already personally responsible for AI risk
🔗 View source
FCA Sanctions Controls Warning:
The FCA found repeated gaps in sanctions screening systems across 150+ firms reviewed, with £37bn in UK-frozen assets underscoring the stakes — weak controls remain a regulatory and financial crime risk
🔗 View source
Misleading Car Finance CMC Adverts:
The FCA is warning consumers about fraudulent social media adverts from claims management companies misusing logos of legitimate firms, which could expose your firm to brand impersonation and reputational damage
🔗 View source
IBM/Red Hat Open Source Security Clearinghouse:
IBM and Red Hat are committing $5bn to build an enterprise open-source security clearinghouse with major banks as early adopters, representing a significant supply chain security development relevant to FinServ technology teams
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MCP Server Prompt Injection — Data Exfiltration via Cursor (MITRE ATLAS AML.CS0045):
A proof-of-concept demonstrates that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into an AI coding agent (Cursor), causing it to execute shell commands and exfiltrate agent configuration files containing credentials — directly relevant if your firm uses AI development tooling

LLM Jailbreak (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054):
Attackers can override safety guardrails in deployed LLMs via adversarial prompting or weight modification, potentially causing data leakage or harmful content generation from AI systems used in customer-facing or internal FinServ applications

AI Agent Configuration File Exposure (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0002.002):
Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose tool permissions, credentials, and system prompts to adversaries, making secure storage and access control of agent configs a critical control gap
🔗 View source
Agentic AI Worms:
Researchers warn that self-propagating AI worms capable of adapting to new environments and seeking out vulnerabilities are likely to emerge as an enterprise threat within a year, with financial services' interconnected systems a high-value target
🔗 View source
AI Agent Identity Security Gap:
New research shows AI agent identities are proliferating across enterprises without adequate IAM governance, creating unmanaged non-human identities that represent a growing attack surface in FinServ environments deploying agentic workflows
🔗 View source

CVE-2023-54350 (CVSS 7.5):
WordPress Augmented-Reality plugin has an unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in its elFinder connector, allowing arbitrary PHP file upload and execution — patch or remove immediately if any public-facing WordPress instances are in scope

Multiple publicly-disclosed SQL injection vulnerabilities in SourceCodester Class and Exam Timetabling System 1.0 (login endpoints) allow remote, unauthenticated exploitation — relevant if any third-party or legacy scheduling applications share this codebase
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-11482 / 11483 / 11484 / 11485 / 11486 / 11488 (CVSS 7.3):
A cluster of publicly-disclosed SQL injection vulnerabilities across multiple archive and booking system endpoints in SourceCodester products are actively exploitable remotely — check your software inventory for any use of these open-source components

Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have issued a joint statement warning that frontier AI models now exceed skilled human cyber practitioners in speed and scale, directly raising the threat bar for UK financial firms. The NCSC has separately flagged active software supply chain attacks via compromised open-source packages, a risk amplified for firms with complex dependency trees. Multiple high-severity vulnerabilities published this week, including a CVSS 8.6 SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified CM, warrant immediate patch prioritisation. ---

FCA/BoE/Treasury Joint Statement on Frontier AI and Cyber Resilience

— FCA CEO highlights investment fraud powered by organised, cross-border criminal networks with average victim losses of £25,000, underlining the financial and reputational stakes for firms failing to detect it early

NCSC: Software Supply Chain Attacks — Check Your Dependencies
— Attackers are actively compromising open-source packages to distribute malware, a direct risk for financial firms relying on third-party or open-source libraries in trading, payments, or data platforms
🔗 View source
345 Days of Untested Exposure at a Bank
— A point-in-time penetration test leaves roughly 345 days of attack surface unvalidated each year, a critical gap as financial institutions' cloud and API footprints expand continuously

— With major banks as early adopters, this initiative signals sector-wide recognition that unmanaged open-source dependencies represent a systemic financial services risk

MITRE ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can bypass LLM safety controls via adversarial prompts or model weight modification to extract sensitive data or generate malicious content, directly relevant if your firm uses LLMs in customer-facing or internal tools

— A proof-of-concept shows that a malicious Model Context Protocol server can inject prompts into an AI coding assistant, causing it to execute shell commands and exfiltrate agent configuration files including credentials

5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have issued a joint statement warning that frontier AI models now exceed skilled human practitioners in cyber capability, directly raising the threat bar for UK financial firms. An actively exploited Oracle WebLogic vulnerability (flagged by CISA) and a VS Code zero-day enabling GitHub token theft compound immediate operational risk. MITRE ATLAS research highlights that AI agent deployments introduce new attack surfaces—from prompt injection via MCP servers to credential theft from exposed configuration files—requiring urgent governance attention. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA/BoE/Treasury Joint Statement on Frontier AI and Cyber Resilience
Regulators warn that frontier AI amplifies cyber threats at speed and scale, and firms that have underinvested in core cyber security fundamentals face the greatest exposure

FCA Financial Crime Conference — Evolving Threat Landscape
Organised criminal groups using advanced technology are driving investment fraud losses averaging £25,000 per victim, signalling that fraud prevention controls need to keep pace with professionalised adversaries

As financial institutions accelerate digital transformation, cloud misconfigurations and supply chain risks are increasingly material to operational resilience

CISA Flags Actively Exploited Oracle WebLogic Flaw

MITRE ATLAS — LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
Adversaries can override safety guardrails in deployed LLMs to extract sensitive data or generate harmful content, directly threatening any financial firm using LLMs in customer-facing or internal workflows

A proof-of-concept shows that a malicious MCP tool can inject prompts into an AI coding agent, causing it to execute shell commands and exfiltrate agent configuration files containing credentials
🔗 View source

Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, system prompts, and tool access details that adversaries can exploit for lateral movement
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
Attackers can use prompt injection to force agentic AI systems into excessive API calls, draining operational budgets and degrading service availability
🔗 View source
Securing AI Agents Before They Go Rogue
High-autonomy AI agents with broad permissions represent an uncontrolled risk vector that most enterprises have not yet addressed with adequate governance or least-privilege controls
🔗 View source

CVE-2026-49143 (CVSS 8.8)

A server-side request forgery flaw in Medplum before 5.1.14 allows authenticated users to exfiltrate cloud IAM credentials and patient records via crafted FHIR subscription endpoints

Unauthenticated SQL injection in ARMember Premium WordPress plugin up to version 7.3.1 could allow an attacker to extract sensitive database contents without any credentials
