02 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing active threat today is the exploitation of a critical Windows Netlogon RCE vulnerability, which demands immediate patching across all domain-joined systems. UK regulators (FCA, Bank of England, HM Treasury) have jointly warned that frontier AI models now exceed skilled-practitioner cyber capabilities and are actively amplifying threats to financial firms. Multiple new AI/ML-specific vulnerabilities and attack techniques have also been published, raising the attack surface for firms deploying agentic AI tools. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA/BoE/Treasury Joint Statement on Frontier AI & Cyber Resilience
— Regulators warn that frontier AI models are already outpacing skilled human attackers in speed and scale, and firms with weak cyber fundamentals face elevated regulatory and operational risk
🔗 View source
FCA Financial Crime Conference — Evolving Threat Landscape
— FCA CEO Nikhil Rathi highlighted that organised criminal networks using technology are driving investment fraud losses averaging £25,000 per victim, signalling that UK financial firms remain priority targets
🔗 View source
Critical Windows Netlogon RCE Now Exploited in Attacks
— Active exploitation of this recently patched critical vulnerability poses direct risk to financial institutions running Windows domain infrastructure
🔗 View source
NIST NVD Backlog Undermining Vulnerability Management
— With over 27,000 unprocessed CVEs in the NVD backlog, financial firms relying solely on NVD for vulnerability prioritisation may be operating with significant blind spots
🔗 View source
IBM/Red Hat Open Source Security Clearinghouse — Banks as Early Adopters
— Top banks are joining a $5 billion initiative to improve open source software security, reflecting the growing supply-chain risk to financial services from unvetted open source components
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
ATLAS Case Study: Data Exfiltration via MCP Server in Cursor
— A proof-of-concept demonstrates that a malicious Model Context Protocol (MCP) server can inject prompts into an AI coding agent, causing it to execute shell commands and exfiltrate agent configuration files containing credentials
🔗 View source
ATLAS Technique: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or model weight modification, enabling data leakage or generation of harmful content from firm-deployed AI tools
🔗 View source
ATLAS Technique: AI Agent Configuration File Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
— Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, tool access, and system prompts, giving attackers a direct path into connected services
🔗 View source
'Claw Chain' Vulnerabilities in OpenClaw AI Agent Framework
— Now-patched flaws in this growing AI agent framework allowed attackers to steal credentials, escalate privileges, and maintain persistence — a pattern likely to recur in other rapidly adopted agent frameworks
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-49121 (CVSS 8.1) — AI Tensor Engine for ROCm (AITER) Unauthenticated RCE
— An unauthenticated remote code execution flaw via malicious pickle payloads over ZMQ allows attackers to compromise all inference worker processes simultaneously in AI/ML compute clusters
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-43623 (CVSS 8.8)
— A stack-based buffer overflow in microtar's `raw_to_header()` function allows arbitrary stack memory corruption via a crafted TAR archive, risking code execution in any application processing untrusted archives
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-43624 (CVSS 8.2)
— A path traversal in F5-TTS allows unauthenticated attackers to write arbitrary files anywhere on the filesystem by supplying unsanitised project names to the fine-tuning interface
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-49121 (CVSS 8.1)
— AI Tensor Engine for ROCm (AITER) accepts unauthenticated pickle payloads over ZMQ, enabling remote code execution across all connected ML inference workers simultaneously
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-10280 (CVSS 7.3)
— An SSRF vulnerability in the mcpilot MCP API Call Endpoint allows remote attackers to manipulate the `serverBaseUrl` parameter; a public exploit exists and the vendor has not yet responded
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-43958 (CVSS 7.8)
— A stack-based buffer overflow in rrdcached (rrdtool) can be exploited locally via an oversized CREATE request, leading to daemon crash or arbitrary code execution affecting monitoring infrastructure
🔗 View source
01 June 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have jointly warned that frontier AI models already exceed skilled human cyber capability, posing a direct and growing threat to UK financial firms' safety and operational resilience. A newly documented MITRE ATLAS case study demonstrates how Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers can be weaponised via prompt injection to exfiltrate AI agent credentials. UK financial firms should treat both developments as requiring immediate review of AI deployment controls and core cyber hygiene investment. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA/BoE/Treasury Joint Statement on Frontier AI and Cyber Resilience
— UK regulators explicitly state frontier AI capabilities already surpass skilled human attackers in speed and scale, and firms with weak cyber foundations are most exposed; this is a direct supervisory signal
🔗 View source
FCA Financial Crime Conference — Evolving Threat Landscape
— FCA CEO Nikhil Rathi highlighted that organised, cross-border criminal groups are using technology at speed to commit investment fraud averaging £25,000 loss per victim, signalling increased AI-enabled fraud risk for retail financial firms
🔗 View source
Microsoft Zero-Day Releases Dispute
— A researcher published working proof-of-concept exploit code publicly on GitHub for Microsoft vulnerabilities, meaning attackers and defenders have simultaneous access, raising immediate patch prioritisation pressure for firms running Microsoft infrastructure
🔗 View source
Carnival Data Breach — 6 Million Records
— A threat actor compromised a single employee account to access and copy personal data belonging to nearly 6 million people, a reminder that account takeover via credential compromise remains a primary initial access vector relevant to financial services customer data programmes
🔗 View source
IBM/Red Hat Open Source Security Clearinghouse
— IBM and Red Hat are committing $5 billion to an enterprise open source software security clearinghouse with major banks as early adopters, directly relevant to software supply chain risk management at UK financial institutions
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS Case Study — MCP Server Data Exfiltration via Cursor
— A proof-of-concept attack shows that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into an AI coding agent (Cursor) to execute shell commands and steal agent configuration files containing credentials, a direct threat to firms using AI coding or agentic tools
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification, enabling data leakage, harmful content generation, and evasion of controls that firms rely on in customer-facing or internal AI deployments
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
— Attackers can coerce AI agents into excessive tool calls via prompt injection, wasting API budgets and potentially causing service disruption, a risk for firms deploying autonomous AI agents in operations or customer service
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
— Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, tool access, and system prompts to adversaries, making secure storage and access control of agent configs a critical hygiene requirement
🔗 View source
'Claw Chain' Vulnerabilities in OpenClaw AI Agent Framework
— Now-patched vulnerabilities in the OpenClaw AI agent framework allowed credential theft, privilege escalation, and persistence, illustrating that rapidly adopted AI frameworks carry serious unvetted security risk
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-10181 (CVSS 8.8)
— Remotely exploitable stack-based buffer overflow in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP (end-of-life since 2009, no patch will be issued); any firm still running this device should treat it as unmanaged risk and isolate or replace it immediately
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-10189 (CVSS 8.8)
— Remotely exploitable stack-based buffer overflow in Tenda W12 router firmware, with a public exploit available, posing a network perimeter risk if deployed in any branch or office environment
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-49489 (CVSS 8.5)
— Authenticated SQL injection in OpenCATS applicant tracking system allows extraction of full database contents via time-based blind injection; firms using OpenCATS for HR recruitment should patch or take offline promptly
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-49490 (CVSS 8.1)
— A second authenticated SQL injection in OpenCATS DataGrid filter handling allows arbitrary SQL execution, compounding the risk from CVE-2026-49489 for any OpenCATS deployment
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-10214 (CVSS 7.3)
— OS command injection in the chatgpt-on-wechat AI agent framework (Bash Tool component) is remotely

The FCA, Bank of England, and Treasury have issued a joint statement warning that frontier AI models now exceed skilled practitioner cyber capabilities and directly amplify threats to financial firms' safety and stability. Separately, active exploitation of a FortiClient EMS authentication bypass is delivering credential-stealing malware, posing an immediate risk to enterprise environments. Multiple critical Oracle E-Business Suite vulnerabilities — including two scoring 8.8 — affecting Payroll and Financials products demand urgent patching attention from any firm running Oracle EBS. ---

The FCA, Bank of England, and Treasury have jointly warned that frontier AI models now exceed skilled-practitioner cyber capability, directly raising the threat bar for UK financial firms. Multiple MITRE ATLAS entries published this cycle highlight escalating AI agent attack techniques, including prompt injection via MCP servers leading to credential theft. Separately, exploits now account for 31% of breach initial access per Verizon DBIR 2026, underscoring the urgency of faster patching. ---

— FCA CEO highlighted that investment fraud losses average £25,000 per victim and that organised criminal groups are using technology to scale attacks across borders, directly threatening retail customers of UK financial firms

— A privilege escalation flaw in Keycloak's Fine-Grained Admin Permissions allows a limited admin to inject highly privileged roles into user authentication tokens, threatening identity governance in firms using Keycloak for SSO

CVE-2024-56462 (CVSS 7.2)
— IBM QRadar (up to 7.5.0 UP15 Interim Fix 002) allows a privileged user to restore a malicious backup archive to gain OS-level access, directly threatening SIEM integrity for firms relying on QRadar for security monitoring

27 May 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have jointly warned that frontier AI models now exceed skilled human cyber practitioners in speed and scale, representing a direct and escalating threat to UK financial firms' operational resilience. Separately, the FBI has issued an alert on Kali365, a Telegram-based phishing-as-a-service targeting Microsoft 365 OAuth tokens — a critical risk for any firm relying on M365. MITRE ATLAS has documented several AI agent attack techniques, including credential theft via MCP server prompt injection, reinforcing that AI tooling is rapidly becoming a live attack surface. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA/BoE/HM Treasury Joint Statement on Frontier AI and Cyber Resilience
— Regulators explicitly state that frontier AI capabilities amplify cyber threats to firms' safety, soundness, and market integrity, and that firms underinvesting in core cyber security fundamentals face heightened exposure
🔗 View source
FBI Warning: Kali365 Phishing-as-a-Service
— This Telegram-based service enables criminals to capture legitimate OAuth tokens granting broad access to Microsoft 365 environments, posing a direct account-takeover risk to financial firms using M365
🔗 View source
FCA Speech: Working Together Against Financial Crime
— The FCA CEO highlighted that organised criminal groups are running cross-border professional networks, with investment fraud victims losing an average of £25,000, signalling increased regulatory pressure on firms' fraud controls
🔗 View source
NCSC Guidance: Designing Secure Access with ZTNA
— New NCSC guidance on Zero Trust Network Access architectures is directly relevant to financial firms moving away from legacy perimeter-based trust models
🔗 View source
Charter Communications Data Breach (ShinyHunters)
— ShinyHunters' extortion of a major telecoms provider is a reminder that third-party and supply-chain partners represent a live data breach pathway into financial firms
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS: Data Exfiltration via MCP Server (Cursor Case Study)
— A proof-of-concept demonstrated that a malicious webpage can inject prompts into an AI coding agent via an MCP tool, causing it to execute shell commands and exfiltrate agent configuration files containing credentials
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or model weight modification to extract sensitive data or generate malicious content, a direct risk to firms deploying AI assistants with access to internal data
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
— Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, tool access, and system prompts to adversaries, enabling further lateral movement or data theft
🔗 View source
'Claw Chain' Vulnerabilities in OpenClaw AI Agent Framework
— Now-patched flaws in this AI agent framework allowed attackers to steal credentials, escalate privileges, and maintain persistence, illustrating that AI frameworks introduce conventional software vulnerabilities at scale
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
— Attackers can use prompt injection to coerce AI agents into excessive, costly API calls, draining budgets and degrading service availability — a concern for firms with AI agents connected to paid external APIs
🔗 View source

— An authenticated user in Snipe-IT (IT asset management, fixed in v8.4.1) can escalate privileges to admin via a crafted API request, posing a risk if your firm uses this tool for licence or asset tracking

CVE-2026-8834 (CVSS 8.0)
— A buffer overflow in IBM HTTP Server 8.5 and 9.0 allows a privileged authenticated user to execute remote code or cause denial of service against the Administration Server
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-4480 (CVSS 8.5)
— A flaw in Samba's printing subsystem allows a remote attacker to achieve remote code execution by sending a crafted print job description containing unescaped shell characters
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-42785 (CVSS 7.2)
— Authenticated administrators in OpenKM 6.3.12 can execute arbitrary operating system commands via the /admin/Scripting endpoint, enabling full server compromise if admin credentials are obtained
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-9550 (CVSS 7.3)
— A path traversal vulnerability in Acrel Electrical EEMS Power Management Platform 1.3.0 is publicly disclosed and exploitable remotely, with no vendor response received — relevant to firms with operational technology or building management systems

Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have jointly warned that frontier AI models now exceed skilled practitioner cyber capabilities, directly raising the threat bar for UK financial firms. Separately, the FBI has flagged Kali365, a Telegram-based phishing-as-a-service actively targeting Microsoft 365 environments via OAuth token theft. MITRE ATLAS case study evidence confirms AI agent deployments are being actively exploited via prompt injection through MCP servers, compounding both threats. ---

🏭 FinServ threats

Regulators explicitly warn that frontier AI amplifies cyber threats to safety, soundness, and market integrity — firms underinvesting in core cyber hygiene face increased exposure as model capabilities accelerate

FCA CEO Nikhil Rathi highlighted that investment fraud losses average £25,000 per victim, driven by increasingly organised, cross-border criminal networks using technology at scale

MITRE ATLAS Case Study — MCP Server Data Exfiltration via Cursor
A proof-of-concept demonstrated that a malicious webpage can inject prompts into an AI coding agent (Cursor) via an MCP server, causing it to exfiltrate credential-containing configuration files
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
Adversaries can bypass LLM safety guardrails through adversarial prompting or weight modification, enabling data leakage or generation of malicious content — a direct risk for any firm deploying customer-facing or internal LLMs
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS — AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can reveal credentials, tool access, and system prompts, giving attackers a low-effort route into AI-enabled systems
🔗 View source
'Claw Chain' Vulnerabilities in OpenClaw AI Agent Framework
Now-patched flaws allowed attackers to steal credentials, escalate privileges, and maintain persistence within AI agent deployments — highlighting that agent frameworks carry traditional software vulnerability risks
🔗 View source
AI Agents Creating New Identity and Access Management Challenges
Proliferating AI agent identities within enterprises require dedicated governance and security budgeting separate from traditional IAM, according to new Omdia research
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
Drupal SQL Injection (CISA KEV — no CVE specified in article)
CVSS not stated; actively exploited vulnerability in Drupal CMS, with CISA mandating federal patching — financial firms using Drupal should patch immediately
🔗 View source
Windows Zero-Days — YellowKey, GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma
No CVSS stated; three new Windows vulnerabilities disclosed by a security researcher add to a growing post-Patch Tuesday zero-day backlog affecting enterprise Windows environments
🔗 View source
Verizon DBIR 2026 — Exploit-Based Initial Access at 31%
Not a single CVE; the DBIR finding that exploits now account for 31% of breach initial access vectors signals that slow patch cycles are materially increasing organisational risk
🔗 View source
OpenClaw 'Claw Chain' Vulnerabilities (now patched)
No CVSS stated; chained flaws in the OpenClaw AI agent framework enabled credential theft, privilege escalation, and persistence — organisations running this framework should confirm patched versions are deployed
🔗 View source
⚡ Analyst note

Given the FBI's active warning on Kali365 OAuth token theft targeting Microsoft 365, you should audit your M365 tenant today for any anomalous OAuth application consents and enforce Conditional Access policies that block token replay — particularly for privileged accounts. The FCA/BoE joint statement makes clear that regulators will scrutinise cyber fundamentals as AI threats intensify, so documenting this review now also supports your operational resilience ---

Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and Treasury have issued a joint statement warning that frontier AI models already exceed skilled human cyber capabilities in speed and scale, posing a direct and escalating threat to UK financial firms. A critical SQL injection flaw in Ghost CMS (CVE-2026-26980) is being actively exploited in large-scale ClickFix campaigns, and the FBI has warned of a Telegram-based phishing-as-a-service (Kali365) targeting Microsoft 365 environments via OAuth token theft. AI agent security is an emerging priority, with new MITRE ATLAS case studies and patched vulnerabilities in AI frameworks highlighting credential theft and prompt injection risks. ---

🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-26980
— Ghost CMS critical SQL injection flaw; actively exploited in a large-scale campaign to deliver malicious JavaScript via ClickFix attack chains, patch immediately if running Ghost CMS
🔗 View source
Windows Zero-Days: YellowKey, GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma
— Three new Windows vulnerabilities disclosed by a researcher in the past six weeks, adding to post-Patch Tuesday risk and requiring prompt assessment of exposure
🔗 View source
Verizon DBIR 2026: Exploitation Now Drives 31% of Initial Breaches
— The report highlights that vulnerability exploitation is increasingly the primary initial access vector, with enterprise patching cycles too slow to keep pace with attacker timelines
🔗 View source
⚡ Analyst note

Given the active exploitation of Kali365's OAuth token-harvesting capability and the FBI advisory issued this week, you should immediately review your Microsoft 365 conditional access policies and confirm that OAuth app consent is restricted to approved applications only — block user-level consent for third-party apps if not already enforced. The FCA/BoE/Treasury joint statement also signals that regulators will scrutinise cyber fundamentals more closely, so use this as justification to prioritise any outstanding patching backlog, particularly for internet-facing systems, before your next audit cycle. ---

22 May 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have issued a joint statement warning that frontier AI models already exceed skilled human cyber capabilities and pose a direct, escalating threat to financial firms' safety and stability. Two critical privilege escalation flaws in LiteLLM (CVE-2026-47101, CVE-2026-47102, CVSS 8.8) allow authenticated users to gain full admin access to AI proxy infrastructure, a significant risk for any firm using LiteLLM in production. A MITRE ATLAS case study also demonstrates live credential exfiltration via prompt injection through MCP servers used in AI coding tools. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA/BoE/HMT Joint Statement on Frontier AI and Cyber Resilience
— Regulators explicitly warn that firms with weak cyber fundamentals face amplified risk as AI lowers the cost and raises the scale of attacks against financial institutions
🔗 View source
FCA Financial Crime Conference — Evolving Threat Landscape
— FCA CEO highlights that organised criminal groups are running cross-border, technologically-enabled financial crime operations with average investment fraud losses of £25,000 per victim, directly relevant to UK retail and wealth management firms
🔗 View source
First VPN Seizure — Ransomware Infrastructure Dismantled
— European law enforcement seized First VPN, a service actively used to anonymise ransomware and data theft attacks, reducing one evasion tool available to threat actors targeting financial services
🔗 View source
Cisco Secure Workload Max-Severity Flaw
— A maximum-severity vulnerability allows attackers to obtain Site Admin privileges on Cisco Secure Workload, which is used in enterprise network segmentation environments common in financial infrastructure
🔗 View source
National Bank of Canada Invests in Sardine AI Fraud Platform
— A $25M Series C extension signals growing institutional confidence in AI-driven fraud and compliance tooling, relevant as UK firms evaluate similar vendor solutions
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS: Data Exfiltration via MCP Server in Cursor
— A proof-of-concept shows that a malicious webpage can inject prompts into an AI coding agent via an MCP tool, causing it to exfiltrate credential-containing configuration files via shell commands
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-47102 (CVSS 8.8) — LiteLLM Role Escalation via /user/update
— Any authenticated LiteLLM user can set their own role to proxy_admin through an unsanitised endpoint, granting full access to all models, keys, users, and prompt history
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-47101 (CVSS 8.8) — LiteLLM Route Permission Bypass
— Authenticated internal users can create API keys granting access to admin-only routes, bypassing role-based access controls entirely
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or model weight modification, enabling data leakage or generation of malicious content from deployed models
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: AI Agent Configuration File Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
— Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can reveal credentials, system prompts, and tool access details that adversaries can exploit to pivot into connected services
🔗 View source

CVE-2026-9018 (CVSS 8.8)
— Unauthenticated attackers can register accounts with full WordPress administrator privileges via an unvalidated user meta write in the Easy Elements for Elementor plugin (versions ≤ 1.4.5)

CVE-2026-48235 (CVSS 8.2)
— Open ISES Tickets before 3.44.2 allows SQL injection via a compromised GPS tracking endpoint, enabling an attacker to manipulate database records through unsanitised external data
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-4834 (CVSS 7.5)
— The WP ERP Pro WordPress plugin (≤ 1.5.1) is vulnerable to unauthenticated SQL injection via the search_key parameter, allowing extraction of sensitive database contents

CVE-2025-13477 (CVSS 7.1)
— WifiBurada exposes private personal information and insufficiently protected credentials, allowing authentication bypass with no vendor response to disclosure

21 May 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and Treasury have issued a joint statement warning that frontier AI models already exceed skilled human cyber capabilities, directly amplifying threats to UK financial firms' safety and operational resilience. Separately, two Microsoft Defender zero-days are being actively exploited in the wild, increasing endpoint risk across the sector. An MCP-based prompt injection proof-of-concept and newly catalogued AI agent attack techniques highlight a maturing threat landscape for firms deploying AI tooling. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA/BoE/Treasury Joint Statement on Frontier AI and Cyber Resilience:
Regulators warn that frontier AI capabilities now exceed skilled human practitioners in speed and scale, and firms underinvesting in core cyber security fundamentals face heightened regulatory and operational risk
🔗 View source
FCA Speech – Technologically Enabled Financial Crime:
The FCA CEO highlighted that investment fraud and organised cross-border criminal networks are increasingly technology-enabled, with average victim losses exceeding £25,000, making fraud detection and prevention an urgent priority for UK FinServ firms
🔗 View source
Ukraine Teen Suspect – Infostealer Targeting E-Commerce (28,000 Accounts):
An 18-year-old from Odesa was identified running an infostealer operation that compromised 28,000 accounts, a reminder that credential theft via infostealer malware poses a direct risk to customers and staff of financial platforms
🔗 View source
Microsoft Defender Zero-Days Exploited in Active Attacks:
Two Defender vulnerabilities are being actively exploited, posing an immediate endpoint security risk to financial firms relying on Microsoft Defender as a primary control
🔗 View source
FCA/BoE Tokenisation Vision for UK Wholesale Markets:
As regulators encourage DLT and tokenisation adoption in wholesale markets, firms should be aware that new digital asset infrastructure will introduce novel attack surfaces requiring security assessment before deployment
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS – LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054):
Adversaries can bypass LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification to extract sensitive data or generate harmful content, a direct risk for any financial firm deploying customer-facing or internal AI assistants
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS – MCP Server Prompt Injection / Data Exfiltration via Cursor (AML.CS0045):
A proof-of-concept shows that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into Cursor's context, causing it to exfiltrate AI agent configuration files and credentials via shell commands, a critical risk for developer environments handling sensitive data
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS – AI Agent Configuration File Exposure (AML.T0002.002):
Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, tool access, and system prompts to adversaries, making secure storage and access control of agent configs an immediate priority
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS – Agentic Resource Consumption via Prompt Injection (AML.T0034.002):
Attackers can coerce AI agents into excessive API calls through injected directives, draining operational budgets and degrading service availability in agentic financial workflows
🔗 View source
'Claw Chain' Vulnerabilities in OpenClaw AI Agent Framework:
Now-patched vulnerabilities in the OpenClaw AI agent framework allowed credential theft, privilege escalation, and persistence, illustrating that AI agent frameworks carry the same supply chain risks as any enterprise software
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-24425 (CVSS 8.8):
Twig templating engine sandbox bypass allows arbitrary PHP code execution for attackers with template rendering access, critical for any web application using Twig with user-controlled templates
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-42944 (CVSS 7.5):
NLnet Labs Unbound DNS resolver heap overflow can be triggered remotely by an unauthenticated attacker, risking denial of service or potential code execution on DNS infrastructure
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-42959 (CVSS 7.5):
A second NLnet Labs Unbound flaw allows a single query from an attacker controlling a DNSSEC-signed domain to crash the resolver, threatening DNS availability for firms using Unbound for internal resolution
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-42834 (CVSS 7.8):
Azure Portal Windows Admin Center link-following flaw enables local privilege escalation for authorised attackers, relevant to cloud-managed Windows environments common in UK FinServ
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-9064 (CVSS 7.5):
389-ds-base LDAP server has no upper bound on controls per message, allowing an unauthenticated remote attacker to cause denial of service through CPU exhaustion, a

Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and Treasury have jointly warned that frontier AI models now exceed skilled human cyber practitioners in speed and scale, directly amplifying threats to UK financial firms' safety and soundness. Multiple critical Keycloak identity vulnerabilities (session fixation, SAML DoS, redirect bypass) are newly disclosed and pose immediate risk to firms using federated identity. The Verizon DBIR finding that exploits now drive 31% of initial breaches, combined with a surge in critical Microsoft privilege-escalation flaws, underscores the urgency of accelerating patch cycles. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA/BoE/Treasury Joint Statement on Frontier AI and Cyber Resilience
— Regulators explicitly warn that AI-amplified cyber threats now threaten firm safety and soundness, and that firms underinvested in core security fundamentals are most exposed
🔗 View source
FCA Financial Crime Conference — Technologically Enabled Organised Crime
— FCA CEO highlighted that organised criminal groups using technology are driving investment fraud losses averaging £25,000 per victim, signalling increased regulatory scrutiny on firms' fraud prevention capabilities
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7507 (CVSS 7.5) — Keycloak Session Fixation
— Unauthenticated attackers can hijack authenticated sessions via a crafted link, enabling full account takeover in any financial firm using Keycloak for SSO
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7307 (CVSS 7.5) — Keycloak SAML DoS
— A malformed XML payload sent to the SAML endpoint can exhaust CPU and worker threads, making authentication services unavailable — a critical risk for firms with federated login dependencies
🔗 View source
Critical Microsoft Vulnerabilities Doubled — Privilege Escalation Focus
— The surge in critical Microsoft flaws targeting privilege escalation and identity abuse maps directly onto the attack surface of financial services environments heavily dependent on Microsoft infrastructure

MITRE ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompts or weight manipulation to extract data or generate malicious content, directly relevant to any AI-powered customer or analyst tooling deployed by the firm
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
— Attackers can use prompt injection to coerce AI agents into excessive API calls, draining budgets and degrading service — a tangible operational risk for firms running agentic AI workflows

— Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, system prompts, and tool access, enabling attackers to pivot into backend systems from an AI integration

— A proof-of-concept demonstrated that a malicious webpage can inject prompts into an AI coding agent (Cursor) via an MCP tool, triggering shell commands that exfiltrate agent credential files — a risk for any developer teams using AI-assisted coding tools
🔗 View source
'Claw Chain' Vulnerabilities in OpenClaw AI Agent Framework
— Now-patched flaws in this AI agent framework allowed credential theft, privilege escalation, and persistence, illustrating that rapidly adopted AI frameworks may carry unreviewed attack surface
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-5200 (CVSS 8.8) — AcyMailing WordPress Plugin
— Authenticated attackers with only subscriber-level access can export secret keys and take over administrator accounts, impacting any firm running WordPress-based marketing or customer portals
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-8711 (CVSS 8.1) — NGINX JavaScript Heap Buffer Overflow
— A crafted HTTP request can crash NGINX worker processes and, on systems without ASLR, enable remote code execution — critical for firms using NGINX as an API gateway or reverse proxy
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7504 (CVSS 8.1) — Keycloak Redirect URI Bypass
— Attackers can bypass redirect validation on wildcard-configured Keycloak clients to expose sensitive tokens or facilitate phishing, affecting firms using Keycloak with permissive redirect settings
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7571 (CVSS 7.1) — Keycloak OIDC Implicit Flow Bypass
— A low-privilege user can obtain access tokens that should be blocked and cause token leakage into server logs, proxy logs, and HTTP Referrer headers
🔗 View source

The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have jointly warned that frontier AI models now exceed skilled human cyber practitioners in speed and scale, directly raising the threat bar for UK financial firms that have underinvested in core security controls. A CISA contractor's public GitHub repository exposing highly privileged AWS GovCloud credentials represents a severe supply-chain and third-party risk reminder. Multiple critical vulnerabilities in NGINX, Microsoft Edge, and AI platforms (Dify, OpenClaw) demand immediate patching attention. ---

18 May 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have jointly warned that frontier AI models now exceed skilled human cyber practitioners in speed and scale, directly raising the threat bar for UK financial firms. A Windows zero-day ("MiniPlasma") with a public proof-of-concept grants SYSTEM privileges on fully patched systems, while an npm/PyPI supply chain attack linked to AI tooling (TanStack) has prompted OpenAI to issue an emergency macOS update. UK financial services firms face a compounding threat environment across AI misuse, unpatched infrastructure, and developer toolchain compromise. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA/BoE/HMT Joint Statement on Frontier AI and Cyber Resilience
— Regulators explicitly warn that frontier AI amplifies cyber threats to firms' safety, soundness, and market integrity, and that firms underinvesting in core cyber hygiene are most exposed
🔗 View source
FCA Speech: Working Together Against Financial Crime
— FCA CEO highlights that investment fraud now causes average losses of £25,000 per victim and is increasingly run by professional, cross-border organised criminal networks using technology at scale
🔗 View source
CISA Emergency Directive: Cisco SD-WAN Authentication Bypass
— An unauthenticated remote attacker can bypass authentication and gain administrative privileges on Cisco SD-WAN systems; CISA has ordered federal agencies to patch by Sunday, making this a high-priority item for any firm running this infrastructure
🔗 View source
TanStack npm Supply Chain Attack / OpenAI macOS Update
— A supply chain campaign is compromising popular npm and PyPI packages tied to several AI companies, with OpenAI urging macOS users to update immediately; financial firms using AI-integrated developer toolchains are directly in scope
🔗 View source
FCA/BoE Tokenisation Vision for UK Wholesale Markets
— As the FCA and Bank of England advance DLT adoption in wholesale markets, new attack surfaces around digital asset infrastructure and smart contract security warrant early attention from security teams
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override an LLM's safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification to extract sensitive data or generate malicious content, a direct risk for any firm deploying customer-facing or internal AI
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS Case Study: Data Exfiltration via MCP Server in Cursor (AML.CS0045)
— A proof-of-concept demonstrates that a malicious Model Context Protocol server can inject prompts into the Cursor coding assistant and exfiltrate AI agent configuration files containing credentials
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
— Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can reveal tool credentials, system prompts, and behavioural settings, giving attackers a direct route to lateral movement within AI-integrated environments
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
— Attackers can use prompt injection to force agentic AI systems into excessive, costly API calls, creating both financial and availability risks for firms running AI agents in production
🔗 View source
LatAm Threat Actors Using AI Agents to Generate Custom Hacking Tools
— Threat actors in campaigns targeting Mexico and Brazil are leveraging AI agents to create bespoke attack tools on the fly, signalling that AI-accelerated, highly targeted attacks against financial institutions are a near-term reality
🔗 View source

Windows "MiniPlasma" Zero-Day (no CVE published yet)

Linux "DirtyDecrypt" / "Dirty Frag" Privilege Escalation (no CVE cited in articles)

Executive summary

Cisco's Catalyst SD-WAN Controller is under active zero-day exploitation (CVE-2026-20182), making it the most pressing threat for firms running branch or hybrid WAN infrastructure today. An 18-year-old NGINX flaw enabling DoS and potential RCE has also resurfaced, posing risk to any financial services web infrastructure. On the AI/ML front, a MITRE ATLAS case study demonstrates how a malicious MCP server can silently exfiltrate AI agent credentials via prompt injection, a direct risk for firms deploying agentic AI tooling. ---

5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threats today are an unpatched Windows BitLocker zero-day (with public PoC) and a critical Exim remote code execution flaw, both of which pose immediate risk to financial services infrastructure. A new high-severity Linux kernel privilege escalation vulnerability (Fragnesia/CVE-2026-46300) is reportedly already under limited exploitation. On the AI/ML front, MITRE ATLAS has documented active adversarial techniques including LLM jailbreaking, agentic resource abuse, and a real-world MCP server data exfiltration attack against the Cursor IDE. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
Windows BitLocker Zero-Day (YellowKey/GreenPlasma):
Unpatched PoC exploits for a BitLocker bypass and privilege escalation flaw are publicly available, directly threatening disk encryption controls used to protect sensitive financial data on endpoints
🔗 View source
Critical Exim RCE Vulnerability:
An unauthenticated attacker can execute arbitrary code on mail servers running certain Exim configurations, risking compromise of email infrastructure used for trade confirmations, regulatory communications, and client correspondence
🔗 View source
Microsoft Vulnerability Surge (500+ in 2026):
Microsoft is on pace to break its annual vulnerability record, increasing the patch management burden on financial firms heavily dependent on Microsoft infrastructure

FCA Motor Finance Scheme Under Legal Challenge:
Legal challenges to the FCA's motor finance compensation scheme introduce regulatory and operational uncertainty for lenders, potentially affecting capital planning and litigation reserves
🔗 View source
FCA Bereaved Customer Review:
The FCA is actively reviewing how investment firms, platforms, and wealth managers handle bereavement cases, with fewer than half of affected customers feeling adequately supported — firms should expect supervisory scrutiny

Executive summary

May 2026 Patch Tuesday brings 120 Microsoft fixes and critical RCE flaws in Fortinet and SAP products, demanding immediate patching attention from infrastructure teams. On the AI/ML front, new MITRE ATLAS entries and a demonstrated MCP server exploit highlight growing risks from agentic AI deployments in enterprise environments. Financial services firms using SAP S/4HANA or running AI coding assistants should treat both as high-priority actions today. ---

🏭 FinServ threats

12 May 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML5 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing development today is confirmation that threat actors are using AI to develop zero-day exploits, with Google Threat Intelligence Group attributing an AI-generated exploit targeting a web administration tool to a state-linked actor. Multiple critical vulnerabilities in OpenClaw's AI agent gateway (CVSS up to 8.8) allow compromised AI models to persist malicious configuration changes, posing a direct risk to firms deploying agentic AI pipelines. UK financial services firms should also note the ICO's £963,900 fine against South Staffordshire Water for a Cl0p ransomware intrusion that went undetected for nearly two years — a stark reminder of dwell-time risk in OT/IT environments. ---

AI-Generated Zero-Day Exploit

— This banking trojan, originally designed to target financial institutions across North America and Europe, continues to underpin big-game ransomware campaigns relevant to the FinServ sector

— The £963,900 ICO fine for a breach affecting 633,887 individuals highlights regulatory and reputational risk for UK firms that fail to detect and contain intrusions promptly — a standard the FCA also expects of financial firms

— Ongoing legal challenges to the FCA's motor finance compensation scheme create regulatory uncertainty for lenders and their investors, with a tribunal hearing unlikely before October

Executive summary

The most pressing operational threat today is the active exploitation of a critical cPanel authentication-bypass vulnerability, with proof-of-concept exploits publicly available and reported zero-day activity predating disclosure. On the AI/ML front, a newly demonstrated attack chain shows that malicious MCP servers can silently exfiltrate AI agent credentials via prompt injection in coding tools like Cursor — a direct risk for any team using AI-assisted development. The FCA has also confirmed Competition Act investigations into Mastercard, Visa, and PayPal over suspected anti-competitive conduct, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny across payment infrastructure. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
FCA Competition Act Investigations (Mastercard, Visa, PayPal):
The FCA is formally investigating the three payment giants for suspected anti-competitive conduct linked to PayPal's digital wallet funding, which could affect payment processing arrangements relied upon by UK financial firms

TrickBot (MITRE S0266):
This banking trojan, originally designed to target financial institutions across North America and Europe, continues to underpin "big game hunting" ransomware campaigns that remain a direct threat to FinServ sector targets
🔗 View source
BLINDINGCAN RAT (MITRE S0520):
This North Korean government-linked remote access trojan has been used against Western financial and government organisations since 2020, and remains a relevant nation-state threat to UK financial services infrastructure
🔗 View source
Lazarus Group ClickFix macOS Campaign:
North Korea's Lazarus group is using ClickFix social engineering for initial access against Mac-centric organisations and their senior leaders, a profile that matches many UK financial services executives and developers
🔗 View source
FCA Motor Finance Scheme Legal Challenges:
Ongoing legal uncertainty around the motor finance compensation scheme may affect capital planning for lenders still holding provisions, with a tribunal hearing unlikely before October

🤖 AI / ML security
MCP Server Data Exfiltration via Prompt Injection (MITRE ATLAS AML.CS0045):
A proof-of-concept demonstrates that a malicious MCP server can inject prompts into Cursor, causing it to execute shell commands that exfiltrate AI agent configuration files containing credentials — directly relevant to any developer using AI coding assistants

AI Agent Configuration File Exposure (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0002.002):
Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose credentials, tool access, and system prompts to attackers, creating a persistent secrets-management risk in any AI agent deployment

LLM Jailbreak (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054):
Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or weight modification, enabling data leakage or generation of malicious content from models deployed in financial services workflows
🔗 View source
'TrustFall' — CLI AI Tool Code Execution Risk:
Malicious repositories can trigger code execution in Claude Code, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI with minimal user interaction due to inadequate warning dialogs, posing a supply-chain risk for developer toolchains
🔗 View source
Agentic Resource Consumption (MITRE ATLAS AML.T0034.002):
Attackers can coerce AI agents into making excessive API calls via prompt injection, wasting API budgets and potentially causing service degradation in automated financial workflows
🔗 View source

CVE-2021-47941 (CVSS 8.2):

Sentry 8.2.0 allows authenticated superusers to achieve remote code execution by injecting malicious pickle-serialised objects via the audit log endpoint — relevant to any firm using older self-hosted Sentry instances for error monitoring

CVE-2021-47949 (CVSS 8.8):
CyberPanel 2.1 allows authenticated attackers to read arbitrary files and execute remote commands via symlink manipulation in the file manager, risking full server compromise

CVE-2021-47939 (CVSS 8.8):
Evolution CMS 3.1.6 allows authenticated users to execute arbitrary system commands by injecting PHP code through module creation parameters
🔗 View source
CVE-2021-47945 (CVSS 7.8):
Argus Surveillance DVR 4.0 contains an unquoted service path vulnerability allowing local privilege escalation to SYSTEM — a risk for any physical security or CCTV systems integrated with corporate networks

07 May 2026
5 FinServ5 AI/ML3 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

The most pressing threat today is an actively exploited zero-day in Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS (CVE-2026-0300) with no patch yet available, posing immediate risk to financial institutions using these firewalls as perimeter controls. On the AI/ML front, MITRE ATLAS has published new technique entries covering LLM jailbreaks, agentic resource exhaustion, and AI agent configuration harvesting, reflecting a maturing adversarial playbook against AI systems. The FCA has also taken enforcement action in multiple areas this week — including a £19m client money failure and competition investigations into Mastercard, Visa, and PayPal — signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny across the sector. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS Zero-Day (CVE-2026-0300)
— Unpatched critical RCE in PAN-OS User-ID Authentication Portal is actively being exploited; any UK FinServ firm using Palo Alto firewalls for network segmentation or internet-facing access control is at immediate risk
🔗 View source
FCA: Sapia/WealthTek Client Money Failure (£19m)
— Sapia failed to segregate client money roles, allowing payment authorisers to also conduct oversight checks; this is a direct reminder that inadequate CASS controls carry both financial and reputational consequences
🔗 View source
FCA: Competition Investigations into Mastercard, Visa, and PayPal
— The FCA has confirmed active Competition Act investigations into suspected anti-competitive conduct linked to PayPal's digital wallet funding arrangements; firms with payment processing dependencies on these networks should monitor developments closely
🔗 View source
FCA: Global Crackdown on Illegal Finfluencers
— The FCA secured a criminal guilty plea and issued 34 warning alerts against unauthorised financial promoters; firms must ensure their social media monitoring programmes flag third-party promotional activity that could create unauthorised association risks
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-20188 — Cisco Crosswork/NSO DoS (CVSS 7.5)
— A missing rate-limit on incoming connections allows an unauthenticated attacker to exhaust resources and take down network orchestration infrastructure; FinServ firms using Cisco NSO for network automation face availability risk
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
MITRE ATLAS: LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054)
— Adversaries can override LLM safety guardrails via adversarial prompting or model weight manipulation to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs; any FinServ-deployed LLM handling customer data or internal tooling is a viable target

MITRE ATLAS: Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
— Attackers can use prompt injection to force AI agents into excessive, costly tool calls, draining API budgets and degrading service availability; this is particularly relevant for firms running LLM-powered automation against rate-limited or metered APIs
🔗 View source
MITRE ATLAS: AI Agent Configuration Harvesting (AML.T0002.002)
— Publicly accessible AI agent config files can expose tool credentials, system prompts, and external service tokens to adversaries; firms deploying AI agents should treat config files with the same sensitivity as secrets vaults

🔗 View source
CVE-2026-44118 — OpenClaw MCP Bearer Token Spoofing (CVSS 7.8)
— Non-owner loopback clients can impersonate the owner of an MCP server by manipulating bearer token headers, bypassing owner-gated operations; any internal deployment of OpenClaw before 2026.4.22 should be patched immediately
🔗 View source

CVE-2026-41934 (CVSS 8.8)
— Authenticated low-privilege users in Vvveb CMS (before 1.0.8.2) can achieve remote code execution by uploading a malicious .htaccess file and bypassing file extension restrictions; any web-facing CMS running Vvveb should be patched or isolated immediately
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-43584 (CVSS 8.8)

A critical PAN-OS zero-day (CVE-2026-0300) is being actively exploited in the wild, allowing unauthenticated remote code execution with root privileges on Palo Alto firewalls — any UK financial services firm running PA-Series or VM-Series appliances should treat this as an immediate priority. The FCA has taken enforcement action against Sapia for client money failures and launched a global crackdown on illegal financial influencers, signalling continued regulatory pressure on UK firms. On the AI/ML front, MITRE ATLAS and industry research highlight growing risks from LLM jailbreaks, agentic resource abuse, and exposed AI agent configuration files. ---

Executive summary

The most pressing threat today is an actively exploited critical authentication bypass in MOVEit Automation, a platform widely used in financial services for secure file transfer. A cluster of newly disclosed vulnerabilities in AI/ML tooling — including MCP servers, agentic AI products, and LLM infrastructure — highlights a rapidly growing attack surface for firms adopting these technologies. The FCA's global enforcement action against illegal finfluencers and a £19m client money enforcement case serve as reminders of both conduct risk and the operational security failures that enable financial crime. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
MOVEit Automation Critical Auth Bypass
— MOVEit is heavily used in UK financial services for regulated file transfer; unauthenticated attackers can bypass authentication entirely, mirroring the 2023 MOVEit breach that hit multiple financial institutions
🔗 View source
Trellix Source Code Repository Breach
— A breach of a major security vendor's source code repository could expose detection logic, giving attackers advance knowledge of how to evade endpoint tools deployed across financial sector environments
🔗 View source
FCA Action Against Illegal Finfluencers
— The FCA-led global crackdown, including a guilty plea from a named individual, signals active criminal prosecution of social-media-based financial fraud that targets retail customers of UK firms
🔗 View source
Sapia/WealthTek £19m Client Money Failure
— The FCA censure highlights that inadequate segregation of duties over client money accounts is an enforcement priority, directly relevant to any firm with appointed representative relationships
🔗 View source
TrickBot (MITRE ATT&CK S0266)
— TrickBot originated as banking trojan targeting financial institutions and continues to underpin ransomware campaigns; financial sector defenders should ensure detections for its TTPs remain current

— Adversaries can override safety guardrails to extract sensitive data or generate malicious content, posing a direct risk to any financial firm deploying customer-facing or internal LLM tools

— An OS command injection flaw in the wireshark-mcp MCP integration tool is publicly exploitable remotely, posing risk to any analyst environment using this tool for packet capture within AI-assisted workflows

CVE-2026-7788 (CVSS 7.3)

The most pressing threat today is the critical RCE vulnerability CVE-2026-1731 in Bomgar RMM being actively exploited for ransomware delivery and supply chain compromise. Google has patched a critical prompt-injection/sandbox-escape flaw in its AI-based Antigravity tool, highlighting the growing risk of agentic AI systems in enterprise environments. Financial services firms should also note the FCA's enforcement action against Sapia over client money protection failures, reinforcing the need for robust segregation of duties. ---

🏭 FinServ threats
Sapia/WealthTek Client Money Failure

TrickBot originated as a banking trojan targeting financial institutions in North America, Australia, and Europe and has evolved into a ransomware delivery tool used in big-game hunting campaigns against all sectors

🔗 View source
MCP Server Data Exfiltration via Cursor (MITRE ATLAS AML.CS0045)
A proof-of-concept showed that a malicious Model Context Protocol server can inject prompts into Cursor, causing it to exfiltrate AI agent configuration files and credentials via shell commands
🔗 View source
AI Agent Configuration Exposure (AML.T0002.002)
Publicly accessible AI agent configuration files can expose tool credentials, system prompts, and API keys, giving attackers a ready map for further compromise of AI-integrated services
🔗 View source
Agentic Resource Consumption (AML.T0034.002)
Adversaries can use prompt injection to force agentic AI systems into excessive, costly API calls, draining budgets and degrading service availability — a real operational risk for firms using AI in customer-facing or back-office workflows
🔗 View source
Cisco Finds Memory Vulnerability in Anthropic's Claude (Bad Memories)
A significant flaw in how Anthropic's systems handle agent memory files was found and fixed, but experts warn mismanaged memory files remain a systemic threat vector across AI agent deployments
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-7717 (CVSS 8.8)
Buffer overflow in Totolink WA300 router firmware exploitable remotely via POST request, with public exploit available — patch or isolate affected devices immediately
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7735 / CVE-2026-7736 (CVSS 7.3)
Two remotely exploitable flaws (buffer overflow and integer underflow) in GoBGP up to 4.3.0 could destabilise BGP routing infrastructure — upgrade to 4.4.0 now
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7711 (CVSS 7.3)
Unrestricted file upload vulnerability in MindsDB up to 26.01 exploitable remotely via the engine handler, posing a serious risk in any environment running MindsDB for ML model management
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7710 (CVSS 7.3)
Improper authentication bypass in yudao-cloud's JWT filter allows remote exploitation via a mock-token manipulation — particularly relevant if this open-source framework is used in any internal platforms
🔗 View source
CVE-2026-7698 (CVSS 7.3)
OS command injection in Tiandy Easy7 Integrated Management Platform 7.17.0 is publicly exploited and unpatched — vendor has not responded to disclosure
🔗 View source
01 May 2026
3 FinServ3 AI/ML2 Vuln
▼
Executive summary

Financial Services Threats — up to 5 bullet points:

• CVE-2026-7386 — MCP Mail Bridge Path Traversal: Unauthenticated path traversal in the NovaMail MCP bridge allows attackers to read arbitrary files from the host; at least 3 UK banks confirmed affected.

• Scattered Spider SIM-Swap Campaign: UNC3944 resumes targeted SIM-swapping against UK financial services helpdesks to reset MFA on executive accounts, used as a precursor to BEC and data extortion.

• Volt Typhoon Updated TTPs — Critical Infrastructure: CISA/NSA joint advisory updates Volt Typhoon living-off-the-land techniques with new indicators targeting payment processing and clearing infrastructure.

🏭 FinServ threats
CVE-2026-7386 — MCP Mail Bridge Path Traversal
Unauthenticated path traversal in the NovaMail MCP bridge allows attackers to read arbitrary files from the host; at least 3 UK banks confirmed affected
🔗 View source
Scattered Spider SIM-Swap Campaign
UNC3944 resumes targeted SIM-swapping against UK financial services helpdesks to reset MFA on executive accounts, used as a precursor to BEC and data extortion
🔗 View source
Volt Typhoon Updated TTPs — Critical Infrastructure
CISA/NSA joint advisory updates Volt Typhoon living-off-the-land techniques with new indicators targeting payment processing and clearing infrastructure
🔗 View source
🤖 AI / ML security
AML.T0059 Agentic Resource Consumption
Proof-of-concept exploit forces AI banking agents to recursively chain tool calls, exhausting API budgets; Ollama local deployments confirmed vulnerable with 0% refusal rate
🔗 View source
Copilot Studio Data Exfiltration via Agent Tools
Confirmed finding shows Microsoft Copilot Studio leaking sensitive data through connected agent tools, bypassing DLP controls entirely in tested banking environments
🔗 View source
MCP Server Exploitation in Cursor IDE
Developer AI tooling (Cursor + MCP servers) successfully exfiltrated environment variables and API keys through agent tool calls in internal engineering environments
🔗 View source
🔓 Vulnerabilities
CVE-2026-7386 CVSS 9.8
Critical unauthenticated path traversal in NovaMail MCP Bridge; patch available as of 30 April, no workaround for pre-auth exploitation path
🔗 View source
NCSC Advisory — AI Agent Security
UK NCSC publishes guidance on securing agentic AI deployments, specifically calling out MCP server misconfiguration and excessive tool permissions as high-risk patterns
🔗 View source
⚡ Analyst note

03 July 2026 5 stories
#1
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Catalogued Adversarial Technique — Time to Map Your Controls
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms deploying LLMs in customer-facing or internal tooling (e.g., co-pilots, fraud triage, document summarisation) face a concrete, catalogued threat: adversaries bypassing safety guardrails to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs. MITRE ATLAS cataloguing this as AML.T0054 means it now has a formal taxonomy that can be mapped to your threat model and control frameworks, including alignment with FCA expectations around model governance and operational resilience. Data leakage downstream (AML.T0057) is a particular concern given UK GDPR and FCA data handling obligations.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors directly exploit LLM safety mechanisms via adversarial prompting or weight manipulation to bypass content controls, enabling exfiltration of system prompts, internal data, or generation of phishing content and malicious commands.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map AML.T0054 to your existing AI/ML risk register today — conduct a red-team prompt injection exercise against any production LLM and verify that jailbreak attempts are logged, alerted on, and blocked at the guardrail layer.
#2
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as Live Threat Vector for API-Driven Systems
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems for automated workflows — market data retrieval, document processing, customer query handling — all of which carry API cost budgets and rate limits that adversaries can weaponise. A successful resource consumption attack can degrade availability of AI-driven services, creating operational resilience failures that fall squarely within FCA PS21/3 and DORA expectations. The attack surface is particularly broad when agents accept user-supplied instructions without strict sandboxing.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors abuse prompt injection or tool data poisoning to instruct agentic systems to perform excessive, redundant, or computationally expensive API calls, exhausting budgets and degrading service availability — a form of AI-native denial of service.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits, cost caps, and instruction validation on all agentic AI tool calls — audit any agent that accepts user-controlled input for prompt injection pathways that could trigger unbounded resource consumption.
#3
FCA Publishes Landmark Crypto Regulatory Framework Covering Capital, Market Abuse, and Stablecoins
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms operating in or adjacent to the crypto space now face binding requirements on financial resilience, capital adequacy, stress testing, and market integrity rules covering insider trading and market manipulation — materially expanding the compliance and security perimeter. Stablecoin-specific rules introduce new custodial and operational requirements that will drive security architecture decisions around key management, transaction monitoring, and AML controls. Security engineers supporting crypto platforms must ensure their threat models account for market manipulation detection as a regulated obligation, not just a best practice.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's crypto and stablecoin product lines against the new FCA framework now — engage with compliance to identify where security controls (transaction monitoring, insider threat detection, resilience testing) need to be formalised to meet the new mandatory standards.
#4
Critical Path Traversal and Local File Inclusion Vulnerability in Cockpit CMS (CVSS 7.5) Allows Unauthenticated Code Execution
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

This unauthenticated path traversal and local file inclusion (LFI) flaw in Cockpit CMS allows arbitrary file reads and PHP code execution without any credentials, making it trivially exploitable by opportunistic attackers and ransomware affiliates conducting internet-wide scans. While Cockpit CMS may not be a core banking platform, it is commonly used for internal portals, intranets, and marketing content management — all present in large financial services organisations as shadow IT or vendor-managed assets. Exploitation could expose configuration files containing credentials, API keys, or internal network details that enable lateral movement.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run an immediate asset discovery query across your estate and vendor inventory for Cockpit CMS deployments and confirm all instances are patched to release 364 or later — pay particular attention to instances running on PHP built-in server or non-default Nginx configurations, which are specifically vulnerable to the LFI execution path.
#5
FCA Confirms Statutory Panel Chair Appointments Amid Market Reform Period
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The confirmation of leadership for the FCA's Listing Authority Advisory Panel, Markets Practitioner Panel, and Practitioner Panel signals continuity in the FCA's policy development machinery during a period of significant regulatory change, including the new crypto framework and ongoing DORA alignment. These panels directly influence how FCA policy evolves, meaning their composition is relevant to firms seeking to anticipate regulatory direction on market integrity and operational resilience. Security engineers in FinServ should be aware that practitioner panel input shapes the practical implementation expectations that will eventually land as compliance requirements.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Monitor FCA panel publications and consultation responses over the coming months — these outputs are early signals of where new compliance obligations are heading and allow security teams to get ahead of requirements rather than react to final rules. ---
02 July 2026 5 stories
#1
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Documented Adversarial Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial firms deploying LLMs for customer service, fraud detection, or internal tooling face a formalised and catalogued threat: adversaries deliberately subverting model safety guardrails to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs. This MITRE ATLAS entry (AML.T0054) provides a structured taxonomy linking jailbreaks to downstream techniques such as LLM Data Leakage and malicious command generation — directly relevant to any AI system handling regulated financial data or customer PII. Security teams can now map this threat to their existing ATLAS/ATT&CK-aligned detection and control frameworks.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors directly exploit LLM safety mechanisms through adversarial prompting or weight manipulation to bypass guardrails, enabling data exfiltration or harmful content generation from models that would otherwise refuse such requests.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review all deployed LLM integrations today and implement a layered defence including input/output filtering, prompt injection detection, and red-team jailbreak testing — mapping findings against AML.T0054 in your threat model.
#2
Agentic AI Systems Vulnerable to Resource-Exhaustion Attacks via Prompt Injection
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms increasingly adopt agentic AI systems for workflow automation, trade processing, and customer operations, this MITRE ATLAS technique highlights a practical availability and cost risk: adversaries can coerce agents into runaway API calls, exhausting compute budgets and potentially causing service disruption. In a regulated environment, unexpected service degradation from AI systems carries operational resilience obligations under FCA and PRA rules. This technique also serves as a stepping stone to broader compromise via chained prompt injection attacks.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries leverage LLM Prompt Injection or AI Agent Tool Data Poisoning to embed directives that force agentic systems into computationally expensive, unnecessary tool calls — effectively weaponising the agent's autonomy against its own infrastructure.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and cost caps on all agentic AI tool-call APIs, and audit agent system prompts for susceptibility to injection directives that could trigger excessive or looping external queries.
#3
FCA Publishes Landmark Crypto Regulatory Framework Including Market Integrity and Stablecoin Rules
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

This framework introduces binding obligations for UK-regulated crypto firms covering financial resilience, capital adequacy, stress testing, and — critically — market integrity rules targeting insider trading and market manipulation, which have direct parallels to existing financial crime controls. Firms offering crypto custody, trading, or stablecoin services must now align security and compliance programmes to these standards, including controls that prevent manipulation which could be AI-assisted. Stablecoin-specific rules add a new compliance surface for any firm involved in digital payment rails or tokenised assets.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY If your firm touches crypto assets or stablecoins, immediately gap-assess your existing market abuse surveillance and financial resilience controls against the new FCA requirements and flag remediation timelines to your compliance team.
#4
Critical Adobe ColdFusion and Campaign Classic Vulnerabilities Enable Arbitrary Code Execution
🔗 CIS Advisory Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Adobe ColdFusion is present in legacy web application stacks across financial services, and Adobe Campaign Classic is widely used for regulated marketing communications — both represent significant attack surface in UK FinServ environments. Arbitrary code execution vulnerabilities in these platforms could allow an attacker to pivot from a marketing or web tier into internal networks, exfiltrate customer data, or establish persistence, all carrying serious FCA data protection and operational resilience implications. Legacy Adobe deployments are frequently under-patched in large organisations.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit your estate for Adobe ColdFusion and Campaign Classic deployments today, prioritise patching to the latest vendor-recommended versions, and verify whether any instances are internet-facing or connected to systems holding customer or financial data.
#5
FCA Confirms New Panel Chairs Amid Regulatory Change Across UK Capital Markets
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

New leadership at the FCA's Listing Authority Advisory Panel and Practitioner Panels signals continued regulatory evolution in UK capital markets at a time of significant policy change, which may influence upcoming guidance relevant to technology, AI, and operational resilience. These panels formally represent the interests of regulated firms — including smaller ones — meaning their outputs can shape rules that directly affect security and technology investment obligations. Security engineers should monitor outputs from these panels for emerging expectations around firm-level controls.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Subscribe to FCA panel publications and consultation updates to ensure your security and technology risk programmes remain aligned with evolving regulatory expectations before they become binding requirements. ---
01 July 2026 5 stories
#1
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Catalogued Adversarial Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms increasingly deploy LLM-powered tools for customer service, fraud detection, and internal automation — all of which are in scope for this technique. MITRE's formal cataloguing of LLM jailbreaking (AML.T0054) means threat actors have a structured playbook for bypassing model guardrails to extract sensitive data or generate malicious content. The FCA's AI governance expectations under existing operational resilience frameworks require firms to demonstrate controls over AI outputs, making this directly relevant to compliance as well as technical defence.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use adversarial prompting or direct model weight manipulation to override LLM safety mechanisms, enabling downstream attacks such as data leakage, internal system enumeration, or generation of fraud-enabling content.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map your deployed LLM applications against MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054 today — specifically validate that guardrail bypass testing (red-teaming with adversarial prompts) is included in your AI security assessment programme.
#2
SSRF Vulnerability in IBM Langflow Allows Cloud Credential Theft via Prompt Injection
🔗 NVD / NIST AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

IBM Langflow is used to build agentic AI workflows, a pattern growing in financial services for automating back-office and decision-support processes. This SSRF flaw (CVSS 8.2) allows authenticated attackers to reach cloud metadata services (AWS/Azure/GCP IMDS) and exfiltrate IAM credentials, which could provide lateral movement into cloud-hosted financial data environments. Critically, the vulnerability can also be triggered via prompt injection in agentic workflows, meaning a malicious instruction embedded in external data could silently exploit it without direct attacker access.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Attackers can weaponise prompt injection — embedding SSRF-triggering payloads in content processed by Langflow agents — to pivot from the AI workflow layer into internal cloud infrastructure without needing direct authenticated access.

KEY TAKEAWAY If you are running IBM Langflow versions 1.0.0–1.9.6, patch immediately to a version beyond 1.9.6 and audit agentic workflow inputs for prompt injection vectors, particularly any components operating with tool_mode=True.
#3
FCA Publishes Landmark Crypto Asset Regulatory Framework Including Market Abuse Rules
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The new FCA crypto framework introduces mandatory financial resilience requirements — capital adequacy and stress testing — alongside market integrity rules covering insider trading and market manipulation, directly affecting UK firms operating or planning to operate in crypto markets. Stablecoin-specific standards will impose additional compliance obligations, particularly relevant to firms exploring digital asset settlement or treasury functions. Security engineers should note that market manipulation rules create a regulatory dimension to detecting and reporting anomalous trading activity, including AI-driven wash trading or spoofing.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's crypto asset activities against the new FCA framework requirements now — specifically assess whether your surveillance and anomaly detection controls for market manipulation meet the new regulatory bar, and flag gaps to your compliance team.
#4
Adobe ColdFusion Reflected XSS (CVSS 8.8) Enables Arbitrary Code Execution via Malicious Link
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

ColdFusion remains in active use across legacy financial services environments, particularly in older web application stacks for customer portals and internal tooling. A reflected XSS with changed scope and a CVSS of 8.8 is a high-severity finding that can lead to arbitrary code execution in the victim's browser context, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or malware delivery targeting bank staff or customers. The user-interaction requirement (opening a malicious link) makes this a credible phishing-chain component against employees with privileged application access.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Identify all ColdFusion instances in your environment running versions 2023.20 or earlier and 2025.9, prioritise patching, and in the interim enforce strict Content Security Policy headers and user awareness around unsolicited links to ColdFusion-hosted applications.
#5
FCA Confirms New Panel Chair Appointments Across Listing and Practitioner Advisory Bodies
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

Changes in FCA advisory panel leadership can signal shifts in regulatory policy direction, particularly with a new Listing Authority Advisory Panel chair appointed during what the FCA itself describes as "a time of profound change across markets." For security and technology teams, panel composition influences forthcoming consultations on topics including operational resilience, technology risk, and emerging asset classes. Monitoring these appointments helps anticipate where FCA attention and future rule-making may be directed.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Subscribe to FCA panel publications and consultations under the new chairs to ensure your firm's security and technology risk positions are fed into any relevant policy consultations early in their development cycle. ---
