#1
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Catalogued Adversarial Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms increasingly deploying LLM-based tools (customer service, fraud detection, document analysis) face a formally catalogued threat where adversaries bypass safety guardrails to extract sensitive data or generate malicious outputs. MITRE's classification under Defence Evasion (AML.TA0007) signals this is now a recognised, structured attack vector requiring the same programmatic controls as traditional TTPs. Regulators such as the FCA and PRA are beginning to scrutinise AI governance, meaning an unmitigated jailbreak leading to data leakage could carry both operational and compliance consequences.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use adversarial prompting techniques — crafted inputs designed to confuse or override alignment training — to coerce LLMs into revealing system prompts, customer data, or generating content that bypasses compliance controls, directly exploiting the model's own reasoning mechanisms.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map all internal LLM deployments against AML.T0054 today: implement input/output guardrail logging, red-team each model with prompt injection scenarios, and ensure jailbreak attempts are treated as security events feeding into your SIEM.
#2
FCA Publishes Landmark Crypto Asset Regulatory Framework Including Insider Trading and Stablecoin Rules
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

Any UK financial services firm touching crypto — custody, trading, or stablecoin issuance — now faces binding capital adequacy, stress testing, and market integrity obligations, including explicit rules on insider trading and market manipulation. Failure to comply carries direct regulatory risk, and the stablecoin provisions in particular will affect firms exploring blockchain-based payment rails or tokenised assets. Security engineers must understand that these rules create new data classification and audit trail requirements that directly intersect with your firm's security architecture.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's crypto-adjacent services against the new FCA framework immediately and engage your compliance team to assess whether existing security controls (audit logging, access controls on trading systems) satisfy the new market integrity and capital reporting obligations.
#3
MITRE ATLAS Catalogues Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as Distinct Denial-of-Service Attack Vector
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Agentic AI systems — increasingly used in financial services for automated research, workflow orchestration, and customer interactions — can be weaponised against their own API budgets and compute resources through carefully crafted prompt injections, creating both availability risk and unexpected financial cost. For UK FinServ firms operating under operational resilience requirements (SS1/21), an adversarially induced resource exhaustion event affecting an AI agent could constitute a material operational disruption. The technique compounds with prompt injection (AML.T0051), meaning existing LLM deployments are already potentially exposed.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit prompt injection or tool data poisoning to direct agentic AI systems into recursive, expensive, or fan-out API calls — effectively turning the AI agent's autonomous tool-use capability into an internally-sourced denial-of-service mechanism against the firm's own infrastructure and cloud budget.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and cost-cap circuit breakers on all agentic AI tool calls today, and add anomalous API call volume to your monitoring playbooks as an indicator of prompt injection-driven resource abuse.
#4
FCA Refines Consumer Duty Scope — Clarifies Application Across Wholesale Firm Value Chains
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's clarification of Consumer Duty obligations for wholesale firms confirms that the Duty's requirements propagate through service and supply chains, meaning wholesale firms providing data, platforms, or technology to retail-facing entities remain in scope. For security engineers, this reinforces that data handling practices, system availability, and the integrity of outputs (including AI-generated outputs) that ultimately affect retail consumers carry regulatory weight. Any AI or automated decision-making tool in the value chain that influences consumer outcomes should be reviewed for Consumer Duty alignment.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit whether any AI/ML systems your firm operates or supplies to downstream retail firms affect consumer outcomes, and document the governance controls in place — this will be a likely FCA supervisory focus area as the Duty matures.
#5
Publicly Exploitable SQL Injection (CVSS 7.3) Identified in SourceCodester Timetabling System
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

This remotely exploitable SQL injection in a SourceCodester application carries a CVSS score of 7.3 with a public exploit already available, meaning opportunistic attackers can weaponise it with minimal effort. While this specific product is unlikely to be deployed in a major UK financial services environment, it serves as a reminder to audit any open-source or low-cost scheduling/timetabling tools that may have been adopted by operations or HR teams outside of formal IT governance processes. Shadow IT adoption of tools like this represents a realistic attack surface in large firms.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run an asset discovery sweep today to identify any SourceCodester or similar open-source web applications running within your environment — particularly those onboarded outside formal procurement — and validate they are not internet-exposed. ---
29 June 2026 5 stories
#1
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Catalogued Adversarial Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms increasingly deploy LLMs for customer-facing services, fraud detection, and internal tooling — all of which are potential jailbreak targets. MITRE's formal cataloguing of this technique (AML.T0054) signals it is mature enough to appear in structured threat models, meaning regulators and auditors will increasingly expect controls against it. A successful jailbreak could lead to data leakage of sensitive customer or transaction data, or generation of content that breaches FCA conduct rules.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use adversarial prompting techniques — crafted inputs designed to override model safety guardrails — to extract restricted information or generate harmful outputs such as fraudulent scripts or social engineering content. Model weight modification is also cited as a vector, relevant where firms use fine-tuned or self-hosted models.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map your deployed LLMs against MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054 today — document what guardrails exist, test them with a structured red-team prompt injection exercise, and ensure any LLM output is validated by a downstream content filter before reaching end users or automated workflows.
#2
Agentic AI Systems at Risk of Resource Exhaustion via Prompt Injection Abuse
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms piloting agentic AI — such as automated research assistants, trade support agents, or regulatory reporting bots — face a real risk of adversarially induced API budget exhaustion and service disruption. In a FinServ context, runaway API costs or degraded agent availability could constitute an operational resilience incident, which the FCA and PRA increasingly scrutinise under DORA-aligned expectations. Poisoned tool directives could also be used to exfiltrate data disguised as legitimate, high-volume API queries.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit prompt injection or tool data poisoning to embed resource-exhausting directives into agentic pipelines, causing excessive external API calls or compute consumption — either as a denial-of-service mechanism or to mask malicious lateral activity within noisy query volumes.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and cost caps on all agentic AI tool calls, and add anomaly detection for unusual query fan-out patterns — review your current AI agent architecture today to confirm no external-facing input can reach tool-calling logic without sanitisation.
#3
FCA Censures CACEIS UK with £31.7m Penalty for Ignoring Financial Crime Red Flags
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

This enforcement action demonstrates the FCA's continued willingness to hold custodians and sub-custodians directly accountable for third-party financial crime risks, even when the primary fraud was committed by another firm. For UK FinServ security and compliance teams, it is a direct signal that failure to act on observable warning signs — such as authorisation status checks on the Financial Services Register — is treated as a control failure, not merely an oversight. The £31.7m figure, combined with prior actions against Sapia Partners and Barclays, shows the FCA is pursuing the full custodian chain.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your third-party and sub-custodian due diligence processes today — specifically confirm that automated or periodic checks against the FCA Financial Services Register are in place and that results trigger a documented escalation workflow when anomalies are found.
#4
UK Bond Market Consolidated Tape Goes Live — New Real-Time Data Surface Requires Security Attention
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The launch of ETS Connect UK's bond consolidated tape creates a new centralised, real-time data feed that firms will integrate into trading and risk systems — expanding the attack surface for data integrity and supply chain risks. Firms consuming this feed should treat it as a third-party data dependency requiring integrity validation, particularly given that manipulated market data could influence algorithmic trading or automated risk models. The rapid shift from under 5% to over 75% real-time reporting also means data volumes and dependencies on this single source are now significant.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Before integrating the new bond consolidated tape feed into any automated trading, risk, or AI/ML pricing model, conduct a supply chain risk assessment of ETS Connect UK as a critical third-party data provider and implement data integrity checks on ingested feed values.
#5
Publicly Exploitable SQL Injection Vulnerability Disclosed in Timetabling System (CVE-2026-13487)
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

While the affected product — SourceCodester Class and Exam Timetabling System 1.0 — is unlikely to be deployed in core FinServ infrastructure, the public availability of a working exploit for this SQL injection flaw (CVSS 7.3) is a reminder that unmanaged or shadow IT applications within a firm's estate can carry serious risk. UK financial firms with broad internal application portfolios, including HR, training, or scheduling tools, should verify this software is not present in their environment. Publicly available exploits dramatically reduce attacker effort and time-to-exploitation.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run a search across your software asset inventory and any shadow IT discovery tooling today to confirm SourceCodester Class and Exam Timetabling System 1.0 is not present in your environment — and use this as a prompt to audit any unmanaged web applications running on internal or DMZ infrastructure. ---

#1

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms deploying LLMs in customer-facing or internal tooling (fraud detection, document summarisation, chatbots) face a formally catalogued threat where adversaries bypass safety controls to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs. The MITRE ATLAS classification (AML.T0054) means this is now a trackable, mappable technique that should appear in your AI threat models and risk registers. FCA expectations around model governance and operational resilience make it critical to demonstrate you have assessed and mitigated this risk explicitly.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors directly exploit the AI/ML system itself — using adversarial prompting or weight manipulation to override alignment guardrails, enabling data leakage or generation of malicious content without needing to compromise underlying infrastructure.

KEY TAKEAWAY Add AML.T0054 to your internal AI risk register today and test your deployed LLMs against a structured jailbreak evaluation framework (e.g., red-teaming with ATLAS-aligned scenarios) before your next model release or audit cycle.
#2
Agentic AI Systems Vulnerable to Resource Exhaustion via Prompt Injection — A New DoS Vector for AI-Powered Finance Tools
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems for tasks like automated research, trade data retrieval, and client onboarding — all of which involve tool calls and external API access. Adversaries can exploit prompt injection to coerce these agents into runaway API consumption, draining operational budgets and potentially causing service degradation that triggers FCA operational resilience obligations. This is a low-sophistication, high-impact attack requiring no infrastructure compromise.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors leverage prompt injection (AML.T0051) or tool data poisoning to embed directives inside agent inputs, manipulating autonomous AI systems into performing thousands of unnecessary tool calls — turning the AI's own agentic capabilities against the organisation.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and budget caps on all agentic AI tool-call interfaces immediately, and introduce input validation that flags or blocks anomalous directive patterns (e.g., repetition instructions or excessive fan-out requests) before they reach the agent runtime.
#3
FCA Censures CACEIS UK and Secures £31.7m for WealthTek Clients Over Financial Crime Control Failures
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

This enforcement action demonstrates the FCA's willingness to hold custodian and sub-custodian firms accountable for acting on red flags visible in publicly available sources — in this case, the Financial Services Register. For UK financial services security and compliance teams, it reinforces that failure to operationalise available intelligence (including regulatory data) into controls constitutes a sanctionable oversight failure. The £31.7m voluntary payment signals significant reputational and financial consequences even where no formal fine is levied.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your third-party and sub-custodian due diligence processes today to confirm that automated or regular checks against the FCA Financial Services Register and other authorisation databases are built into your onboarding and ongoing monitoring controls — document evidence of these checks for audit purposes.
#4
Vim's Bundled Zip Plugin Allows Arbitrary Command Execution via Crafted Archive Entry Names — Patch to 9.2.0678 Now
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Vim is widely used by developers and security engineers across financial services environments, including in Linux-based build pipelines, code review workflows, and analyst workstations. A crafted zip archive entry name can trigger arbitrary PowerShell command execution with the privileges of the running user — a realistic attack vector via malicious file delivery to technical staff. CVSS 6.5 may understate real-world exploitability given how commonly engineers open archives in Vim.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Patch Vim to version 9.2.0678 or later across all developer and analyst endpoints today, and check your software asset inventory for any CI/CD pipeline tooling or container images that bundle an affected Vim version (9.1.1784–9.2.0677).
#5
UK Bond Market Gets Real-Time Consolidated Tape — New Data Surface Raises Market Integrity and Data Security Considerations
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The launch of ETS Connect UK's bond consolidated tape centralises real-time bond trade data that was previously fragmented, increasing market transparency but also creating a single high-value data aggregation point that warrants security scrutiny. For UK FinServ security teams, any firm consuming or contributing to this feed should assess the integrity and availability controls around the data pipeline. Manipulation or disruption of a consolidated tape could have systemic market implications, making it a potential target.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY If your firm connects to or ingests the ETS Connect UK consolidated tape, request the operator's security assurance documentation and validate that your own ingestion pipeline includes integrity checks (e.g., cryptographic validation of feed data) to detect potential tampering or spoofing of reported trade prices. ---
25 June 2026 5 stories
#1
Flowise Auth Bypass Exposes OAuth Secrets in Cleartext — Patch to 3.1.0 Now
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Flowise is a widely deployed low-code LLM orchestration platform used to build AI workflows, including in financial services automation contexts. This unauthenticated endpoint leaks OAuth client secrets for Google, Microsoft/Azure, GitHub, and Auth0 — identity providers commonly integrated into enterprise FinServ environments. An attacker harvesting these credentials could pivot into cloud infrastructure, data stores, or downstream AI pipelines with legitimate-looking OAuth tokens, bypassing standard controls.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors targeting AI/ML infrastructure can exploit this pre-auth flaw to steal credentials that grant access to the LLM agent's tool integrations, enabling them to manipulate or monitor agentic workflows without triggering authentication alerts.

KEY TAKEAWAY Immediately audit all Flowise deployments (cloud and self-hosted) and upgrade to version 3.1.0 or later; if patching is not immediately possible, firewall the `/api/v1/loginmethod` endpoint and rotate all OAuth client secrets for affected integrations today.
#2
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Catalogued Adversarial Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

MITRE's formal classification of LLM jailbreaking (AML.T0054) under the Defence Evasion tactic gives UK FinServ security teams a structured framework to assess risk across internally deployed or third-party LLM products, including copilots, customer-facing chatbots, and fraud detection assistants. Regulatory expectations around model governance (e.g., FCA PS7/24 on AI) mean firms need documented controls against known adversarial ML techniques. This technique can lead to data leakage, generation of malicious commands, or extraction of system prompt content — all material risks in a regulated environment.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries directly exploit LLM safety mechanisms via adversarial prompting or weight modification to override guardrails, enabling them to extract proprietary data, generate harmful content, or pivot the model into unintended operational roles within the target firm's infrastructure.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map your deployed LLMs against MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054 today — specifically verify that jailbreak resistance is included in your model acceptance testing criteria and red-team scope, and ensure system prompts are not recoverable via prompt injection.
#3
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as Distinct Attack Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms deploy agentic AI systems for tasks such as trade research, compliance monitoring, and customer servicing, the attack surface for resource exhaustion expands significantly — malicious prompt injection can coerce agents into runaway API call loops, inflating cloud costs and degrading service availability, both of which carry regulatory and operational consequences. This technique is distinct from traditional DDoS and may not be caught by existing availability monitoring. The financial impact includes both direct API budget burn and potential SLA breaches on regulated services.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use prompt injection or tool data poisoning to embed directives within content processed by an AI agent, instructing it to perform excessive or redundant tool calls that consume compute and API quotas — effectively weaponising the agent against its own infrastructure operator.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and budget caps on all agentic AI tool calls at the orchestration layer, and add anomaly detection for unusual API call volume patterns originating from AI agent processes.

WHY IT MATTERS

The launch of the ETS Connect UK bond consolidated tape centralises real-time price and trade data for the entire UK bond market for the first time, representing a significant new critical data infrastructure dependency for market participants. For security engineers, a centralised tape creates a high-value single point of failure — integrity or availability attacks against this feed could distort market perception or trigger automated trading decisions. Firms consuming this feed in algorithmic or AI-driven trading systems should assess its trustworthiness as an upstream data source.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's data ingestion pipeline for the bond consolidated tape feed — ensure integrity validation (e.g., cryptographic signing or anomaly thresholds) is applied before the data reaches any automated trading, risk, or AI/ML model that acts on market prices.

MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Catalogued Adversarial Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms deploying LLM-based tools (e.g., customer-facing chatbots, internal copilots, fraud triage assistants) face a formally documented threat: adversaries can bypass safety guardrails to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs. MITRE's cataloguing under Defense Evasion (AML.TA0007) means this should now be mapped into your firm's AI risk framework and threat models. Regulatory expectations around model governance (FCA, PRA) make it essential to demonstrate controls against this class of attack.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors directly exploit LLM safety mechanisms via adversarial prompting or weight modification to cause data leakage, generate malicious commands, or extract system prompt information — making the AI itself the attack surface.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map AML.T0054 into your firm's AI/ML threat model today and implement red-team jailbreak testing against any production LLM, ensuring failed safety checks are logged and alerted rather than silently bypassed.
#2
MITRE ATLAS Documents Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion Attack via Prompt Injection
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems for tasks like research automation, regulatory reporting, and transaction monitoring — all of which involve expensive API calls and external tool use. An attacker able to inject directives into an agent's input pipeline could rapidly exhaust API budgets, degrade service availability, or trigger runaway cloud spend, with direct operational and financial impact. This technique chains with prompt injection (AML.T0051), meaning any externally-facing AI agent ingesting untrusted data is a potential entry point.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit prompt injection or data poisoning to embed directives inside content the AI agent processes, coercing it into performing thousands of unnecessary tool calls or API queries to cause resource exhaustion or denial of service.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and cost-cap guardrails on all agentic AI API calls, and ensure agents processing any external or user-supplied content are sandboxed with strict tool-call quotas and anomaly alerting on usage spikes.
#3
Unauthenticated SQL Injection in WordPress Plugin Exploitable Without Valid Nonce
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

While this vulnerability affects a WordPress plugin rather than core financial infrastructure, many UK financial services firms operate WordPress-based marketing, portal, or documentation sites that could be in scope for PCI DSS or hold customer-facing functionality. The combination of no effective authentication, a bypassed nonce check (due to a commented-out `die()`), and PHP type-juggling making the SQL injection trivially exploitable on PHP < 8.0 means this is a low-barrier, high-impact flaw. If exploited, attackers could read, modify, or delete database contents on any affected site.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit your firm's WordPress estate immediately for the ClearSale Total plugin at versions ≤ 3.4.2, patch or remove it, and confirm all WordPress instances are running PHP 8.0 or above as a defence-in-depth measure against type-juggling vulnerabilities.

Not applicable.

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms rapidly deploy LLM-powered tools (e.g., customer service bots, document analysis, fraud triage), jailbreaking represents a direct path to extracting sensitive data, generating fraudulent content, or bypassing compliance guardrails. MITRE's formal cataloguing under ATLAS (AML.T0054) means this technique now has a structured taxonomy your threat modelling and red-team exercises should explicitly reference. The FCA increasingly expects firms to demonstrate robust AI governance — an unmitigated jailbreak risk in a customer-facing LLM could constitute a control failure under SYSC and operational resilience frameworks.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

This technique IS the AI attack — adversaries craft adversarial prompts or tamper with model weights to override safety mechanisms, enabling data leakage, harmful content generation, or misuse of the model as an attack tool within a firm's own infrastructure.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map your deployed LLMs against AML.T0054 today — document what guardrails exist, test them with adversarial prompts in a controlled red-team exercise, and ensure outputs are monitored for policy violations at the application layer, not just the model layer.
#2
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as Exploitable Attack Surface
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms piloting agentic AI systems — autonomous agents that call external APIs, query data sources, or execute workflows — are exposed to a denial-of-budget and denial-of-service attack vector that most security teams are not yet monitoring. An attacker using prompt injection to force an agent into thousands of redundant API calls could exhaust rate limits, spike cloud costs, or degrade availability of critical financial processes. This is particularly relevant where agentic systems interact with market data feeds, payment APIs, or regulatory reporting pipelines.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors leverage LLM Prompt Injection (AML.T0051) or AI Agent Tool Data Poisoning (AML.T0099) to embed directives inside untrusted data the agent processes, coercing it into wasteful or disruptive tool call patterns without direct system access.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard limits on tool call counts, API spend, and execution time for any agentic AI system in your environment today, and treat all data ingested by an agent from external sources as untrusted input subject to input validation controls.
#3
Path Traversal Vulnerability in WebP Server Go Allows Unauthenticated File Read on Windows (CVSS 7.5)
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

WebP Server Go is an open-source image optimisation proxy that may be present in financial services web infrastructure, developer tooling, or internal portals — particularly in firms with modern web stacks or DevOps pipelines running on Windows. The vulnerability requires no authentication and exploits a subtle discrepancy between Go's path normalisation and Windows filesystem APIs, meaning standard WAF rules that look for literal backslashes may not detect percent-encoded bypass attempts (%5C). Arbitrary file reads could expose configuration files, credentials, or internal data accessible to the server process.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit your Windows-based infrastructure for WebP Server Go instances at or below version 0.14.4, apply the patch or restrict network access immediately, and add detection for percent-encoded path traversal patterns (%5C, %2F variants) in your WAF and web server logs.

Financial services firms piloting agentic AI systems — such as automated research agents, compliance assistants, or trade data processors — face a novel denial-of-budget attack where adversaries coerce agents into runaway API calls, potentially racking up significant cloud or third-party API costs and degrading service availability. In a FinServ context, unauthorised manipulation of billing or usage records could constitute fraud and trigger regulatory reporting obligations. The CVSS 7.6 score reflects meaningful real-world exploitability for any authenticated user, not just privileged accounts.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit your software inventory for Capgo installations and ensure any instance is patched to version 12.128.2 or later; also review Supabase RPC endpoint permissions to confirm no unnecessary EXECUTE grants exist on SECURITY DEFINER functions.

WHY IT MATTERS

An adversary injecting directives to trigger thousands of redundant API calls could exhaust cloud budgets, degrade service availability, or trigger rate-limiting that disrupts legitimate operations, all of which carry regulatory implications under DORA's ICT availability requirements. For a financial services firm, the phishing and deepfake generation use cases are immediately material given the sector's exposure to business email compromise and authorised push payment fraud. Threat actors obtain and self-host generative AI models to autonomously produce phishing content, deepfakes, and malicious commands — effectively using AI as an offensive force multiplier that operates entirely outside observable commercial AI infrastructure.

WHY IT MATTERS

Therese Chambers' speech signals that the FCA's enforcement approach to financial crime is sustained and resource-intensive, with lengthy investigations preceding public action — meaning firms should not interpret an absence of visible regulatory activity as reduced scrutiny. For UK financial services security and compliance teams, this reinforces the importance of maintaining robust, continuous financial crime controls rather than reacting only to headline enforcement actions. The speech also contextualises the regulatory environment in which AI-assisted financial crime (e.g. AI-generated fraud, money laundering via automated layering) will increasingly be scrutinised.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Use this speech as a prompt to review your financial crime controls documentation and ensure your audit trail is complete and defensible — the FCA's enforcement timeline means today's control gaps may surface in an investigation years from now.
#5
FCA Warns Consumers of AI-Amplified Misleading Social Media Ads in Car Finance Claims
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's identification of deceptive social media promotions misusing logos and impersonating trusted entities is a direct signal that synthetic or misleading digital content targeting financial consumers is on the regulator's radar, with implications for firms whose brand assets are being misappropriated. Financial services firms have a reputational and regulatory interest in monitoring for unauthorised use of their brand, logos, and public figures in fraudulent ad campaigns — a threat that generative AI makes significantly easier to scale. This also previews the type of consumer harm narrative the FCA may use to justify tighter controls on AI-generated marketing content.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

While not explicitly stated in the article, the scalable production of convincing fake "money tips" personas and misappropriated brand imagery is consistent with AI-assisted content generation techniques that lower the cost of large-scale deceptive advertising campaigns.

KEY TAKEAWAY Set up automated brand monitoring today to detect unauthorised use of your firm's logos, executive likenesses, and trademarks in social media advertising, and establish a clear takedown process aligned with your FCA obligations. ---

#1
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Catalogued Adversarial Technique — Time to Audit Your AI Guardrails
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms deploying LLMs for customer service, fraud detection, or decision support face a formally catalogued threat where adversaries bypass safety controls to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs. The ATLAS framework classification (AML.T0054) means this technique is now mappable to your threat model and red team exercises, which is increasingly expected under FCA governance requirements for AI systems. Jailbreaks can directly enable downstream harms such as data leakage or generation of fraudulent content — both material risks for FinServ firms operating under Consumer Duty.

Threat actors use adversarial prompting techniques — crafted inputs designed to confuse or override alignment mechanisms — to bypass LLM guardrails, enabling exfiltration of system prompts, customer data, or generation of phishing content at scale.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map AML.T0054 into your existing threat model today and schedule adversarial prompt injection testing against any internally deployed or third-party LLM integrations, documenting results as evidence for FCA AI governance enquiries.
#2
FCA Sets Out AI Governance Expectations for Financial Services — SM&CR and Consumer Duty Are Your Compliance Anchors
🔗 FCA News AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA has confirmed it will not introduce standalone AI regulations but will instead enforce AI governance through existing frameworks — specifically Consumer Duty, SM&CR, and model risk controls — meaning accountability for AI failures sits explicitly with named Senior Managers today. For security engineers, this means AI model testing, outcome monitoring, and explainability controls are not aspirational best practice but regulatory expectations that could trigger enforcement action. Firms should expect supervisory scrutiny on how they oversee third-party AI vendors and embedded AI tools.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a regulatory policy article, though the FCA's focus on model monitoring indirectly addresses risks from adversarially manipulated AI outputs.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's AI inventory and confirm that each deployed model has a named Senior Manager accountable for its governance, testing cadence, and outcome monitoring — document this mapping as FCA engagement is actively ongoing with industry.
#3
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as a Prompt Injection-Driven DoS Vector
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms begin deploying agentic AI systems — automated workflows that call external APIs, databases, and tools — adversaries can weaponise prompt injection to force excessive tool calls, burning API budgets and degrading service availability, which carries operational resilience implications under FCA and PRA rules. This technique (AML.T0034.002) is particularly relevant for firms using LLM agents in back-office automation or customer-facing orchestration pipelines where untrusted input reaches the agent. The financial cost of runaway API consumption and the availability impact on critical services make this both a security and an operational risk.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit LLM prompt injection or tool data poisoning to coerce agentic systems into performing thousands of redundant, expensive API calls — effectively using the AI agent as an amplifier for a denial-of-service or cost-exhaustion attack.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and budget caps on all agentic AI tool calls today, and ensure any user-controlled input that reaches an AI agent is treated as untrusted and sanitised before it influences tool invocation logic.
#4
Command Injection RCE in galaxy_ng Legacy Role API Allows Authenticated Remote Code Execution
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

CVE-2026-12398 (CVSS 7.5) affects galaxy_ng, the backend powering Ansible Automation Hub, which is used in enterprise infrastructure automation pipelines — relevant to financial services firms using Ansible for infrastructure-as-code or compliance automation. An authenticated attacker with control of a git repository can inject shell metacharacters into branch or tag names to achieve remote code execution on the Pulp worker, potentially pivoting into broader infrastructure. While the vulnerable endpoint requires a non-default configuration flag (GALAXY_ENABLE_LEGACY_ROLES=True), organisations that have enabled legacy role imports for migration purposes are at real risk.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a classic command injection vulnerability with no AI/ML component.

KEY TAKEAWAY Check immediately whether GALAXY_ENABLE_LEGACY_ROLES is set to True in any galaxy_ng deployment in your environment, and if so, disable it or apply available patches and restrict access to the legacy role import endpoint pending remediation.

Firms have a Consumer Duty obligation to consider harms to customers occurring outside their direct channels, including third-party impersonation of their brand.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

While not explicitly stated in the article, AI tools can trivially scale the production of convincing fake personas, synthetic endorsement imagery, and tailored ad copy — lowering the barrier for claims fraud operators to impersonate trusted brands at volume.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run a search today across major social media platforms for adverts misusing your firm's logo or brand in the context of motor finance claims, and establish a takedown process with your fraud and brand protection teams ahead of anticipated claim volumes increasing. ---

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems (e.g., automated research assistants, workflow automation, or AI-driven trading tools), adversaries can exploit prompt injection to trigger runaway API calls that exhaust operational budgets and degrade service availability. This represents both a financial cost risk and a potential availability/DoS risk to critical business processes. The technique is particularly relevant where agentic AI has access to external APIs, data feeds, or transactional systems common in FinServ environments.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors leverage LLM prompt injection or AI agent tool data poisoning to coerce agentic systems into performing unnecessary, high-volume, or computationally expensive tool calls, effectively weaponising the agent's own capabilities against the operator's infrastructure and budget.

UK FinServ firms running external-facing WordPress sites (e.g., for branch finders, event bookings, or marketing microsites) may have unpatched legacy plugins that are newly searchable by attackers using CVE databases. The late cataloguing of old vulnerabilities is a known attacker reconnaissance opportunity.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a legacy web application vulnerability with no AI/ML component described in the article.

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems — autonomous agents that call external APIs, execute workflows, and access data stores — this formally catalogued technique highlights a real availability and financial risk: adversaries can coerce agents into runaway API calls that exhaust budgets and degrade services. In a FinServ context, this could disrupt automated trading assistants, customer onboarding agents, or fraud investigation pipelines. The technique chains with prompt injection (AML.T0051) and tool data poisoning (AML.T0099), meaning a single malicious input could trigger cascading resource abuse.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors embed directives via prompt injection or poisoned tool data to force agentic AI systems into excessive, unnecessary, or recursive API calls — effectively using the AI agent as an amplifier for denial-of-service or cost-exhaustion attacks against the firm's infrastructure.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits, per-session API call budgets, and anomaly alerting on all agentic AI tool invocations, and ensure any external data sources feeding agents are treated as untrusted input requiring validation.

Not applicable — this is a traditional memory corruption vulnerability with no AI/ML component described in the advisory.

#3
Critical Heap Overflow in 389 Directory Server SASL Layer Enables RCE for Any Domain User
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

389 Directory Server underpins FreeIPA and Red Hat Identity Management, which are used for centralised authentication and authorisation in many hybrid Linux environments within UK financial services. The fact that any valid domain user, enrolled host, or service account can trigger this heap overflow post-SASL bind dramatically widens the attack surface — insider threat and compromised service accounts are realistic vectors in regulated environments. A successful exploit yields DoS or full RCE against a core identity infrastructure component, which could cascade into authentication outages or privilege escalation.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a classical memory corruption vulnerability with no AI/ML component.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit your environment for 389-ds-base and FreeIPA deployments, apply available patches or vendor mitigations immediately, and review whether nsslapd-maxsasliosize limits are correctly enforced as a compensating control pending patching.
#4
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Consumption Attack — Prompt Injection Can Drain API Budgets and Cause Outages
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms pilot agentic AI systems for tasks such as customer service automation, transaction analysis, and report generation, adversarial resource consumption attacks represent both an operational resilience risk and a financial cost risk — both areas of direct FCA scrutiny. An attacker able to inject directives that trigger excessive API calls or tool fan-outs could degrade service availability or exhaust costly third-party API quotas, potentially constituting a denial-of-service against business-critical workflows. This technique is particularly insidious because it may not trigger traditional security alerts, appearing as legitimate — if excessive — system activity.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit prompt injection (AML.T0051) or tool data poisoning (AML.T0099) to embed directives within agent inputs that coerce the AI into performing thousands of unnecessary, expensive operations, effectively weaponising the agent's own capabilities against its operator.

KEY TAKEAWAY Before deploying any agentic AI system to production, implement hard rate limits and cost caps on all tool calls and API integrations, and add anomaly detection for unusual tool call volumes or patterns that could indicate prompt injection in progress.

11 June 2026 5 stories
#1
Critical Splunk RCE via Unsafe Deserialization Puts Security Monitoring Infrastructure at Risk
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Splunk is a cornerstone SIEM and monitoring platform across UK financial services, making this CVSS 8.8 RCE particularly dangerous — a low-privileged user (e.g. a compromised analyst account) can achieve full code execution without admin rights. The vulnerability stems from unsafe deserialisation via the `jsonpickle` library in Splunk Secure Gateway, a well-understood class of bug that is reliably exploitable. FCA-regulated firms relying on Splunk for audit logging and fraud detection could have their monitoring capability silently compromised or destroyed.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Immediately patch Splunk Enterprise to 10.2.4 / 10.0.7 / 9.4.12 / 9.3.13 or above and Splunk Secure Gateway to 3.10.6+ today; if patching is not immediately possible, disable or restrict access to the Splunk Secure Gateway app as a temporary mitigation and audit low-privileged user activity against KV Store endpoints.
#2
Splunk SSRF Flaw Allows Internal Network Traversal via Trivially Bypassed Domain Allowlist
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

This CVSS 7.6 Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in Splunk's Dashboard Studio PDF export feature could allow a low-privileged attacker to probe and interact with internal services — including cloud metadata endpoints, internal APIs, or segregated network segments — which is a significant concern in financial services environments with complex internal network topologies. The bypass technique (prefix-match spoofing via attacker-controlled subdomains combined with unchecked redirect following) is simple to execute and requires no special tooling. In a UK FinServ context, this could be used as a stepping stone to reach internal treasury, payments, or data lake infrastructure.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Patch to the fixed Splunk Cloud Platform or Enterprise versions listed in the advisory, and in parallel review your Splunk deployment's network egress rules to ensure the PDF export service cannot reach sensitive internal subnets or cloud metadata services (e.g. 169.254.169.254).

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security

The formal inclusion of LLM jailbreaking (AML.T0054) in MITRE ATLAS gives UK financial services firms a standardised framework reference for threat modelling AI deployments — critical as firms integrate LLMs into customer-facing services, fraud detection, and internal copilots. Jailbreaks can lead to data leakage of system prompts (potentially exposing business logic or PII), generation of harmful or fraudulent content, and defence evasion against model-level guardrails. Regulatory expectations around AI governance (FCA's AI Sprint outcomes, PRA Dear CEO letters on model risk) will increasingly require firms to demonstrate they have assessed and mitigated this class of risk.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors directly weaponise jailbreaks to bypass content filters on enterprise LLMs, extracting confidential system prompt instructions, leaking training data, or generating outputs (e.g. phishing scripts, fraudulent communications) that the model was explicitly designed to refuse.

#4
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Consumption as Viable DoS and Budget-Drain Attack Vector
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms deploy agentic AI systems (e.g. autonomous research assistants, workflow automation, customer service agents with tool-use capabilities), this ATLAS technique highlights a practical availability and cost risk: adversaries can coerce agents into runaway API calls that exhaust rate limits, blow cloud cost budgets, and degrade service. In a FinServ context, an agentic system used for trade research, client onboarding, or regulatory reporting could be manipulated into denial-of-service conditions affecting business operations. This also intersects with operational resilience obligations under DORA and FCA PS21/3.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors leverage prompt injection or tool data poisoning to embed directives inside agent-accessible data sources, causing the AI agent to autonomously trigger excessive, expensive tool calls — effectively a low-effort, indirect denial-of-service that may evade traditional rate-limit monitoring.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard per-session and per-user API call budgets and execution time limits on all agentic AI deployments, and ensure anomaly alerting is in place for tool-call volume spikes — treat runaway agent behaviour as a security event, not just an operational one.
#5
SQL Injection in Ghidra's Password Change Function Enables Privilege Escalation to Database Superuser
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Ghidra is widely used by security engineering and reverse engineering teams in financial services for malware analysis and vulnerability research; a CVSS 8.8 SQL injection allowing privilege escalation to PostgreSQL superuser means an attacker with any authenticated access to a shared Ghidra instance can gain full control of the underlying database and all stored analysis artefacts. In a threat intelligence or SOC context, Ghidra instances may hold sensitive reverse-engineered malware samples, proprietary tooling, or analysis of firm-specific threats. Compromise of this tooling could expose operational security tradecraft.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Upgrade Ghidra to version 12.1 or later immediately; if running a shared or multi-user Ghidra deployment, audit current user accounts for unexpected privilege levels and treat any shared PostgreSQL backend as potentially compromised pending patch confirmation. ---

Threat actors use prompt injection or tool data poisoning to coerce agentic AI systems into performing excessive, unnecessary tool calls — effectively weaponising the agent's autonomy to cause denial-of-service or financial harm via API cost inflation.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and budget caps on all agentic AI tool calls, and set up cost anomaly alerting on AI API usage so unexpected consumption spikes are flagged to the security team immediately.
#4
FCA Warns Consumers Over Deceptive AI-Amplified Car Finance Claims Ads on Social Media
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

Claims management companies are running covert paid promotions disguised as organic personal finance advice, misusing logos of trusted institutions — a social engineering threat that directly undermines consumer trust in legitimate financial brands. For UK FinServ firms, this highlights the brand impersonation risk landscape that your threat intelligence programme should be monitoring, as your firm's logo or executive imagery could be similarly misused. The FCA's active intervention signals regulatory appetite to pursue misleading financial promotions, which could create compliance exposure if your firm's brand is co-opted without a takedown response.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable based on article content, though AI-generated synthetic personas and deepfake imagery are commonly used in this category of social media fraud at scale.

KEY TAKEAWAY Set up automated brand monitoring (e.g., via social media scanning tools) to detect unauthorised use of your firm's logos, imagery, or executive names in paid promotions, and establish a takedown escalation process.
#5
CVSS 8.2 SQL Injection in WordPress Simply Poll Plugin — Unauthenticated Database Extraction Risk
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

While a WordPress plugin vulnerability may seem peripheral, financial services firms frequently run WordPress instances for public-facing marketing sites or blogs that may share infrastructure or credentials with internal systems. An unauthenticated SQL injection via a public-facing endpoint (admin-ajax.php) scoring 8.2 CVSS represents a straightforward path to database exfiltration with no authentication required. If any WordPress instance in your estate runs Simply Poll 1.4.1, sensitive data exposure is trivial for an attacker.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a classic SQL injection vulnerability with no AI/ML component described in the article.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run an immediate audit of all WordPress instances in your organisation's estate to identify any using the Simply Poll plugin version 1.4.1, and remove or update it — also review whether any WordPress databases contain or have access to sensitive financial or customer data. ---

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

MITRE ATLAS cataloguing this as AML.T0054 means it now has a formal taxonomy that security and compliance teams can reference in risk assessments and threat models. #3
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as Viable Attack Vector
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems for automated workflows such as transaction monitoring, report generation, and customer servicing — all of which involve real API budgets and compute costs. An adversary who can coerce an agent into runaway tool calls could inflate operational costs significantly or degrade service availability, both of which carry regulatory and reputational consequences in a UK FinServ context. This technique is particularly relevant where agentic systems have access to external data sources or third-party APIs.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit prompt injection or tool data poisoning to issue directives that cause the AI agent to perform excessive, unnecessary, or recursive tool calls, consuming API quotas and compute resources in a denial-of-resource attack.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and cost-cap guardrails on all agentic AI tool calls, and ensure prompt injection defences are in place before any agent is granted access to external APIs or paid third-party services.
#4
Critical RCE Vulnerability in OpenBullet2 Enables Full System Compromise via Path Traversal
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

OpenBullet2 is widely used by credential-stuffing threat actors to automate account takeover attacks against financial services platforms — meaning this RCE vulnerability (CVSS 8.8) could allow attackers to compromise infrastructure used specifically to target UK banks and FinTechs. While firms themselves are unlikely to run OpenBullet2, understanding attacker toolchain vulnerabilities informs threat intelligence on adversary capability and potential disruption opportunities. Security teams should be aware this tool — and compromised instances of it — may be weaponised in credential attacks against their own login endpoints.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security

MITRE ATLAS cataloguing LLM jailbreaking as a formal adversarial technique (AML.T0054) means it is now part of structured threat modelling frameworks that security teams should be incorporating into AI risk assessments. For UK FinServ firms deploying LLMs in customer-facing or internal tooling, jailbreaks can lead to data leakage, generation of harmful content, or circumvention of compliance guardrails — all with direct regulatory consequences under Consumer Duty. #3
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Consumption Attack — Prompt Injection Can Drain API Budgets and Cause Denial of Service
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms adopt agentic AI systems (e.g. automated research, compliance checking, or customer service agents with tool access), adversaries can exploit prompt injection to trigger runaway API calls, excessive compute consumption, or budget exhaustion — effectively a DoS against AI-powered workflows. This has direct operational resilience implications, particularly relevant under FCA/PRA operational resilience requirements and DORA for firms in scope. The attack requires no privileged access — malicious content in processed data (e.g. a document the agent reads) can be sufficient to trigger it.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors embed directives within data sources processed by AI agents (via prompt injection or tool data poisoning) to coerce the agent into performing thousands of unnecessary tool calls or API queries, exhausting compute budgets and degrading service availability without any direct system compromise.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and cost caps on all agentic AI tool calls, and treat any data ingested by an AI agent as untrusted input subject to sanitisation — particularly content from external sources such as emails, documents, or web pages.
#4
Comodo Firewall Driver Integer Underflow Allows Remote Pre-Firewall Code Execution via Crafted IPv6 Packet
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

This CVSS 7.5 vulnerability in Comodo Internet Security's kernel-mode firewall driver (Inspect.sys) is particularly dangerous because the IPv6 parsing occurs before firewall rules are enforced — meaning a single crafted packet can trigger an out-of-bounds read even on hosts with all ports blocked. Any endpoint running Comodo Internet Security in a FinServ environment (including developer workstations or legacy systems) may be remotely exploitable with no authentication required. Kernel-mode exploitation could lead to full system compromise or privilege escalation.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a traditional memory corruption vulnerability in a security driver, with no AI/ML component described in the article.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit your estate immediately for Comodo Internet Security deployments and check for vendor patches or mitigations; if none are available, consider disabling IPv6 on affected hosts as a temporary workaround until a fix is applied.
#5

🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

While SourceCodester's Class and Exam Timetabling System is unlikely to be a core financial services application, its presence in the NVD with a publicly available exploit serves as a reminder that open-source or third-party scheduling/admin tools in use across a firm's broader estate (e.g. in HR, training, or facilities) can introduce SQL injection risk if unpatched. The CVSS 7.3 score and remote exploitability with no authentication noted make this trivially weaponisable. Supply chain and shadow IT risk are relevant here for FinServ firms with complex application portfolios.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a straightforward SQL injection vulnerability with no AI/ML component described in the article.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run a software asset inventory check to confirm whether any instance of this or similar SourceCodester applications exists in your environment, and enforce a policy requiring security review before open-source or freeware web applications are deployed anywhere on the corporate network. ---

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems for tasks such as data retrieval, report generation, and customer service automation, this MITRE ATLAS technique (AML.T0034.002) highlights how adversaries can weaponise those agents against the firm's own API budgets and compute resources through prompt injection or data poisoning. The financial impact is twofold: direct cost from excessive API consumption and indirect operational disruption if agents become unavailable or degraded — both relevant to operational resilience requirements under FCA/PRA rules. This is a low-sophistication attack vector that could be triggered by malicious user input in a multi-tenant or customer-accessible agentic deployment.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries exploit LLM prompt injection or AI agent tool data poisoning to coerce agentic systems into performing unnecessary, expensive, or repetitive tool calls, effectively conducting a cost-based or availability denial-of-service against AI infrastructure.

#4
Critical OpenShift Credential Operator Flaw Grants Account-Wide AWS Destructive Permissions — Cross-Scope Blast Radius
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

CVE-2026-10843 (CVSS 7.2) affects the OpenShift Cloud Credential Operator in AWS Mint-mode, provisioning operator credentials with account-wide scope for destructive actions rather than restricting them to cluster-owned resources — a serious least-privilege failure. For UK FinServ firms running OpenShift workloads on AWS (common in hybrid cloud and containerised application strategies), a single credential compromise could enable an adversary to cause destructive impact far beyond the intended cluster boundary, affecting production data or infrastructure. This has direct implications for cloud security posture, incident blast radius, and operational resilience obligations.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY If you run OpenShift on AWS in Mint-mode, immediately audit the IAM policies attached to operator credentials and apply the principle of least privilege to restrict permissions to cluster-scoped resources only — check for vendor patches and apply them promptly.

Not applicable.

MITRE ATLAS formalising LLM jailbreak as technique AML.T0054 means it is now part of the structured adversarial ML threat framework that mature security programmes should be mapping controls against — analogous to how MITRE ATT&CK techniques drive detection engineering. For a UK FinServ firm deploying any LLM (customer-facing chatbots, internal copilots, document summarisation tools), jailbreaking represents a direct path to data leakage of sensitive customer or market data and generation of malicious outputs. The technique explicitly chains to LLM Data Leakage (AML.T0057) and System Information Discovery (AML.T0069), both high-impact in a regulated financial context.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use adversarial prompting or model weight modification to circumvent safety guardrails, enabling exfiltration of data the LLM has been exposed to (e.g., customer PII, internal policy documents) or weaponisation of the model to generate harmful or fraudulent content at scale.

KEY TAKEAWAY If your firm operates any LLM-based service, run a structured jailbreak assessment against it today using MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054 as your test framework, and ensure output filtering and prompt injection detection controls are logged and alerting — not just silently blocking.
#4
MITRE ATLAS Catalogues Agentic AI Resource Consumption Attack — Prompt Injection Drives API Budget Exhaustion
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems (autonomous research agents, workflow automation, API-integrated copilots), this MITRE ATLAS technique documents a concrete availability attack vector: coercing an agent into excessive, wasteful tool calls through prompt injection or data poisoning to exhaust API budgets and degrade service. In a FinServ context where agentic AI may be touching trading data feeds, compliance workflows, or customer service systems, a resource exhaustion attack could constitute an operational resilience incident reportable to the FCA. The low barrier to execution (natural language directives are sufficient) makes this accessible to unsophisticated threat actors.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries use LLM prompt injection (AML.T0051) or AI Agent Tool Data Poisoning (AML.T0099) to embed directives that force agentic systems into computationally expensive loops — unnecessary API fan-outs, repeated summarisation, or mass translation tasks — as a denial-of-service mechanism.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement rate limiting, maximum tool-call budgets, and anomaly detection on agentic AI API usage patterns now — before production deployment — and include resource exhaustion scenarios explicitly in your AI system threat model.
#5
Publicly Exploitable SQL Injection (CVSS 7.3) Identified in Online Blood Bank Management System
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

CVE-2026-10249 is a remotely exploitable, publicly available SQL injection vulnerability in a specific web application (itsourcecode Online Blood Bank Management System 1.0); this is unlikely to be directly relevant to a UK financial services firm's production environment. However, it serves as a reminder that SQL injection remains a live, weaponised vulnerability class, and any legacy or third-party web applications in your supply chain with similar parameter-handling patterns (particularly admin interfaces) carry the same risk profile. The public exploit availability means automated scanning and exploitation is essentially immediate upon disclosure.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Use this CVE as a prompt to verify your vulnerability scanning coverage includes all admin-facing web interfaces and third-party supplied applications, and confirm that SQLi is included in your next web application penetration test scope — particularly for any legacy systems that may not receive rapid patching. ---

MITRE ATLAS Codifies LLM Jailbreak as Formal Adversarial Technique for Defence Evasion
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

MITRE ATLAS formalising LLM jailbreak as technique AML.T0054 means security teams now have a structured, vendor-neutral reference to build detection and control frameworks around AI system misuse — critical for any UK FinServ firm deploying LLMs in customer-facing or internal tooling. The technique covers both prompt-based and weight-modification attack paths, broadening the scope beyond simple prompt injection to include supply chain and model integrity concerns. This is directly relevant to firms using third-party LLM APIs or hosted models where guardrail integrity cannot be fully verified.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors can use jailbreaks to bypass safety guardrails, enabling LLM data leakage, extraction of system prompts, or generation of malicious commands — all of which are downstream techniques also catalogued in ATLAS.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map AML.T0054 to your AI system inventory today — for each deployed LLM, document what guardrails are in place, who controls them, and whether you have logging to detect anomalous or policy-violating outputs that may indicate a jailbreak attempt.
#4
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Consumption as Emerging DoS and Cost-Drain Attack Vector
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems — autonomous agents with access to APIs, databases, and external tools — this technique highlights a low-sophistication attack path that can cause significant operational and financial damage through API budget exhaustion or service degradation. This is particularly relevant for firms using LLM-powered workflow automation or copilot-style tools where prompt injection via untrusted data sources (e.g., emails, documents) is a realistic attack vector. The financial cost dimension (API billing abuse) adds a novel risk category that may not yet be covered by existing operational resilience frameworks.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries exploit prompt injection or data poisoning to coerce agentic AI systems into executing unnecessary, expensive tool calls — effectively weaponising the agent's autonomy against the deploying organisation's resources and availability.

KEY TAKEAWAY If your firm runs any agentic AI pipelines, implement rate limiting and cost alerting on all API tool calls today, and ensure agents cannot be directed by untrusted input sources without human-in-the-loop validation at key decision points.
#5
EOL TRENDnet Router Carries Public CVSS 8.8 Stack Overflow Exploit With No Patch Available
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

A remotely exploitable stack-based buffer overflow with a public exploit and no vendor patch represents a tangible network perimeter risk for any organisation still running this 15-year-EOL TRENDnet device — the vendor has confirmed it will not remediate. While niche in a large enterprise context, UK FinServ firms with legacy branch office or out-of-band network infrastructure should treat any EOL network device as a high-priority asset management concern given FCA operational resilience requirements. The public exploit availability means this is immediately weaponisable with minimal attacker skill.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run a query against your network asset inventory and CMDB today to confirm no TRENDnet TEW-432BRP devices are present in your environment — if found, isolate and replace immediately given the absence of any patch path and the availability of a public exploit. ---

#4
Path Traversal Vulnerability in DeepCode Exposes SSH Keys and TLS Certificates to Unauthenticated Attackers
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

DeepCode is an AI-powered code analysis tool, meaning this vulnerability sits directly at the intersection of AI tooling and supply chain risk — both critical concerns for UK FinServ security teams adopting AI-assisted development. An unauthenticated attacker exploiting this path traversal (CVSS 7.5) can exfiltrate SSH private keys, TLS certificates, and application secrets with a single HTTP request, which could enable lateral movement or full infrastructure compromise. Firms using DeepCode in CI/CD pipelines or developer environments should treat this as urgent.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a classical web vulnerability in an AI tooling product, not an attack leveraging AI/ML techniques by a threat actor.

KEY TAKEAWAY Immediately identify whether DeepCode (any version through commit c991dc2) is present in your development or security toolchain, isolate or patch affected instances, and rotate any credentials or certificates that may have been accessible via the exposed endpoint.
#5
MITRE ATLAS Catalogues Agentic Resource Consumption as Denial-of-Service Vector Against AI Systems
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

KEY TAKEAWAY Before deploying any agentic AI system in production, implement hard rate limits and cost caps on tool calls and API queries, and include resource exhaustion scenarios in your AI red-teaming exercises. ---

#4
Keycloak Privilege Escalation Flaw Allows Token Manipulation via Role Injection — CVSS 7.3
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Keycloak is widely used as an identity and access management (IAM) platform in financial services environments, including cloud-native and hybrid architectures. This vulnerability allows a limited-privilege administrator to escalate to highly privileged roles by injecting realm roles into client scope mappings, which then propagate into users' authentication tokens — a serious lateral movement and privilege escalation risk. In a FinServ context, this could allow an insider threat or compromised admin account to gain access to high-value systems or data without triggering conventional access control alerts.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit your Keycloak deployments immediately for instances of Fine-Grained Admin Permissions (FGAPv2) in use, review which administrators hold client management permissions, and apply vendor patches or mitigations as soon as available — treating any unpatched instance as a high-priority remediation given the token-injection privilege escalation path.
#5
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Consumption as Emerging Denial-of-Service Attack Vector
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As financial services firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems — automated workflows, AI copilots with tool access, and LLM-driven decision pipelines — the ability for adversaries to coerce these agents into excessive API calls or computationally expensive operations creates a credible operational resilience and cost risk. 🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

KEY TAKEAWAY For any agentic AI system your firm operates, implement hard rate limits and cost caps on all external API and tool calls today, and establish alerting for anomalous tool-call volumes that could indicate an active resource consumption attack or compromised prompt pipeline. ---

For UK FinServ security engineers, this means fraud detection, customer authentication, and anti-money laundering controls will face growing regulatory scrutiny tied directly to AI-enabled criminal capabilities. The organised and transnational nature of these groups means threat intelligence sharing across firms and with the FCA is becoming a supervisory expectation.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

The speech implies that criminal groups are leveraging technology — including AI tooling — to industrialise fraud operations, enabling higher-volume, more convincing attacks such as AI-generated investment scam content and deepfake impersonation.

KEY TAKEAWAY Ensure your firm's fraud detection models and customer-facing authentication controls are reviewed against AI-enabled social engineering scenarios (e.g., deepfake voice/video, synthetic identity fraud) and consider whether your firm is actively participating in FCA-facilitated intelligence-sharing initiatives.
#3
MITRE ATLAS Documents LLM Jailbreak as a Formal Adversarial Technique Enabling Defence Evasion and Data Leakage
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

MITRE's formal classification of LLM jailbreaking (AML.T0054) under the ATLAS framework gives UK FinServ teams a structured, vendor-neutral reference for threat modelling AI systems deployed within their environments — such as customer-facing chatbots, internal copilots, or fraud triage tools. Jailbreaks can lead to data leakage of system prompts or sensitive context, or cause the model to generate harmful outputs including malicious commands, directly threatening operational integrity. This is particularly relevant for firms using third-party LLM APIs where guardrail implementation and testing responsibility may be ambiguous.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries exploit jailbreak techniques specifically to circumvent AI safety controls, either through adversarial prompt crafting or model weight manipulation, enabling downstream attacks such as data exfiltration or malicious content generation from within a trusted AI pipeline.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map any LLM deployments in your firm against ATLAS technique AML.T0054 and validate that guardrail bypass scenarios are included in your AI red-teaming or penetration testing scope — do not assume vendor-supplied safety filters are sufficient without independent testing.
#4
MITRE ATLAS Flags Adversarial Use of Generative AI Tools — Including Locally-Hosted LLMs — to Power Full Attack Lifecycles
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

MITRE ATLAS technique AML.T0016.002 documents how threat actors are actively acquiring and deploying generative AI models — including open-source LLMs run locally via frameworks like Ollama or vLLM — to support attack operations including malware generation, deepfakes, phishing content, and command generation. For UK FinServ defenders, this means the barrier to sophisticated, AI-augmented attacks (spear-phishing, synthetic fraud documents, AI-written malware) has fallen dramatically, and volume and personalisation of attacks will continue to increase. The use of locally-hosted models also means these actors leave no commercial API footprint, complicating attribution and detection.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors are directly using generative AI — including uncensored locally-run open-source models — as an offensive capability multiplier across reconnaissance, weaponisation, and delivery phases, producing phishing lures, deepfakes, and malware at scale with minimal cost.

KEY TAKEAWAY Update your threat model and phishing simulation programme to account for AI-generated, highly personalised lure content — static signature-based email filtering will be insufficient, so validate that your email security controls include behavioural and contextual detection capable of catching AI-crafted spear-phishing targeting finance staff.
#5
MITRE ATLAS Identifies Agentic AI Resource Consumption Attack — Prompt Injection Can Drain API Budgets and Disrupt Operations
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms begin deploying agentic AI systems — autonomous agents that call external APIs, query databases, or orchestrate workflows — this ATLAS technique (AML.T0034.002) documents how adversaries can weaponise prompt injection or data poisoning to force excessive, costly tool calls, creating both denial-of-service conditions and significant unexpected API expenditure. For FinServ environments where agentic AI may be integrated into customer service, compliance automation, or transaction monitoring, uncontrolled resource consumption could degrade operational resilience — a direct regulatory concern under FCA/PRA operational resilience rules. This is an emerging but under-modelled risk for firms in early stages of agentic AI adoption.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries exploit prompt injection vulnerabilities in agentic AI systems to inject directives that trigger computationally expensive or high-volume tool calls, effectively using the AI agent itself as the mechanism of a resource exhaustion or cost-amplification attack.

KEY TAKEAWAY Before deploying any agentic AI system in production, implement hard rate limits, cost caps, and anomaly alerting on all external API and tool calls made by the agent, and include prompt injection resilience testing as a mandatory pre-deployment security gate. ---

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

The speech highlights that financial crime is increasingly technologically enabled, consistent with threat actors leveraging AI for fraud automation, deepfake social engineering, and scaled investment scam operations.
