#3
MITRE ATLAS Documents LLM Jailbreak as Formal Adversarial Technique — Defence Evasion at the Model Layer
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

MITRE ATLAS formalising LLM jailbreak as technique AML.T0054 means security teams now have a standardised taxonomy to map, detect, and defend against this threat — critical for UK FinServ firms deploying LLMs in customer-facing or internal tooling. A successfully jailbroken model could leak sensitive data (e.g. customer PII, internal system prompts) or generate harmful outputs, creating regulatory exposure under GDPR and FCA conduct rules. This technique is directly chained to data leakage and malicious command generation, making it a multi-stage attack risk.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use adversarial prompting or direct model weight manipulation to bypass LLM safety guardrails, enabling them to extract restricted information or generate malicious content from models that are supposed to prevent such outputs.

KEY TAKEAWAY If your firm uses any LLM-based tooling (internal chatbots, copilots, document summarisers), map AML.T0054 into your threat model today and implement prompt injection detection controls, system prompt hardening, and output filtering — then test them with red-team jailbreak attempts.
#4
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Consumption Attack — Prompt Injection Can Drain API Budgets and Disrupt Operations
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms increasingly adopt agentic AI systems (autonomous agents that call APIs, query databases, and execute workflows), this MITRE ATLAS technique highlights a practical availability and cost risk that could constitute an operational resilience incident under FCA rules. Adversaries can use prompt injection or data poisoning to force agents into expensive, repetitive, or wasteful tool calls — potentially exhausting API budgets, degrading performance, or triggering denial-of-service conditions. This is especially relevant for FinServ firms running agentic AI in trading, compliance monitoring, or customer service workflows.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors leverage LLM prompt injection (AML.T0051) or AI agent tool data poisoning (AML.T0099) to embed directives within agent inputs that trigger excessive or unnecessary computational operations, weaponising the agent's autonomy against its own operator.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement rate limiting, tool-call budgets, and anomaly detection on any agentic AI systems in your environment today — specifically monitor for unusual API call volumes or repetitive tool invocations that could indicate an active resource consumption attack.
#5
Unauthenticated SQL Injection in WP ERP Pro Plugin (CVSS 7.5) Exposes WordPress Database
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

This SQL injection vulnerability in WP ERP Pro (versions up to 1.5.1) requires no authentication, meaning any internet-exposed WordPress instance running this plugin is at immediate risk of database extraction by unskilled attackers. While WordPress may not be a core banking platform, UK FinServ firms commonly use WordPress for marketing sites, investor portals, or intranet tools — any of which may hold sensitive customer or firm data subject to GDPR and FCA data handling obligations. Unauthenticated SQLi vulnerabilities are consistently exploited within hours of public disclosure.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run an immediate inventory of all WordPress deployments across your firm's estate — including shadow IT and marketing-managed sites — and confirm whether WP ERP Pro is installed; if so, patch to a fixed version or disable the plugin immediately pending remediation. ---

In UK FinServ, where AI systems may handle sensitive customer data or generate regulatory outputs, a successful jailbreak could lead to data leakage, harmful content generation, or manipulation of AI-assisted decisions. 🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

#4
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as Emerging DoS Attack Vector via Prompt Injection
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial services firms begin deploying agentic AI systems — autonomous agents that call external APIs, query databases, and execute multi-step workflows — this ATLAS technique highlights a practical and underappreciated risk: adversaries can trigger runaway resource consumption through crafted prompts, exhausting API budgets and degrading service availability. For FinServ environments, this could translate to operational resilience failures, unexpected cloud cost spikes, or disruption of AI-assisted processes during critical periods such as market open or end-of-day settlement. This attack surface grows directly in proportion to how many tool integrations and autonomous capabilities your AI agents are granted.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries exploit prompt injection or tool data poisoning to coerce agentic AI systems into performing excessive, unnecessary, or recursive tool calls — effectively using the AI's own autonomy as a denial-of-service weapon against the organisation's infrastructure and budget.

KEY TAKEAWAY Before deploying any agentic AI system, implement hard rate limits, API call budgets, and anomaly alerting on tool invocation frequency — and ensure prompt injection defences are in place at every external input boundary.
#5
TOCTOU Race Condition in Rsync Daemon Enables Arbitrary File Write and Privilege Escalation (CVSS 7.0)
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Rsync is widely used for file synchronisation, backup pipelines, and data transfer in enterprise Linux environments — including FinServ infrastructure supporting data feeds, regulatory reporting, and log management. This TOCTOU vulnerability allows an attacker with write access to a module path to redirect file writes via symlink manipulation, potentially overwriting sensitive system files or achieving privilege escalation when the daemon runs with elevated privileges. The condition requiring `chroot = false` narrows scope but many legacy or misconfigured deployments do run without chroot.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Check all rsync daemon deployments in your environment immediately — upgrade to version 3.4.3 or later, and where patching is not immediately possible, verify that `chroot = true` is enforced in all rsync module configurations as an interim mitigation. ---

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial firms adopt agentic AI systems — autonomous agents that call external APIs, query databases, and execute multi-step workflows — this MITRE ATLAS technique (AML.T0034.002) documents a concrete denial-of-service vector where adversaries coerce agents into burning API budgets and compute resources through prompt injection or tool data poisoning. #5
Unauthenticated SQL Injection in WordPress Contest Gallery Plugin (CVSS 7.5) Exploitable via Public Nonce
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

This unauthenticated SQL injection vulnerability in the Contest Gallery WordPress plugin (versions up to 28.1.6) requires only a publicly visible nonce to exploit, meaning any external attacker can extract database contents without credentials — a significant risk for any UK FinServ firm or third-party supplier running WordPress-based public-facing sites, such as marketing portals, investor relations pages, or event registration forms that may sit adjacent to internal infrastructure. Supply chain exposure is also relevant: third-party agencies managing WordPress sites on behalf of financial firms may be running vulnerable plugins without the firm's direct visibility. The low exploitation barrier (public frontend nonce exposed in page source) means automated scanning and exploitation is highly feasible.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run an immediate inventory of all WordPress installations across your organisation and third-party web suppliers, identify any deployments of Contest Gallery plugin at or below version 28.1.6, and prioritise patching or removal — this is trivially exploitable by unauthenticated attackers with no specialist tooling required. ---

#4
Path Traversal Vulnerability in Dify AI Platform (CVSS 7.7) Exposes Internal APIs to Authenticated Attackers
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Dify is a widely used open-source LLM application development platform, and this path traversal flaw in versions up to 1.14.1 allows authenticated users to escape their tenant context and access internal debug endpoints — a serious privilege boundary violation. For FinServ firms piloting or running Dify-based AI workflows, this could expose internal REST API interfaces and multi-tenant data boundaries. The fact that Dify Cloud permits unauthenticated free self-registration significantly lowers the barrier for external attackers to obtain the authenticated access needed to exploit this.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this is a conventional path traversal vulnerability in AI platform infrastructure, though exploitation could enable an attacker to manipulate or surveil AI agent behaviour via exposed internal APIs.

KEY TAKEAWAY If Dify is in use anywhere in your environment, patch to a version beyond 1.14.1 immediately and audit tenant isolation controls; if using Dify Cloud, assume the authenticated-attacker bar is effectively zero for external threat actors.

#5
2018-Era Stack Buffer Overflow (CVSS 8.4) in Allok AVI MPEG Splitter Receives NVD Entry — Legacy Risk Reminder
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

CVE-2018-25322 affects Allok Fast AVI MPEG Splitter 1.2, a legacy media processing utility, with a stack-based buffer overflow exploitable via a malicious licence name string allowing local code execution. While this is unlikely to be present in core financial services infrastructure, legacy and shadow IT — including media processing utilities on analyst or back-office workstations — can persist undetected in large organisations and represent a local privilege escalation path. UK FinServ firms with broad software estates should treat this as a prompt to audit for end-of-life and unsupported software.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run a software asset inventory query today for end-of-life or unsupported media utilities across your endpoint estate — if Allok Fast AVI MPEG Splitter or similar legacy tools are present, prioritise removal and review your software allowlisting policy to prevent future installation. ---
15 May 2026 5 stories
#1
FCA Chief Warns Financial Crime is Faster, More Organised, and Tech-Enabled Than Ever
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

Direct regulatory signal from the FCA CEO that financial crime — particularly investment fraud averaging £25,000 per victim — is now driven by professional, cross-border organised criminal groups leveraging technology at scale. UK FinServ firms should treat this as a strategic priority alignment moment, not just a compliance note. Security teams should expect increased FCA scrutiny on fraud detection controls and technology-enabled crime prevention capabilities.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

The speech references technology-enabled financial crime broadly; organised criminal groups are increasingly deploying AI-assisted fraud tooling (deepfakes, synthetic identity generation) to execute investment fraud at scale, though specific AI techniques are not detailed in this article.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's investment fraud detection controls today and map them against the FCA's 5-year strategy priorities — ensure you can evidence proactive, technology-driven fraud prevention to regulators.
#2
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Adversarial Technique — A Reference Every AI Security Engineer Needs
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

MITRE ATLAS cataloguing LLM jailbreaks (AML.T0054) as a formal adversarial technique gives UK FinServ AI security teams a standardised framework to assess and document risks against deployed LLMs — critical as firms integrate AI into customer-facing services, fraud detection, and internal tooling. Jailbreaks enabling data leakage or malicious content generation are particularly dangerous in regulated environments where customer data and compliance outputs are at stake. This technique directly underpins threat modelling for any internal or third-party LLM deployment.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

This technique is inherently AI-focused — adversaries use adversarial prompting or weight/safety mechanism modification to override guardrails, potentially causing LLMs to leak sensitive data, reveal system configurations, or generate harmful outputs that bypass compliance controls.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map AML.T0054 into your firm's AI threat model today and verify that all deployed LLMs have documented, tested guardrails with red-team jailbreak assessments on record.
#3
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as Viable Attack Vector via Prompt Injection
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK FinServ firms adopt agentic AI systems for tasks such as automated research, customer service, and workflow orchestration, this formalised technique (AML.T0034.002) highlights that adversaries can exploit these agents to drain API budgets, degrade service availability, and create operational disruption — a form of AI-layer DoS. Financial services environments are particularly exposed where agentic systems integrate with third-party APIs or data providers with metered billing. This also chains with prompt injection risks already present in customer-input-driven AI workflows.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use LLM prompt injection or AI agent tool data poisoning to coerce agentic systems into performing excessive, expensive tool calls — such as redundant API queries or mass translation tasks — consuming resources and potentially destabilising dependent services.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement rate limits, cost budgets, and anomaly alerting on all agentic AI tool-call activity, and treat untrusted user input reaching agents as a prompt injection risk requiring sanitisation.
#4
WordPress Database Backup Plugin Exposes Full DB Credentials to Unauthenticated Attackers (CVSS 7.5)
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

CVE-2026-4031 allows unauthenticated attackers to redirect WordPress database backups to publicly accessible directories and intercept them using predictable filenames — resulting in full database credential exposure. While direct impact on core banking infrastructure is limited, UK FinServ firms running WordPress for public-facing content, investor portals, or marketing sites could expose customer data or internal credentials, triggering GDPR and FCA data breach obligations. The predictable naming convention makes exploitation reliable and scriptable at scale.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit all WordPress instances in your organisation's estate for the Database Backup plugin at version 2.5.2 or below and patch or remove immediately; also verify that wp-content/uploads/ and similar directories are not publicly browsable.
#5
FCA Confirms Industry-Wide Motor Finance Redress Scheme Amid Legal Challenges
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's reaffirmation of its motor finance redress scheme signals continued regulatory determination to enforce consumer compensation at industry scale, with direct financial and operational implications for lenders and their investors. Security and technology teams at affected firms should anticipate increased data requests, scheme portal development, and third-party integrations that expand the attack surface. The FCA's emphasis on speed and efficiency may create pressure to deploy solutions without adequate security review.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY If your firm is in scope for the motor finance scheme, ensure any new portals or data-sharing mechanisms built for redress processing undergo a full security review and penetration test before go-live. ---
14 May 2026 5 stories
#1
MITRE ATLAS Formalises LLM Jailbreak as Catalogued Adversarial Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms deploying LLMs for customer-facing or internal tooling (e.g. document summarisation, fraud triage, regulatory Q&A) face a formally catalogued threat where adversaries bypass safety guardrails to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs. The MITRE ATLAS classification (AML.T0054) means this is now a trackable, reportable technique that should appear in your AI risk register and threat models. FCA expectations around model governance and consumer protection make demonstrable controls against jailbreak attempts a regulatory as well as technical necessity.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use adversarial prompting techniques — crafted inputs designed to confuse or override alignment training — to elicit outputs the model is designed to withhold, such as confidential system prompts, customer data, or instructions for generating malicious content. Weight-modification attacks are also catalogued as a jailbreak vector for models where adversaries have supply-chain access.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map AML.T0054 into your existing threat model today — add jailbreak scenarios to your LLM red-teaming test cases and ensure all production LLM deployments log and alert on anomalous or repetitive prompt patterns indicative of jailbreak attempts.
#2
MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion as Emerging DoS Attack Vector
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms increasingly deploy agentic AI systems for automated workflows such as transaction monitoring, customer onboarding, and regulatory reporting; these systems are now a catalogued target for resource-consumption attacks that can drain API budgets, degrade service availability, and generate unexpected operational costs. Prompt injection or data poisoning directing an agent to perform thousands of redundant tool calls could constitute a denial-of-service against business-critical automation. In a heavily regulated environment, unexpected system degradation or cost spikes also carries incident-reporting and operational resilience implications under FCA/PRA rules.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit LLM prompt injection (AML.T0051) or AI agent tool data poisoning (AML.T0099) to embed directives within agent inputs that force the system to perform computationally expensive or unnecessary operations, effectively weaponising the agent's autonomy against its operator's resources.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard limits on tool-call counts, API spend thresholds, and execution timeouts for all agentic AI pipelines, and add anomaly alerting for unusual fan-out patterns or cost spikes in your AI observability stack.
#3
SQL Injection Flaw in Avada Builder WordPress Plugin Allows Unauthenticated Database Extraction
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

While this is a WordPress plugin vulnerability rather than a core financial platform flaw, UK financial services firms that operate public-facing WordPress sites (e.g. marketing, investor relations, or broker portals) running Avada Builder up to version 3.15.1 with a previously installed WooCommerce instance are exposed to unauthenticated SQL injection, potentially leaking database contents including customer or employee data. A CVSS score of 7.5 reflects high severity with no authentication barrier, making it attractive for automated exploitation at scale. Exposure of personal data via such a flaw would trigger ICO breach notification obligations and could attract FCA scrutiny if the site handles regulated communications or client data.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit all WordPress deployments across your estate for Avada Builder versions ≤3.15.1 and patch immediately; where patching is delayed, implement a WAF rule to block malicious manipulation of the 'product_order' parameter as an interim control.

MITRE's formal classification means this is no longer a theoretical concern — it has a defined technique ID (AML.T0054) that should be mapped to your threat model and AI risk register. #2

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms adopting agentic AI workflows (e.g., automated research, trade processing assistants, or customer service agents with tool-use) are exposed to a novel denial-of-service vector where adversaries coerce agents into burning API budgets and compute resources. This could translate directly into operational disruption or unexpected cloud cost spikes, both of which carry regulatory and financial consequences in a UK FinServ context. The technique chains with prompt injection (AML.T0051), which is already a high-priority threat for firms using retrieval-augmented or tool-calling LLMs.

#3
OpenClaw Arbitrary Code Execution via Malicious Repository File (CVSS 7.8)
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

This supply chain-adjacent vulnerability allows arbitrary JavaScript execution simply by placing a malicious file in a repository directory that a user runs OpenClaw commands from — a realistic attack path in developer or CI/CD environments common in FinServ engineering teams. If OpenClaw is used in any part of your software build or deployment pipeline, a compromised or malicious repository could lead to code execution under developer or service account privileges. The CVSS 7.8 score reflects the high impact to confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Check immediately whether OpenClaw is present in any developer workstation, CI/CD pipeline, or build environment; if so, upgrade to version 2026.4.23 or later and audit repository directories for unexpected `extensions/*/setup-api.js` files.
#4
Crabbox Path Traversal Flaw Enables Arbitrary File Deletion and Overwrite (CVSS 7.1)
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

The path traversal vulnerability in Crabbox allows an attacker to craft a malicious configuration file that causes the tool to execute `rm -rf` and `mkdir -p` operations outside its intended workspace — a destructive primitive that could wipe critical files or overwrite configurations in shared build or data environments. In financial services, where data integrity and availability are regulatory requirements, arbitrary file deletion in infrastructure tooling warrants prompt attention. The attack is triggered by a malicious YAML config file, making it a relevant social engineering or supply chain vector.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit all deployments of Crabbox and upgrade to version 0.9.0 or above immediately; additionally, review any externally sourced `.crabbox.yaml` configuration files for path traversal sequences before use.
#5

The FCA's public defence of its motor finance redress scheme signals continued regulatory assertiveness on consumer compensation, which is relevant context for UK FinServ compliance and operational risk teams monitoring FCA posture. Financial services firms piloting agentic AI systems — such as automated research agents, reconciliation bots, or customer interaction pipelines — are exposed to adversarially induced API budget exhaustion that could cause operational disruption and unexpected cost spikes. This technique combines prompt injection with resource abuse, meaning a single malicious input could cascade into thousands of expensive tool calls, affecting both availability and cost controls. Under FCA operational resilience rules, unplanned unavailability of AI-driven services due to resource exhaustion would require incident reporting and root cause analysis.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use prompt injection or tool data poisoning to coerce agentic AI systems into performing excessive, unnecessary API calls, wasting compute budgets and degrading service availability.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits and maximum tool-call budgets at the orchestration layer of any agentic AI system, and ensure these limits are enforced server-side and cannot be overridden by prompt-level instructions from untrusted inputs.
#3
Cisco Crosswork and NSO Hit by Unauthenticated DoS Vulnerability — Network Orchestration at Risk
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Cisco Network Services Orchestrator is widely used in large enterprise and financial services environments to manage network infrastructure at scale; a CVSS 7.5 unauthenticated DoS vulnerability in its connection-handling mechanism could allow an attacker to render network orchestration platforms unresponsive without any credentials. For UK financial institutions, disruption to network orchestration tooling could impact the availability of critical trading, payments, or branch connectivity infrastructure. This aligns directly with FCA and PRA operational resilience requirements around impact tolerances for important business services.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Identify all Cisco Crosswork Network Controller and NSO deployments in your environment immediately, apply Cisco's recommended patches or mitigations, and in the interim enforce strict network-layer access controls to limit exposure of management interfaces to trusted sources only.
#4
WordPress BetterDocs Pro SQL Injection Allows Unauthenticated Database Extraction
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

While WordPress plugins may appear peripheral to core financial services infrastructure, many UK FinServ firms run public-facing documentation, knowledge base, or support portals on WordPress; a CVSS 7.5 unauthenticated SQL injection in BetterDocs Pro (up to v3.7.0) could allow extraction of sensitive database contents including user credentials or internal documentation. Supply chain and third-party web asset risk is an area of increasing FCA scrutiny, and unpatched public-facing CMS vulnerabilities are a common initial access vector. The flaw requires the Encyclopedia feature to be enabled, which narrows but does not eliminate exposure.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit all WordPress deployments across your organisation's web estate for use of BetterDocs Pro at or below version 3.7.0, disable the Encyclopedia feature as an immediate workaround if patching cannot be done immediately, and update to a patched version as soon as one is available.
#5
FCA Pushes Forward with Industry-Wide Motor Finance Redress Scheme Amid Legal Challenges
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's commitment to an industry-wide motor finance compensation scheme signals ongoing regulatory pressure on firms to operationalise large-scale consumer redress processes, which increasingly rely on automated data matching and AI-assisted case handling systems. UK financial services firms in the motor finance space should anticipate regulatory scrutiny of the technology systems underpinning their redress operations, including data integrity and auditability of automated decisions. The FCA's emphasis on speed and fairness implies that firms using AI/ML to triage or assess claims will need to demonstrate those systems are accurate, explainable, and free from bias.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY If your firm is in scope for the motor finance redress scheme, review the auditability and accuracy of any automated or AI-assisted data processing used in customer identification and compensation calculation to ensure it will withstand FCA examination. ---

#1

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms deploying LLMs for customer-facing services, fraud detection, or internal copilots face a formally catalogued threat in which adversaries bypass safety guardrails to extract sensitive data or generate harmful outputs. MITRE ATLAS classification means this technique now has a structured taxonomy that maps to defence evasion, data leakage, and malicious code generation — all directly relevant to regulated environments where data confidentiality and operational integrity are mandated by the FCA and PRA. Security teams can now align jailbreak risks to existing threat modelling frameworks and audit LLM deployments against a recognised standard.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use adversarial prompting or weight manipulation to circumvent safety mechanisms in deployed LLMs, enabling extraction of confidential system prompts, customer data, or generation of fraudulent content that the model was explicitly designed to refuse.

KEY TAKEAWAY Map your organisation's deployed LLMs against MITRE ATLAS AML.T0054 today — run red-team jailbreak exercises against any customer-facing or internal LLM and validate that guardrail bypass attempts are logged, alerted on, and blocked at the application layer.
#2
MITRE ATLAS Catalogues Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion Attack — Prompt Injection Can Burn Your API Budget and Degrade AI Services
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms increasingly deploying agentic AI systems — automated workflows calling external APIs, data feeds, or orchestration tools — are exposed to a novel availability attack where prompt injection forces the agent into computationally expensive, budget-exhausting loops. In a regulated environment, degraded or unavailable AI services (e.g., fraud screening agents, customer onboarding workflows) could constitute an operational resilience failure under FCA/PRA PS21/3 requirements. The attack requires no authentication and can be triggered through malicious content in documents or data the agent processes.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit prompt injection or tool data poisoning to embed directives within agent-accessible content, coercing autonomous AI systems into runaway API call chains that exhaust compute budgets, degrade performance, or trigger cascading downstream service failures.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits, maximum tool-call budgets, and anomaly detection on API call volumes for all agentic AI workflows — and ensure any content ingested by agents is treated as untrusted input subject to sanitisation before being passed to the model.
#3
Critical PAN-OS Zero-Day Allows Unauthenticated RCE with Root Privileges — CISA Adds to KEV Catalogue
🔗 CISA KEV Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Palo Alto Networks firewalls are widely deployed as perimeter and segmentation controls in UK financial services environments; an unauthenticated out-of-bounds write vulnerability granting root-level code execution on PA-Series and VM-Series appliances represents a critical exposure that could allow an attacker to pivot into internal networks, intercept traffic, or disable security controls entirely. CISA's addition to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue confirms active exploitation in the wild, making this an urgent priority rather than a routine patch cycle item. Compromise of network security appliances is a known initial access vector in financially motivated and nation-state campaigns targeting the sector.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Immediately restrict User-ID Authentication Portal access to trusted zones only (or disable it entirely if unused), and fast-track vendor patch deployment — do not wait for your next scheduled maintenance window given confirmed active exploitation.
#4
Cisco Crosswork and NSO DoS Vulnerability Exposes Network Orchestration Infrastructure to Unauthenticated Attackers
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Cisco Network Services Orchestrator and Crosswork are used to automate and manage network infrastructure; a DoS vulnerability exploitable by an unauthenticated remote attacker flooding connections could render network automation platforms unresponsive, disrupting operational resilience in environments where automated network changes underpin trading, payments, or connectivity services. For UK financial firms subject to FCA operational resilience requirements, unavailability of critical network management tooling could cascade into broader service disruptions. The absence of authentication requirements to trigger the vulnerability lowers the bar significantly for opportunistic attackers.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Apply Cisco's vendor mitigations or rate-limiting configurations for CVE-2026-20188 immediately, and review whether Cisco CNC/NSO management interfaces are unnecessarily exposed to untrusted network segments — restrict access to dedicated management VLANs as a priority hardening step.
#5

The FCA's industry-wide motor finance redress scheme introduces significant operational demands on in-scope lenders, including data retrieval, customer notification, and compensation processing workflows that may stress legacy systems or create opportunities for fraud and social engineering targeting consumers expecting redress payments. Security engineers at affected firms should anticipate increased phishing campaigns impersonating the FCA or lenders, as fraudsters routinely exploit high-profile compensation schemes to target consumers. Not applicable.

#3
FCA Censures Sapia Over £19m WealthTek Client Money Protection Failure
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's censure of Sapia for failing to segregate duties over client money accounts is a direct reminder that appointed representative oversight and access controls around financial accounts are active regulatory scrutiny areas. For security engineers, the finding that the same individuals could both make payments from and conduct checks on client money accounts illustrates a segregation-of-duties control failure with direct parallels in system access design. UK FinServ firms should review whether technical access controls mirror the FCA's required role separation, particularly for privileged financial system accounts.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit privileged access controls on client money or custody systems today to confirm that payment initiation and reconciliation/oversight functions are enforced as technically separate roles — do not rely solely on policy or manual process.

FCA Pushes Forward Industry-Wide Motor Finance Redress Scheme Amid Legal Challenges
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's determination to proceed with a mandatory industry-wide motor finance compensation scheme signals continued regulatory pressure on financial firms to operationalise large-scale consumer redress at pace. For security and engineering teams, schemes of this scale require secure data processing pipelines, identity verification, and protection of large volumes of consumer PII — all of which become attractive targets for fraud and data theft. Firms involved in the scheme should ensure their remediation platforms are security-assessed before handling high volumes of sensitive claims data.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

#5
CVSS 8.1 Heap Buffer Overflow in BusyBox DHCPv6 Client Threatens Embedded Network Devices
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

BusyBox is widely embedded in network appliances, IoT devices, and operational infrastructure — including devices that may exist within financial services data centres or branch network equipment. A network-adjacent attacker able to send a crafted DHCPv6 response could achieve arbitrary code execution or denial of service on unpatched embedded systems, potentially pivoting into internal network segments. Without heap hardening mitigations, exploitation is more straightforward on affected devices.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run an asset inventory query today to identify BusyBox-based devices in your environment, confirm whether DHCPv6 client functionality is enabled, and prioritise patching to any commit after 42202bf or apply network-level controls to restrict untrusted DHCPv6 responses reaching affected devices. ---

MITRE ATLAS Flags Agentic AI Systems as DoS/Budget-Drain Attack Surface
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms piloting agentic AI systems — such as automated research agents, trade data processors, or customer service bots with tool-use capabilities — are directly exposed to this resource exhaustion technique. #3
FCA Censures Sapia Over £19m Client Money Protection Failure at WealthTek
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

This enforcement action is a direct reminder that appointed representative (AR) relationships carry significant regulatory liability for principal firms — a common structure in UK FinServ. The FCA specifically identified failure to segregate duties around client money accounts as the root cause, a control deficiency directly relevant to any firm acting as a principal. The £19.6m voluntary payment and public censure signal the FCA's continued focus on client asset (CASS) rule compliance and oversight of AR networks.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's appointed representative oversight programme today — specifically verify that segregation of duties controls around client money accounts meet CASS requirements and that no single individual can both execute and self-audit payments.

Not applicable.

Financial services firms piloting agentic AI systems — such as autonomous trading assistants, compliance bots, or customer service agents with tool-calling capabilities — are exposed to a novel denial-of-service vector that can exhaust API budgets and degrade service availability. This matters for operational resilience obligations under FCA PS21/3 and the wider DORA requirements applicable to UK-aligned firms, where AI-driven service degradation must be anticipated and mitigated. #3
FCA Censures Sapia and Orders £19.6m Repayment for Client Money Control Failures
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's action against Sapia is a direct reminder that appointed representative (AR) relationships carry full regulatory accountability for client money protection, and that inadequate segregation of duties in financial controls remains an enforcement priority. For UK FinServ security and compliance teams, this reinforces the need to ensure that access controls and system-level segregation of duties mirror the organisational controls required under CASS rules — a gap here is both a security and a regulatory risk. The scale of the financial penalty signals the FCA will hold principal firms liable for control failures in their AR networks.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's segregation of duties controls in systems that handle client money accounts, specifically ensuring that no user role or service account can both initiate payments and perform reconciliation or oversight checks on the same accounts.
#4
SQL Injection Flaw in SSCMS v7.4.0 Allows Full Database Compromise via Encrypted Payload Bypass
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

CVE-2026-7435 (CVSS 7.2) affects SSCMS v7.4.0, where unsanitised input to the `/api/stl/actions/dynamic` endpoint enables attackers to execute arbitrary SQL, including authentication bypass and full database compromise — a critical risk if this CMS is used in any customer-facing or internal financial portal. The use of encrypted payloads to deliver the injection means standard web application firewall rules relying on plaintext pattern matching may not detect exploitation attempts. UK financial firms must treat any internet-exposed CMS as a high-priority patching target given PCI DSS and FCA data protection obligations.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Check immediately whether SSCMS v7.4.0 is present anywhere in your web estate or supply chain; if so, apply available patches or restrict access to the `/api/stl/actions/dynamic` endpoint at the WAF/network layer while a fix is deployed.
#5
FCA and PRA Reform SM&CR to Reduce Compliance Burden and Increase Flexibility for Firms
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA and PRA's first-phase SM&CR reforms reduce administrative overhead around senior manager applications and certification role duplication, giving UK financial firms greater flexibility in how they structure accountability — relevant to security engineers whose roles may fall under the Certification Regime. While the core principle of senior leader accountability is preserved, changes to certification scope may affect how accountability for AI/ML systems and cybersecurity decisions is formally documented and assigned. Security teams should ensure that any reduction in certification roles does not inadvertently obscure accountability lines for critical technology and AI governance decisions.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review your firm's SM&CR accountability maps in light of the new reforms and confirm that responsibility for AI system oversight, cybersecurity decisions, and technology risk remains explicitly assigned to a named Senior Manager with no accountability gaps created by the certification role reductions. ---

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors leverage prompt injection or tool data poisoning to direct agentic AI systems into performing excessive, unnecessary computations — effectively weaponising the agent's autonomy against the organisation's own infrastructure and API budgets.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement rate limiting, cost caps, and anomaly detection on all tool calls made by agentic AI systems, and ensure prompt injection mitigations (input validation, sandboxed execution) are in place before any agent is exposed to untrusted input.
#4
Sapia Fined £19m Over WealthTek Client Money Failures — Segregation of Duties at the Core
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's censure of Sapia is a direct reminder that the principle of segregation of duties in client money handling remains a live enforcement priority — firms where the same individuals can both initiate and authorise payments are exposed to significant regulatory and financial risk. For security engineers, this case underscores the importance of enforcing access controls and privilege separation in systems handling client funds, not just as a technical control but as a regulatory requirement. Appointed representative relationships create inherited risk that your firm's security and compliance posture must account for.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — this case involves governance and control failures rather than a cyber or AI threat actor.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review access control configurations in your client money systems to confirm that payment initiation and reconciliation/approval functions are held by distinct roles with no shared accounts — document this separation as evidence for FCA oversight purposes.
#5
CVSS 8.2 Authentication Bypass in OpenClaw Grants Unauthenticated Operator-Level Write Access
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

An authentication bypass scoring 8.2 on CVSS that grants unauthenticated access to privileged runtime write operations is a high-severity risk for any environment running OpenClaw before version 2026.3.31. If this software is present in your technology estate — including via third-party or vendor-managed components — an attacker could perform operator-level actions without any credentials, potentially manipulating runtime configurations or processes. UK FinServ firms should treat unauthenticated privilege escalation paths as critical given regulatory expectations around access control under DORA and FCA operational resilience rules.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable based on the information available, though unauthenticated runtime write access could be exploited to tamper with AI/ML inference infrastructure if OpenClaw is used in that context.

KEY TAKEAWAY Check immediately whether OpenClaw is present anywhere in your environment or vendor supply chain, prioritise patching to version 2026.3.31 or later, and block the affected HTTP routes at the network perimeter as a temporary mitigation if patching cannot be done immediately. ---

#2
MITRE ATLAS Flags Supply Chain Attacks on AI Frameworks Like PyTorch, LangChain as Key Adversary Technique
🔗 MITRE ATLAS Supply Chain
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms are rapidly adopting AI development stacks built on PyTorch, TensorFlow, and LangChain — exactly the targets identified in this MITRE ATLAS technique. A compromised deep learning framework or generative AI integration library in your ML pipeline could silently corrupt models used for fraud detection, credit scoring, or customer-facing applications. The specific callout of Jupyter notebooks and configuration files as attack surfaces is particularly relevant given their common use in data science and MLOps environments within FinServ.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors target dependency chains of AI software packages to compromise legitimate packages or inject malicious code, effectively poisoning the AI development lifecycle before models ever reach production.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit all third-party AI/ML package dependencies in your development and MLOps pipelines today — pin versions, verify checksums, and ensure Jupyter notebooks are not accessible from untrusted networks or stored with embedded credentials.
#3
MITRE ATLAS Documents Agentic AI Resource Exhaustion via Prompt Injection as Emerging DoS Vector
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

As UK financial firms deploy agentic AI systems for tasks such as customer service automation, compliance document processing, or market data analysis, this technique demonstrates that prompt injection can be weaponised not just for data theft but for costly API budget exhaustion and operational disruption. In a FinServ context, runaway API costs and degraded agentic service availability could have direct business and reputational impact. This technique is particularly relevant given the FCA's current focus on AI operational resilience.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries exploit LLM prompt injection or tool data poisoning to coerce agentic AI systems into performing thousands of unnecessary tool calls or API queries, deliberately wasting compute resources and financial API budgets.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement hard rate limits, API budget caps, and anomaly detection on tool-call frequency for any agentic AI systems in your environment — and ensure prompt injection mitigations are in place before production deployment.
#4
Sapia Censured and Ordered to Pay £19.6m After WealthTek Client Money Failures Expose Segregation Control Gaps
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

This enforcement action is a direct reminder that the FCA will hold principal firms accountable for the control failures of their appointed representatives — a critical consideration for any FinServ firm operating through third-party or AR relationships. The specific failure here was a lack of segregation of duties over client money accounts, a control weakness that security engineers should recognise as analogous to access control failures in technical systems. Firms should treat this as a prompt to review both operational and technical controls around privileged access to financial accounts and data.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Cross-reference your firm's client money controls and segregation of duties policies with the FCA's CASS rules today, and verify that no single individual or automated process has both payment execution and reconciliation/checking permissions over client money accounts.
#5
CVSS 8.8 ZipSlip Vulnerability in ProjeQtor Enables Authenticated RCE via Malicious ZIP Upload
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

ProjeQtor is an open-source project management tool that may be present in FinServ environments as a shadow IT or departmental tool — a CVSS 8.8 authenticated RCE vulnerability via ZipSlip path traversal is a high-severity risk if the platform is internet-accessible or reachable by a compromised insider account. The ability to write a PHP webshell to a web-accessible directory with web server privileges provides a persistent foothold that could facilitate data exfiltration or lateral movement within a financial network. Note that the CVE identifier references 2026, which should be verified for accuracy before actioning.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable — however, automated vulnerability scanners and exploit frameworks increasingly use AI-assisted techniques to identify and prioritise ZipSlip-class vulnerabilities at scale.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run an immediate discovery exercise to identify any ProjeQtor instances (versions 7.0–12.4.3) in your environment, restrict upload permissions and internet exposure as an interim control, and apply vendor patches or mitigations as soon as they are available. ---

#2
MITRE ATLAS Flags AI Supply Chain as Prime Attack Surface — Frameworks Like LangChain and PyTorch in the Crosshairs
🔗 MITRE ATLAS Supply Chain
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms are rapidly integrating AI frameworks such as LangChain, TensorFlow, and PyTorch into production systems, making this MITRE ATLAS technique (AML.T0010.001) directly relevant to their attack surface. Adversaries can compromise legitimate packages or publish malicious ones targeting AI DevOps pipelines — including Jupyter notebooks widely used by data science teams in FinServ — potentially leading to model poisoning, data exfiltration, or code execution in sensitive environments. Dependency chain attacks are particularly dangerous because they can bypass traditional application security controls that don't inspect ML-specific tooling.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors exploit the AI software supply chain by compromising or spoofing legitimate packages (e.g. on PyPI) and embedding malicious code that executes within AI training or inference pipelines, potentially corrupting models or stealing sensitive financial data at scale.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit your firm's AI/ML dependency inventory today — enforce pinned, verified package versions, enable software composition analysis (SCA) scanning for AI frameworks, and restrict Jupyter notebook execution environments from accessing production data or credentials.
#3
FCA Open Finance Vision Expands Data Sharing Scope — New Attack Surface for Fraud and Data Theft Emerges
🔗 FCA News Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

The FCA's open finance vision would significantly broaden the perimeter of financial data sharing beyond Open Banking, encompassing mortgages, investments, pensions, and savings — dramatically increasing the volume of sensitive consumer and SME data flowing between third-party providers. For UK FinServ security engineers, this means a larger API attack surface, more third-party integrations requiring rigorous vetting, and heightened risk of data exfiltration or fraudulent account access if consent mechanisms are poorly implemented. The promise of "stronger fraud protection" through richer data also creates a concentration risk if aggregated financial profiles are compromised.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

AI-enabled fraud actors could exploit open finance APIs to harvest aggregated financial profiles at scale, using ML models to identify high-value targets or craft highly personalised social engineering attacks against consumers and SMEs.

KEY TAKEAWAY Begin mapping which consumer and SME data your firm would expose under an open finance model, and assess whether your current API security controls, third-party risk management processes, and consent mechanisms are adequate for the expanded scope being proposed.
#4
MITRE ATLAS Warns Agentic AI Systems Vulnerable to Resource Exhaustion via Prompt Injection
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms are beginning to deploy agentic AI systems for tasks such as customer service automation, trade research, and compliance monitoring — making this MITRE ATLAS technique (AML.T0034.002) an emerging and under-appreciated operational risk. An adversary who can inject malicious prompts into an agentic workflow could trigger thousands of unnecessary API calls or tool executions, burning through API budgets, degrading service availability, and potentially incurring significant unexpected cloud costs. In regulated environments, disruption to AI-assisted compliance or customer-facing processes could also carry regulatory consequences under operational resilience requirements.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors use LLM prompt injection or tool data poisoning to coerce agentic AI systems into executing excessive, costly, or misdirected tool calls — effectively weaponising the agent's autonomy for denial-of-service or budget exhaustion attacks.

KEY TAKEAWAY If your firm runs any agentic AI systems, implement hard limits on tool call counts, API spend thresholds, and automated circuit-breakers per session today, and ensure prompt injection defences are in place at all agent input boundaries.
#5
CVSS 8.4 Buffer Overflow in iSmartViewPro — Low Relevance Legacy Flaw Warrants Quick Triage
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

CVE-2018-25283 is a high-severity (CVSS 8.4) SEH buffer overflow in iSmartViewPro 1.5, a video surveillance client application — its relevance to UK FinServ is limited but non-zero, as physical security systems in bank branches or data centres may use similar legacy CCTV software. The local attack vector reduces urgency compared to remote vulnerabilities, but it could be chained with other techniques by an insider threat or an attacker who has already gained physical or local access. The age of this CVE (2018) means any affected system still running this software version represents a significant patch management failure.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Run a quick search across your asset inventory and any physical security system registers for iSmartViewPro 1.5 deployments — if found, escalate to your facilities and physical security teams for immediate patching or decommissioning. ---

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

#3
MITRE ATLAS Documents Prompt Injection Attacks That Drain Agentic AI API Budgets
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms deploying AI agents for tasks such as customer service, fraud analysis, or automated reporting are exposed to resource-consumption attacks that could generate significant unexpected cloud API costs and degrade service availability — both operational and financial risks in a regulated context. The technique exploits the autonomous tool-calling behaviour of agentic systems, meaning standard perimeter controls offer little protection; the attack surface lives inside the model's instruction-following logic. In a worst case, excessive fan-out queries to external APIs could also trigger data leakage or violate data residency obligations.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries use prompt injection or tool data poisoning to embed directives within agentic AI systems, coercing them into performing thousands of unnecessary, costly, or data-exposing tool calls without direct system access.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement rate limits and cost-ceiling guardrails on all agentic AI tool calls today, and add anomaly alerting for API call volume spikes originating from AI agent workflows in your cloud environment.
#4
MITRE ATLAS Catalogues Adversarial Use of LLMs for Malware, Deepfakes and Phishing at Scale
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial services firms face a materially elevated phishing and social engineering threat as adversaries leverage open-source LLMs hosted locally via Ollama or vLLM to generate high-volume, high-quality lure content without leaving a trail through commercial AI providers. Deepfake generation capability — explicitly referenced in this technique — poses a direct risk to voice and video-based authentication controls used in UK retail banking and wealth management. The lowered barrier to generating functional malware also increases the likelihood of targeted intrusion attempts against financial sector infrastructure.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors obtain and locally self-host open-source LLMs to generate phishing content, deepfakes, malicious commands, and malware, deliberately avoiding commercial API providers that log usage or enforce content policies.

KEY TAKEAWAY Review the effectiveness of your email security and voice authentication controls against AI-generated content specifically — engage your vendor today to confirm whether deepfake audio detection is included in your current contract scope.

The emphasis on SME financial data sharing creates additional exposure, as SMEs typically have weaker security postures and could become entry points into financial data ecosystems. Security engineers should anticipate that expanded open finance APIs will attract increased adversarial probing, particularly through authorised but malicious third-party applications.

Threat actors may use AI-generated phishing content or deepfake voice/SMS campaigns to impersonate FCA or lender redress communications, targeting consumers who are expecting legitimate contact about compensation claims.

16 April 2026 5 stories
#1
Redsys WooCommerce Plugin Flaw Lets Attackers Forge Payment Confirmations Without Paying
🔗 NVD / NIST Financial Services
WHY IT MATTERS

This vulnerability directly targets payment processing flows — a core concern for any financial services firm or merchant using WordPress-based payment infrastructure. An unauthenticated attacker who knows a valid order key and amount can spoof payment callbacks across Redsys, Bizum, and Google Pay flows, triggering order fulfilment without actual payment. UK firms with e-commerce or client portal components built on WordPress should treat this as an urgent supply chain and fraud risk.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Immediately audit any WordPress deployments using the Redsys & WooCommerce Lite plugin and patch to version 7.0.1 or later; if patching is not immediately possible, temporarily disable the plugin and validate all recent payment callbacks for signs of forged confirmations.
#2
Splunk MCP Server Exposes Session and Auth Tokens in Cleartext — AI Integration at Risk
🔗 NVD / NIST AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

The Splunk MCP (Model Context Protocol) Server app is a direct AI/ML integration layer, and this vulnerability exposes session and authorisation tokens in plaintext logs — a critical risk in environments where Splunk is used for security monitoring and AI-assisted threat detection. For UK financial services firms adopting AI-augmented SIEM workflows, compromised tokens could allow lateral movement into security tooling or exfiltration of sensitive log data. The FCA's expectations around operational resilience and data integrity make this particularly relevant.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Threat actors with even limited internal access could harvest AI tool session tokens from logs to hijack MCP-connected AI workflows, potentially manipulating or poisoning the outputs of AI-assisted security monitoring pipelines.

KEY TAKEAWAY Upgrade Splunk MCP Server app to version 1.0.3 or above immediately, and audit current role assignments to ensure only administrator-level roles have access to the `_internal` index — do this before any broader AI/ML tooling expansion in your Splunk environment.
#3
Splunk RCE Flaw Lets Low-Privileged Users Execute Code via Malicious File Upload
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Splunk is widely deployed in UK financial services SOC environments for SIEM and log analytics; a low-privileged user being able to achieve Remote Code Execution by exploiting weak temporary file isolation is a serious privilege escalation risk. Insider threat scenarios — a particular concern under FCA operational resilience guidance — are directly in scope here. Compromise of a Splunk instance could expose the entirety of ingested security telemetry.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Patch Splunk Enterprise to versions 10.2.1, 10.0.5, 9.4.10, or 9.3.11 (as applicable) and Splunk Cloud Platform to the respective minimum fixed versions today; also review `apptemp` directory permissions and audit which low-privileged accounts have file upload capability in your environment.
#4
Lenovo Software Fix Vulnerability Enables Local Privilege Escalation to Arbitrary Code Execution
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

Lenovo endpoints are common across enterprise environments including UK financial services firms, and a local privilege escalation vulnerability in a Lenovo software utility raises insider threat and post-compromise risk concerns. Attackers who have already established a foothold on an endpoint — via phishing or supply chain compromise — could leverage this to gain elevated execution rights and move laterally. This is particularly relevant given FCA expectations around endpoint security controls.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Identify all Lenovo devices running the vulnerable version of Lenovo Software Fix across your estate using your asset management or EDR tooling, and apply vendor patches as soon as they are available — in the interim, enforce least-privilege policies to reduce exposure.
#5
Adobe Photoshop Installer Path Hijacking Flaw Allows Arbitrary Code Execution
🔗 NVD / NIST Vulnerability
WHY IT MATTERS

While Adobe Photoshop is not a core financial services application, it is commonly found on creative, marketing, and communications workstations within broader enterprise environments at UK firms. An uncontrolled search path vulnerability exploitable by a low-privileged local user during installation represents a realistic attack vector in a spear-phishing scenario that tricks a user into re-running an installer. User interaction is required, reducing but not eliminating the risk.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Not applicable.

KEY TAKEAWAY Ensure Adobe Photoshop is patched to the latest version across all endpoints in your estate, and consider restricting installer execution to privileged admin accounts via application control policies to remove the user-interaction attack surface entirely. ---

#1
MITRE ATLAS Framework Details How Threat Actors Weaponize Generative AI for Cyber Attacks
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services firms increasingly rely on AI/ML systems for fraud detection, customer service, and trading algorithms, making understanding adversarial AI use critical. This MITRE technique catalogues how attackers obtain and weaponize generative AI tools to create sophisticated phishing campaigns, malware, and deepfakes that could bypass traditional security controls.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries actively search for and obtain generative AI models like LLMs to generate malware, create deepfakes, craft malicious commands, and produce convincing phishing content using frameworks like Ollama or cloud infrastructure.

#2
New AI Agent Attack Vector Targets API Budgets Through Resource Consumption
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial institutions deploying AI agents for customer service or automated trading face new DoS-style attacks targeting operational budgets rather than infrastructure. Attackers can force agents into expensive API calls through prompt injection, potentially disrupting services while inflating cloud costs.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries exploit LLM prompt injection or data poisoning to coerce AI agents into performing unnecessary computationally expensive operations like excessive translations or repeated API queries.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement rate limiting and cost monitoring controls on AI agent API calls, especially for customer-facing agentic systems.
#3
Supply Chain Attacks Now Target AI Development Frameworks and Dependencies

WHY IT MATTERS

Financial institutions developing in-house AI models face supply chain risks through compromised AI frameworks like PyTorch, TensorFlow, and LangChain. Given the regulatory requirements and sensitive data handling in UK FinServ, compromised AI development tools could lead to model backdoors or data exfiltration.

KEY TAKEAWAY Audit and implement security scanning for all AI development dependencies, including deep learning frameworks and generative AI integration tools used in your ML pipeline.
#4
Cost Harvesting Attacks Target AI Service Budgets Through Resource Manipulation
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial services organizations relying on cloud-based AI services face a new attack vector where adversaries deliberately inflate service costs through high-volume queries or resource-intensive prompts. This could impact budget planning and service availability for critical AI-driven operations.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries craft adversarial prompts and queries designed to maximize computational costs rather than steal resources for their own use.

KEY TAKEAWAY Establish anomaly detection for unusual AI service usage patterns and implement budget alerts with automatic throttling mechanisms.
#5
Prompt Infiltration Technique Allows Persistent AI System Compromise
🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

Financial institutions using RAG systems for document analysis or AI agents processing public data face persistent compromise risks. Malicious prompts embedded in public-facing applications could remain dormant until processed by AI systems, potentially affecting multiple users and tools.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries inject malicious prompts into public-facing applications that get indexed by RAG systems or trigger AI agent actions, creating persistent threats within the organization's AI infrastructure.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement prompt sanitization and content filtering for all data sources feeding into RAG systems and AI agents, especially public-facing inputs. ---

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Adversaries craft either high-volume simple queries or complex resource-intensive prompts to deliberately drive up the computational costs of victim AI systems.

🔗 MITRE ATLAS AI/ML Security
WHY IT MATTERS

UK financial firms using RAG systems for regulatory document analysis or customer query processing could unknowingly index malicious prompts that later compromise AI responses. This creates a persistent backdoor affecting multiple users and AI tools across the organisation.

🤖 AI / ML THREAT ACTOR USE

Attackers inject malicious prompts into public-facing applications that get indexed by RAG systems, creating time-delayed attacks when AI models later process this poisoned content.

KEY TAKEAWAY Implement content sanitisation for all text inputs before indexing in RAG systems and regularly audit indexed content for suspicious prompt patterns. ---
