Describe the reconnaissance-to-exfiltration sequence you can see. - One entry (`192.168.45.201 GET /%2E%2E%2F%2E%2E%2Fproc/self/environ 200`) appears to have succeeded despite using the fully-encoded slash `%2F`. What does this suggest about the server's behaviour?
                5. Now ask Claude.ai: > "In a UK financial services environment, if an attacker successfully retrieved /root/.ssh/id_rsa and /etc/ssl/private/server.key from an internal AI code review platform, what would be the immediate and downstream security risks? What regulatory obligations under FCA rules or UK GDPR might be triggered?"
                6. Add a summary of Claude's answer to your `ioc_analysis.txt` file, noting which risks you think are most relevant to your bank's context. --- **Part 3 — Write a Detection Rule (20 minutes)**
                7. In your text editor, create a file called `detection_rule.txt`. You will write a pseudo-SIEM detection rule (Sigma-style, plain text — no special tools needed) to catch this attack pattern. Use the following template: ``` Rule Name: Path Traversal via Percent-Encoded Segments Description: Detects HTTP GET requests containing percent-encoded dot-dot-slash sequences indicative of path traversal attempts against web applications Log Source: Web application access logs / WAF logs Condition: http.method = GET AND http.uri CONTAINS ANY OF: [ list the specific encoded patterns you would detect — fill this in ] AND (http.status = 200 OR http.status = 403 OR http.status = 500) Severity: HIGH Response: [fill in what the SOC should do immediately] False Positive Notes: [fill in what legitimate traffic might look like and how to exclude it] ```
                8. Fill in the blank sections. For the
                DEFENCE TASK

                ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                NOT STARTED
                28 May 2026
                Audit and Harden AI Agent Identity Permissions in a Simulated FinServ Environment
                Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                ▼
                📄 AI Agents Are Shifting Identity Security Budget Dynamics
                SCENARIO

                Barclayford Bank has recently deployed three AI agents to automate back-office processes: one for fraud alert triage, one for regulatory report generation, and one for customer data lookup. During a routine access review, your team discovers that all three agents were provisioned with broad service account permissions by the development team "to make integration easier." The CISO has flagged this as a critical governance gap ahead of an FCA operational resilience audit next month. You have been asked to assess the identity risk posture of these AI agents and document a hardening plan before the audit window opens.

                OBJECTIVE

                Build a realistic AI agent identity inventory, identify over-privileged access patterns using least-privilege analysis, and produce a prioritised remediation register aligned to UK financial services governance expectations.

                CHALLENGE
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  Design a one-page AI Agent Identity Governance Policy for Barclayford Bank. The policy must include: (1) a mandatory provisioning checklist that enforces least-privilege at the point of creation, (2) a maximum credential lifetime and rotation schedule for AI agent API keys and service accounts, (3) a definition of when human-in-the-loop oversight is mandatory versus optional based on data sensitivity tier, (4) a quarterly access review process specifying who owns the review (CISO, IAM team, or business line), and (5) how the policy maps to FCA operational resilience requirements. Save this as `ai_agent_identity_policy.md`. This document should be written as if it will be submitted to your CISO for sign-off before the FCA audit. ---

                  REFLECTION QUESTIONS
                  1. Q1: AI agent identities are often provisioned by development or data science teams rather than the IAM team. What specific organisational control failures does this represent, and what process change would you recommend to ensure AI agent identities go through the same governance lifecycle as human and traditional service account identities at a UK bank?
                  2. Q2: One of the attack paths your threat analysis likely surfaced is prompt injection — where a malicious actor manipulates the input to an AI agent to make it misuse its own legitimate permissions. How does this differ from a traditional credential theft attack, and why does it make least-privilege even more critical for AI agents than for conventional service accounts?
                  3. Q3: The FCA's operational resilience framework focuses on important business services and impact tolerances. If an AI fraud triage agent were compromised and began suppressing legitimate fraud alerts, which operational resilience obligations would this breach, and what evidence would you need to provide to the FCA to demonstrate you detected and contained the incident within your stated impact tolerance? ---
                  FURTHER READING

                  NCSC — Secure Design Principles for AI Systems (part of the Guidelines for Secure AI System Development, co-authored with CISA and international partners): https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/collection/guidelines-secure-ai-system-development

                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  27 May 2026
                  Mapping AI-Amplified Attack Surfaces: Assessing Your Firm's Exposure to Frontier AI Cyber Threats
                  Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 FCA, Bank of England and Treasury joint statement on frontier AI models and cyber resilience
                  SCENARIO

                  The FCA, Bank of England, and HM Treasury have jointly issued a statement warning that frontier AI models now exceed skilled human practitioners in cyber capability — at greater speed, scale, and lower cost. Your CISO at Meridian Bank PLC has received the statement and wants a rapid assessment before next week's board risk committee meeting. A threat intelligence analyst flagged that a competitor institution suffered a credential-stuffing campaign last month that showed signs of AI-assisted adaptation — the attack modified its patterns in near-real-time to evade rate-limiting controls. Your CISO needs to know: which of Meridian's existing controls could be outpaced or overwhelmed by AI-amplified attacks, and where are the critical gaps?

                  OBJECTIVE

                  Produce a structured AI threat amplification assessment for a fictional UK retail bank, identifying which existing cyber controls are most vulnerable to AI-accelerated attack techniques and ranking them by residual risk. ---

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Open a text editor (Notepad, VS Code, or similar — nothing fancy needed).
                  2. Create a file called `meridian_control_inventory.txt`.
                  3. Write out the following 10 controls exactly as listed — these represent a realistic but deliberately imperfect UK retail bank control set: ``` CONTROL-01: Password policy — 12 char minimum, no MFA on legacy internal systems CONTROL-02: Email gateway with signature-based phishing detection CONTROL-03: WAF with static ruleset, last reviewed 14 months ago CONTROL-04: SIEM with rule-based alerting, 48-hour analyst review cycle CONTROL-05: Vulnerability scanning — monthly cadence, internet-facing only CONTROL-06: Privileged Access Management (PAM) — covers 80% of admin accounts CONTROL-07: Customer-facing login — rate limiting at 10 attempts per minute per IP CONTROL-08: Fraud detection ML model — trained on data from 18 months ago CONTROL-09: Incident response playbooks — last tested in tabletop exercise 22 months ago CONTROL-10: Threat intelligence feed — updated weekly, TI team of 2 analysts ```
                  4. Save the file. This is your baseline. --- **Part 2 — AI Threat Amplification Analysis Using Claude (30 minutes)**
                  5. Go to **claude.ai** (free tier is sufficient).
                  6. Paste in the following prompt exactly, then review the output critically: ``` I am a security engineer at a UK retail bank. I have the following 10 security controls. For each control, explain specifically how a threat actor using a frontier AI model (as described in the FCA/Bank of England/Treasury joint statement on frontier AI and cyber resilience) could amplify, accelerate, or circumvent that control compared to a non-AI-assisted attacker. For each control give: (a) the AI-amplified attack technique, (b) why the existing control is insufficient against it, and (c) a severity rating of HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW. [paste your 10 controls here] ```
                  7. Read the full response carefully. In your text editor, open a new file called `ai_threat_analysis.txt`.
                  8. For each of the 10 controls, record Claude's severity rating and a one-sentence summary of the AI-amplified threat in your own words. Do not copy-paste — paraphrase to confirm you understood it. --- **Part 3 — Challenge the Output (10 minutes)**
                  9. Pick the **two controls Claude rated as HIGH severity**.
                  10. Go back to Claude and paste this follow-up prompt for each one: ``` For CONTROL-[X], you rated the AI-amplified risk as HIGH. What specific indicators of compromise or detection signals should a SOC analyst at a UK bank look for that would suggest this control is being attacked by an AI-assisted threat actor rather than a conventional attacker? Give 3 specific, observable IOCs or behavioural anomalies. ```
                  11. Add these IOCs to your `ai_threat_analysis.txt` file under the relevant control. --- **Part 4 — Residual Risk Ranking (10 minutes)**
                  12. In your text editor, create a third file: `residual_risk_ranking.txt`.
                  13. Rank all 10 controls from **most exposed** to **least exposed** to AI-amplified threats, based on your analysis.
                  14. For your top 3 most exposed controls, write one sentence explaining what makes them structurally vulnerable (not just "AI is faster" — be specific about *why* the control design fails against AI-scale attacks).
                  15. Add a final line: **"Critical Finding for CISO:"** — write 2–3 sentences summarising what Meridian should tell the board risk committee about frontier AI threat exposure, in plain English suitable for a non-technical board member. ---
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  Select the single highest-residual-risk control from your ranking. Design a **layered mitigation plan** for that control by documenting the following in a new file called `mitigation_plan.txt`: - **Immediate action (0–30 days):** One concrete change that can be made quickly with no budget increase — e.g. configuration change, policy update, or process improvement. - **Short-term action (30–90 days):** One control enhancement that would require some resource or tooling — describe what it would do and why it addresses the AI-amplification vector specifically. - **Detection uplift:** Define one new SIEM alert rule or monitoring query *in plain English* (not code) that would help a SOC analyst detect AI-assisted exploitation of this specific control. Be precise about what behaviour it looks for and what threshold would trigger it. - **Residual risk statement:** After your mitigations, what risk remains, and why? Acknowledge any limitations honestly. Your mitigation plan should be written as if it will be handed to your CISO tomorrow morning. ---

                  REFLECTION QUESTIONS
                  1. Q1: The FCA/BoE/Treasury statement says frontier AI models are "already exceeding what a skilled practitioner could achieve." Looking at your residual risk ranking, which of Meridian's controls would still be adequate against a skilled human attacker but become inadequate specifically because of AI amplification — and
                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  26 May 2026
                  Mapping Regulatory Leadership Changes to Threat Intelligence: OSINT Profiling of FCA Executive Appointments
                  Beginner ⏱ 60 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 FCA announces new appointments to executive team
                  SCENARIO

                  Your threat intelligence team at a mid-sized UK retail bank has received an internal alert flagging that the FCA has announced new senior executive appointments. Your Head of Compliance has flagged this as relevant to your security posture — not just for regulatory reasons, but because social engineering actors, including business email compromise (BEC) gangs and impersonation fraudsters, are known to rapidly exploit publicly announced leadership changes at regulators and financial institutions. A threat actor impersonating a new FCA executive director could plausibly target your firm's treasury or wholesale markets desk within days of such an announcement, referencing real names, real roles, and real policy priorities to add credibility to fraudulent communications.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  The engineer will conduct a structured OSINT exercise to build a realistic impersonation threat profile based on a public regulatory leadership announcement, then assess how a social engineer could weaponise this information against a UK financial institution.

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Open your text editor and create a new file called `fca_exec_threat_profile.md`.
                  2. Navigate to https://www.fca.org.uk/news/press-releases and locate the press release about the new executive appointments.
                  3. Record in your file: the full names announced, their new titles, their stated remit, any career history mentioned, and the date of announcement.
                  4. Now search LinkedIn (no account needed for basic results) and Google for each name. Search exactly: `"Simon Walls" FCA` and note any additional publicly available career details, conference appearances, or published speeches. Record everything you find with source URLs.
                  5. Navigate to https://register.fca.org.uk/s/ and search for each executive by name. Note their registered controlled functions, approval dates, and any regulatory history visible in the public register. **Step 2 — Build an impersonation threat profile (15 minutes)**
                  6. In your `fca_exec_threat_profile.md` file, create a section called `## Impersonation Risk Profile`.
                  7. Write a realistic one-paragraph phishing or vishing pretext that a threat actor could use, incorporating only publicly available information you collected above. The scenario should target a wholesale markets or treasury employee at a UK bank — for example, a fabricated urgent request referencing the executive's real policy area (market resilience, wholesale regulation) to pressure the target into sharing data or bypassing a control. **Write this as a threat simulation artefact, not as a real communication.**
                  8. Identify and list at least five specific data points from your OSINT that add credibility to the impersonation (e.g. correct job title, correct policy remit, correct appointment date, correct institutional email domain format, career continuity details). **Step 3 — Analyse the attack surface (15 minutes)**
                  9. Open Claude.ai (free tier is sufficient).
                  10. Paste in the following prompt exactly: > "I am a security analyst at a UK bank. A regulatory executive has just been publicly announced in a new senior role at the FCA. Based on the following publicly available details [paste your collected OSINT summary here], what social engineering attack vectors should I be most concerned about in the first 30 days after this announcement? Please focus on BEC, vishing, and regulatory impersonation threats relevant to a wholesale markets or treasury function."
                  11. Review Claude's response. In your notes file, record: which attack vectors it identifies, which ones you had already thought of, and which were new to you.
                  12. Ask Claude a follow-up: `"What email header or communication indicators would help a recipient verify that a message genuinely came from the FCA rather than an impersonator?"` Record the response. **Step 4 — Check existing FCA guidance (15 minutes)**
                  13. Navigate to https://www.fca.org.uk/consumers/clones-and-impostors and read the FCA's own guidance on impersonation fraud.
                  14. Cross-reference this with your threat profile. Note in your file: does the FCA's public guidance cover the specific attack vector you modelled? What gaps exist?
                  15. Finally, search for the FCA's official contact verification page at https://www.fca.org.uk/contact and document the official channels a bank employee should use to verify the identity of someone claiming to be an FCA official. ---
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  Using your completed threat profile as a reference, write a one-page internal security advisory (in your text editor) addressed to the Wholesale Markets desk at a fictional UK bank called "Meridian Bank PLC." The advisory should: (1) explain the specific impersonation risk arising from the FCA leadership announcement, (2) provide three concrete indicators of compromise or suspicious communication patterns staff should watch for, (3) specify the exact verification steps an employee must follow before acting on any instruction that claims FCA authority, and (4) include a sentence on how staff should report suspected impersonation attempts internally. Format it as you would a real internal memo — include a TLP marking (use TLP:GREEN as appropriate for internal distribution) and a review date 90 days from today.

                  REFLECTION QUESTIONS
                  1. Q1: The FCA press release was published on the official FCA domain and is entirely legitimate — yet it provided direct material for a social engineering threat profile. What does this tell you about the relationship between transparency in regulated industries and the attack surface available to threat actors, and how should your firm's security awareness programme account for this tension?
                  2. Q2: You found that the FCA public register confirms approved persons and their controlled functions by name. A threat actor could use this to craft highly targeted spear-phishing against specific individuals at your firm who hold those same controlled functions. What additional mitigations — beyond staff awareness — would you recommend to a CISO to reduce this risk at a technical or process level?
                  3. Q3: Regulatory impersonation attacks often succeed in the first days after a high-profile appointment because staff assume legitimacy before verification habits kick in. How would you design a 72-hour rapid-response procedure within your bank's security operations function to reduce this window of elevated risk every time a major regulatory appointment is publicly announced?
                  FURTHER READING

                  NCSC Guidance — "Phishing Attacks: Defending Your Organisation" (includes impersonation and BEC-specific advice): https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/guidance/phishing

                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  25 May 2026
                  Mapping Exploit-to-Patch Lag: Building a Vulnerability Prioritisation Model for a UK Bank's Crown Jewels
                  Intermediate ⏱ 90 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 Verizon DBIR: Enterprises Face a Dangerous Vulnerability Glut
                  SCENARIO

                  Barcliff Merchant Bank's CISO has received an early briefing on the Verizon 2026 DBIR, which reports that exploits now account for 31% of initial access vectors in confirmed breaches — a significant jump from prior years. The bank's IT asset register shows over 4,000 internet-facing and internal systems, but the vulnerability management team is struggling to justify patch prioritisation decisions to the board. Following a near-miss last quarter where a known-exploited vulnerability sat unpatched for 47 days on a payment gateway, the security team has been tasked with building a structured, evidence-based vulnerability triage framework before the next audit cycle. You have been asked to prototype and stress-test that framework today.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  Build and validate a practical vulnerability prioritisation framework tailored to a UK financial services environment, using real exploit and patching data to identify where remediation lag creates the highest business risk.

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Navigate to CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalogue: https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog
                  2. Use the filter options to identify the 10 most recently added CVEs in the catalogue. For each one, note: CVE ID, vendor/product, date added to KEV, and the "Due Date" CISA assigns to US federal agencies.
                  3. Open a plain text file or spreadsheet. Create columns: `CVE_ID | Vendor | Product | KEV_Added_Date | CISA_Due_Date | Days_to_Patch_Required | CVSS_Score | Asset_Type_Relevance_to_FinServ`
                  4. Fill in the first seven columns from the KEV catalogue and NVD (https://nvd.nist.gov/). For CVSS scores, look up each CVE on NVD directly. **Step 2 — Assess financial services relevance (15 minutes)**
                  5. For the `Asset_Type_Relevance_to_FinServ` column, categorise each CVE as: HIGH (affects technology commonly used in banking — e.g. network infrastructure, Windows Server, Oracle DB, Citrix, VPN gateways, payment systems), MEDIUM (affects common enterprise tech not finance-specific), or LOW (affects technology rarely seen in regulated FinServ environments).
                  6. Identify which of your 10 CVEs would realistically appear in a UK bank's attack surface. Write a one-paragraph justification for your top 3 highest-risk entries, referencing the specific product and why it matters to a financial institution. **Step 3 — Build a prioritisation scoring model (25 minutes)**
                  7. Open Claude.ai (free tier is sufficient). Paste in your completed table and use this exact prompt: *"I am a security engineer at a UK bank. Here is a table of 10 recently added CISA KEV entries with CVSS scores and asset relevance ratings. Help me design a weighted scoring formula that combines: (1) CVSS score, (2) days available to patch, (3) financial services asset relevance, and (4) whether the vulnerability enables initial access vs privilege escalation vs data exfiltration. Output the formula, explain the weighting rationale, and re-rank my 10 CVEs using the new score."*
                  8. Review Claude's suggested formula critically. Does it account for compensating controls? Does it treat internet-facing assets differently from internal ones? Note at least two weaknesses in the model it produces.
                  9. Iterate: send a follow-up prompt asking Claude to add a fifth factor — **regulatory exposure** — specifically accounting for FCA notification requirements under DORA (Digital Operational Resilience Act) and whether a successful exploit would trigger a material incident report. Ask it to re-score. **Step 4 — Simulate a patch lag scenario (15 minutes)**
                  10. Pick your highest-scoring CVE from the re-ranked list. Open a text editor and write a one-page "Breach Timeline Simulation" — a realistic narrative describing what an attacker could accomplish at a UK bank if this vulnerability remained unpatched for 30, 60, and 90 days respectively. Include: initial access method, lateral movement opportunity, likely target data (e.g. SWIFT credentials, customer PII, trading data), and regulatory consequence at each time stage.
                  11. Reference the Verizon DBIR statistic (31% of breaches via exploit) to frame the probability context in your narrative. **Step 5 — Validate against a real patching benchmark (20 minutes)**
                  12. Navigate to the Qualys TruRisk Research threat landscape reports (free public access): https://www.qualys.com/research/
                  13. Search for their most recent "Threat Research" or patch Tuesday analysis to find data on mean time to patch (MTTP) for enterprise environments. Note the industry average figure.
                  14. Compare this MTTP figure against the CISA "Due Date" windows you recorded in Step 1. In your text file, calculate: for each of your 10 CVEs, how many days of exposure would the average enterprise accumulate if patching at the industry mean rate? Flag any CVEs where the gap exceeds 14 days as "critical lag risk." ---
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  Using everything you have built in the challenge, write a one-page **Vulnerability Prioritisation Policy Draft** for Barcliff Merchant Bank. It must include: - A defined SLA table with patch deadlines tiered by your scoring model (e.g. Score >8: patch within 24hrs; Score 6–8: 7 days; etc.) - A named owner for each tier (e.g. SOC, IT Ops, Application Teams) - A specific clause addressing KEV-listed CVEs — arguing whether the bank should adopt CISA KEV deadlines as a minimum baseline even though CISA's jurisdiction is US federal agencies - A DORA/FCA escalation trigger: define the exact conditions under which an unpatched critical vulnerability must be escalated to the CISO and documented as a regulatory risk - One sentence acknowledging the limitation of CVSS as a sole prioritisation metric, citing a specific reason from your lab findings ---

                  REFLECTION QUESTIONS
                  1. Q1: The Veri
                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  22 May 2026
                  Detecting SQL Injection Vectors in Financial Application Code Reviews
                  Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 CVE-2026-48231 (CVSS 7.1)
                  SCENARIO

                  Your SOC has received a threat intelligence alert indicating that a third-party case management system used by your bank's Financial Crime Compliance team — Open ISES Tickets — is running an unpatched version vulnerable to CVE-2026-48231 (CVSS 7.1). The system holds sensitive SAR (Suspicious Activity Report) drafts, customer due diligence records, and internal investigation notes. An authenticated insider threat actor or compromised analyst account could exploit the SQL injection flaw to exfiltrate or destroy investigation data before your patching window opens next week. Your task is to understand the attack surface, identify similar patterns in your own codebase, and produce detection guidance before the patch is applied.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  Identify and document SQL injection vulnerabilities arising from unsanitised POST parameter concatenation into dynamic SQL identifiers, and produce a detection ruleset applicable to a UK financial services application environment. ---

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. It should: - Accept three POST parameters: `tablename`, `indexname`, `sortby` - Concatenate them directly into a SQL string - Execute the query with no sanitisation You are writing this for defensive code review purposes. You do not need a running server — this is static analysis practice. **Step 3 — Identify Detection Patterns (15 minutes)** Go to the OWASP SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/SQL_Injection_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html Read the section on "Safe ways to incorporate user-supplied table or column names". In your text editor, answer: - What does OWASP recommend for cases where identifiers (not values) must come from user input? - Write a short allowlist validation function in pseudocode that would safely handle the `tablename` parameter for a system that only ever queries five known tables **Step 4 — Simulate a Code Review (20 minutes)** Paste your vulnerable snippet from Step 2 into Claude.ai with this prompt: > "You are a secure code reviewer at a UK financial services firm. Review this code snippet for SQL injection vulnerabilities related to identifier concatenation. Identify the exact lines that are dangerous, explain the risk in terms of data confidentiality, integrity, and availability, and suggest a remediated version using an allowlist approach. Present your findings as if writing a finding in a security review report, with a risk rating and recommended fix." Copy the output into your text editor. Edit it to: - Replace any generic references with references to the financial services context (e.g. mention SAR data, customer due diligence records, FCA reporting obligations) - Add a "Regulatory Impact" section noting which FCA/PRA obligations could be breached if investigation data were exfiltrated or destroyed (hint: look up SYSC 8 and DORA Article 9) **Step 5 — Write a WAF / SIEM Detection Rule (15 minutes)** In your text editor, write a detection rule in plain pseudocode (no specific tool required) that a WAF or SIEM could use to flag suspicious POST requests targeting the vulnerable endpoint. Your rule must: - Specify the HTTP method and target path pattern - List at least six suspicious characters or SQL keywords to match in POST body parameters - Include a condition for authenticated sessions specifically (because this is an authenticated vulnerability) - Include a severity rating and suggested response action (alert, block, or log) Use this structure: ``` RULE NAME: TRIGGER CONDITIONS: SUSPICIOUS PATTERNS: AUTHENTICATED SESSION CONDITION: SEVERITY: RESPONSE ACTION: NOTES: ``` ---
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  You are the security lead responsible for the Financial Crime Compliance system. Write a one-page Compensating Controls Document (in your text editor) covering the period between now and the patching window. It must include: 1. **Immediate network controls** — how you would restrict access to the vulnerable endpoint at the firewall or reverse proxy level without breaking legitimate analyst workflows 2. **Monitoring uplift** — what specific database-level or application-level logging you would enable immediately, and what you would look for in those logs to detect exploitation attempts 3. **User communication** — a two-sentence alert to the 12 analysts who use the system, telling them what to do differently this week without causing panic or disclosing exploitable detail 4. **Patch verification steps** — three specific things you would check after applying version 3.44.2 to confirm the fix is effective, written as testable acceptance criteria ---

                  REFLECTION QUESTIONS
                  1. Q1: CVE-2026-48231 requires an authenticated attacker. In a financial services context, what realistic threat actors could satisfy the authentication requirement, and how does this change your risk model compared to an unauthenticated vulnerability of the same CVSS score?
                  2. Q2: Standard parameterised queries prevent injection into SQL values but do NOT protect against identifier injection (table names, column names). Why is this distinction important when reviewing ORMs and query builders used in financial applications, and what class of developer assumption does it exploit?
                  3. Q3: Your SIEM rule from Step 5 will generate false positives if analysts use special characters in legitimate data entry. How would you tune the rule
                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  21 May 2026
                  Hunt the Hidden XSS: Detecting Fragmented Payload Injection in Financial Network Device Audit Logs
                  Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 CVE-2026-9144 (CVSS 7.6)
                  SCENARIO

                  Your SOC has received an alert from the network operations team at Hartwell Building Society. A Taiko AG1000-01A SMS Alert Gateway — used to send authentication SMS codes to customers and staff — has had its admin interface accessed by three different administrator accounts over the past fortnight. A junior network engineer noticed that some configuration fields in the device's web interface contain unusual text strings that don't match normal device settings. You suspect a threat actor with stolen admin credentials has planted a stored XSS payload across multiple form fields to hijack future admin sessions. Because this device sits in the path of SMS-based MFA for online banking, a successful session hijack could allow the attacker to intercept or redirect one-time passcodes sent to customers.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  By completing this lab, the engineer will be able to identify, reconstruct, and document fragmented stored XSS payloads that have been split across multiple form fields to evade front-end length restrictions, and explain why this technique is particularly dangerous on network management devices in a financial services environment.

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Open a text editor (VS Code, Notepad++, or any plain text editor). Create a new file called `xss_lab.txt` to record your findings throughout this exercise.
                  2. Open Claude.ai. Ask it the following question exactly: *"Explain how an attacker might split a stored XSS payload across multiple HTML form fields using JavaScript comments and template literals to reconstruct executable code. Show me a benign, clearly educational example with no actual harmful capability — use an alert() call only."*
                  3. Read the response carefully. In your `xss_lab.txt` file, write down in your own words: (a) what JavaScript template literals are, (b) how JS comments can be used to join fragments, and (c) why splitting across fields defeats simple front-end `maxlength` attribute restrictions.
                  4. Now ask Claude.ai: *"If a device configuration interface stored these fragments in a database and rendered them together in a dashboard template, at what point does the browser reconstruct and execute the payload?"* Note the answer — this is the key moment the attacker is targeting. --- **Part 2 — Reconstruct a simulated fragmented payload (20 minutes)**
                  5. In your text editor, create a new file called `fragment_analysis.html`. You are going to simulate what the vulnerable device's dashboard might render. Type the following structure manually — do not copy-paste, so you absorb what each part does: ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head><title>Simulated Device Dashboard</title></head> <body> <h2>SMS Gateway Config Review</h2> <!-- Simulating how three admin form fields are stored and rendered --> <p id="field1"><!-- field1 value from DB: --></p> <p id="field2"><!-- field2 value from DB: --></p> <p id="field3"><!-- field3 value from DB: --></p> <!-- DO NOT add actual script tags. This file is for structural analysis only. --> <!-- Your task: annotate below how fragments in field1, field2, field3 could be concatenated by the browser into a complete script --> </body> </html> ```
                  6. In the comment block at the bottom, write out in plain English (not live code) how an attacker might use field1 to open a template literal, field2 to contain the payload body, and field3 to close and execute it. Describe the role of `/*` and `*/` comment characters in bridging the field boundaries.
                  7. Save the file. You will not open this in a browser — this is a static analysis exercise. The point is to think like a code reviewer examining stored database content. --- **Part 3 — Examine real-world XSS detection guidance (15 minutes)**
                  8. Navigate to the OWASP XSS Filter Evasion Cheat Sheet at: `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/XSS_Filter_Evasion_Cheat_Sheet.html`
                  9. Search the page (Ctrl+F) for "template" and then for "comment". Read the relevant sections. In your `xss_lab.txt`, note down two specific evasion techniques listed that would be relevant to this fragmentation attack — techniques that a WAF or input validation rule might miss.
                  10. Now navigate to the OWASP Testing Guide section on stored XSS: `https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/latest/4-Web_Application_Security_Testing/07-Input_Validation_Testing/02-Testing_for_Stored_Cross_Site_Scripting`
                  11. Read the "How to Test" section. In your `xss_lab.txt`, write down the three pieces of evidence an investigator should look for in server-side logs and database records to confirm a stored XSS payload has been planted — translate each one into what it would look like specifically on a network device config interface. --- **Part 4 — Build a detection checklist for network device interfaces (25 minutes)**
                  12. Return to Claude.ai. Ask: *"I am a security analyst investigating a possible stored XSS attack on an embedded network device web interface used in a financial services firm. The attacker is known to fragment payloads across multiple admin form fields. What specific strings, patterns, or character sequences should I search for in database exports or configuration backups to detect this? Give me a practical list suitable for grep or a text search tool."*
                  13. Take the output and paste the most relevant 8–10 indicators into your `xss_lab.txt` under a heading called `INDICATORS OF COMPROMISE — FRAGMENTED XSS`.
                  14. Open a terminal (or use the search function in your text editor). Create a plain text file called `mock_config_export.txt` containing the following simulated device configuration export — type it out manually: ``` device_name=HARTWELL-SMS-GW-01 admin_email=netops@hartwell.co.uk alert_prefix=HARTWELL BANK sms_footer=Replies not monitored notification_field1=Standard alert template ` notification_field2=/* continue */ +document[
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  20 May 2026
                  Detect AI-Enabled Investment Fraud Patterns in Customer Communication Logs Using Behavioural Analysis
                  Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 Working together against financial crime
                  SCENARIO

                  Your threat intelligence team at Meridian Bank (a mid-sized UK retail and investment bank) has escalated an alert. The FCA has issued a Dear CEO letter warning that organised criminal groups are using AI-generated scripts to conduct authorised push payment (APP) fraud via investment scams — specifically targeting the bank's wealthier retail customers aged 55+. Three customers have already reported suspicious unsolicited calls promising high-yield bond investments. The fraud operations team suspects the scripts share linguistic patterns but cannot process the volume manually. You have been asked to build a repeatable detection method before the weekend trading window opens.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  By completing this lab, the engineer will be able to identify linguistic and structural patterns in AI-generated investment fraud scripts that can inform detection rules for customer communication monitoring systems.

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Open Claude.ai (free account).
                  2. Paste the following prompt exactly: > "For a security research and fraud detection exercise at a financial institution, generate 5 short examples of the kind of investment fraud phone script that organised criminal groups use to deceive UK retail banking customers into authorised push payments. Each script should be 4–6 sentences. Label them SCRIPT_A through SCRIPT_E. Include realistic but fictional company names, returns, and urgency tactics."
                  3. Copy the five scripts into a plain text file called `fraud_corpus.txt`. Save it.
                  4. Now prompt Claude.ai again: > "Generate 3 examples of legitimate investment enquiry scripts that a real UK-regulated financial adviser might use when calling an existing client. Label them LEGIT_1 through LEGIT_3."
                  5. Append these to a second file called `legit_corpus.txt`. **Step 2 — Manual linguistic feature extraction (15 mins)**
                  6. Open a new text file called `feature_analysis.md`.
                  7. Read through all 8 scripts carefully. For each one, note: - **Urgency language**: words like "limited time", "only today", "act now" - **Authority spoofing**: references to FCA, HMRC, government, bank names - **Guaranteed return claims**: specific percentages, "guaranteed", "risk-free" - **Isolation tactics**: discouraging the target from consulting others ("don't tell your bank") - **Call-to-action pressure**: requests for immediate bank transfer or account details
                  8. Create a simple markdown table with columns: `Script ID | Urgency | Authority Spoof | Guaranteed Returns | Isolation Tactic | CTA Pressure`. Score each feature 0 (absent) or 1 (present). **Step 3 — Build a keyword detection rule set (15 mins)**
                  9. In `feature_analysis.md`, create a section called `## Detection Rule Set`.
                  10. Write a minimum of **10 keyword/phrase triggers** drawn from your analysis — things that, if found in a customer-reported transcript or email, should flag for human review. Example format: ``` RULE_01: "guaranteed returns" → FLAG: High RULE_02: "FCA registered" + unsolicited context → FLAG: High ```
                  11. For each rule, assign a severity: High / Medium / Low.
                  12. Group rules into two categories: **Single-signal triggers** (flag alone) and **Combination triggers** (only flag if two or more appear together). **Step 4 — Test your rules against a new sample (10 mins)**
                  13. Go back to Claude.ai and ask it to generate one new fraud script and one new legitimate script WITHOUT labelling which is which — ask it to call them MYSTERY_1 and MYSTERY_2.
                  14. Run your rule set manually against both texts. Record your findings in `feature_analysis.md` under a section called `## Rule Validation`.
                  15. Ask Claude.ai to reveal which was which and check your accuracy. **Step 5 — Identify evasion weaknesses (15 mins)**
                  16. Prompt Claude.ai: > "As a fraud detection researcher, what linguistic variations might a sophisticated criminal use to evade the following keyword rules?" — then paste your rule set.
                  17. Document at least **3 evasion techniques** identified and note how each one would bypass your current rules.
                  18. Save your completed `feature_analysis.md`. ---
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  Using your findings, draft a one-page **Fraud Communication Detection Policy** (in your text editor) for Meridian Bank's customer-facing teams. It should include: (1) a triage process for customer-reported suspicious calls, (2) your finalised rule set with evasion-hardened updates, (3) a recommendation on where in the customer journey this detection should sit (e.g. post-call reporting portal, branch staff checklist, or real-time call transcript analysis), and (4) a note on the FCA's regulatory expectation under the Consumer Duty for proactive fraud prevention. Save this as `meridian_fraud_detection_policy_draft.md`.

                  REFLECTION QUESTIONS
                  1. Q1: Your rule set likely performed well on obvious scripts but struggled with subtle ones — what does this tell you about the limitations of purely signature-based detection for AI-generated fraud content, and what complementary detection method would you layer on top in a production environment?
                  2. Q2: The FCA's financial crime strategy emphasises cross-industry data sharing. If Meridian Bank could share anonymised fraud script patterns with other UK banks via a trusted third party (such as UK Finance), what specific data points from your feature analysis would be most valuable to share, and what data protection considerations under UK GDPR would apply?
                  3. Q3: Organised criminal groups operating across borders deliberately mutate their scripts to avoid detection. Given the evasion techniques you identified in Step 5, at what point does a keyword-rule approach become operationally insufficient, and what would the business case look like for escalating to an ML-based behavioural detection model?
                  FURTHER READING

                  FCA Financial Crime Guide (full text, publicly available) — specifically Chapter 2 on fraud controls and the updated Consumer Duty obligations: https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/FCG/2/

                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  19 May 2026
                  Hunt the Browser Beachhead — Detecting Chromium-Based RCE Indicators in a FinServ Endpoint Environment
                  Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 CVE-2026-45495 (CVSS 8.8)
                  SCENARIO

                  Your SOC has received an alert from the EDR platform at Meridian Bank's London trading floor. Three analysts have reported their Microsoft Edge browser crashing unexpectedly after visiting what appeared to be a legitimate financial news aggregator site. The threat intelligence team suspects a drive-by download campaign exploiting a Chromium-based RCE vulnerability (CVE-2026-45495, CVSS 8.8) targeting UK financial institutions. Given that trading floor workstations have privileged access to order management systems and Bloomberg terminals, a successful browser compromise could be catastrophic. You need to understand the attack surface, identify what post-exploitation indicators to hunt for, and help the team decide whether to pull the affected machines offline.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  By completing this lab, the engineer will be able to map the attack chain for a Chromium-based RCE exploitation scenario, identify realistic detection artefacts across the kill chain, and produce a prioritised indicator-of-compromise (IoC) hunting checklist tailored to a FinServ Windows endpoint.

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Open your text editor and create a new file called `cve-2026-45495-analysis.md`.
                  2. Navigate to https://attack.mitre.org and search for "Drive-by Compromise". Read the T1189 technique page carefully.
                  3. Also read T1203 (Exploitation for Client Execution) at https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1203/
                  4. In your markdown file, write a section called `## Vulnerability Profile` and answer these four questions in your own words: - What is the general mechanism by which a Chromium renderer process can be leveraged for RCE? - Why does a CVSS score of 8.8 suggest user interaction is likely required (hint: check the CVSS scoring criteria at https://www.first.org/cvss/calculator/3.1)? - What is the "renderer sandbox" in Chromium and why does an attacker typically need a *second* vulnerability (a sandbox escape) to fully compromise the host OS? - Why are trading floor workstations a higher-value target than standard corporate desktops in a FinServ context? **Step 2 — Build the Attack Chain (15 minutes)**
                  5. In your markdown file, create a section called `## Attack Chain Mapping`.
                  6. Using the MITRE ATT&CK framework pages you already have open, map out a realistic 6-stage attack chain an adversary would follow *after* successfully triggering this RCE. Label each stage with its ATT&CK technique ID. Your chain must include: - Initial access via the browser exploit - Establishing persistence on the endpoint - Credential harvesting (think about what lives in a browser profile) - Lateral movement toward internal systems - Exfiltration of trading data or credentials - Command and control
                  7. For each stage, write one sentence explaining *why* this step is particularly dangerous in a UK bank context (e.g. FCA regulatory implications, access to SWIFT, market abuse risk). **Step 3 — Generate Realistic IoCs and Detection Artefacts (20 minutes)**
                  8. Open Claude.ai (free tier is fine).
                  9. Use the following prompt — copy it exactly, then review and critically evaluate the output: ``` I am a SOC analyst at a UK financial institution. A Chromium-based browser RCE vulnerability (similar in class to CVE-2026-45495, CVSS 8.8) may have been exploited on trading floor workstations running Windows 11 and Microsoft Edge. Please provide:
                  10. A list of 10 realistic Windows Event Log event IDs I should hunt for that could indicate post-exploitation activity following a browser RCE, with a one-line explanation of why each is relevant.
                  11. Five realistic file system artefact paths or registry keys an attacker might use for persistence after a browser-based initial access on Windows 11.
                  12. Three network-based detection signatures (in plain English, not Snort syntax) for C2 traffic that commonly follows browser exploitation. Format your response as a structured list I can paste into a detection runbook. ```
                  13. Paste the Claude output into your markdown file under a new section: `## Detection Artefacts`.
                  14. Now critically review the output: mark any artefact with ⚠️ if you think it is too generic or might generate excessive false positives in a busy financial services environment. Add a one-line note explaining your concern for each flagged item. **Step 4 — Build Your Hunting Checklist (15 minutes)**
                  15. Create a new section in your file: `## SOC Hunting Checklist`.
                  16. Based on your attack chain and the detection artefacts above, write a prioritised checklist of exactly 10 hunting actions a Tier-1 SOC analyst should take *within the first 30 minutes* of this alert.
                  17. Number each action 1–10 in order of priority. For each action specify: - The data source (e.g. Windows Event Log, EDR telemetry, proxy logs, DNS logs) - The specific thing to look for - The triage decision it informs (isolate / escalate / monitor)
                  18. Highlight with **[FCA NOTIFIABLE?]** any hunting action that, if it confirmed compromise, would trigger a mandatory notification obligation under FCA SYSC rules or the UK GDPR breach notification window (72 hours to ICO). **Step 5 — Simulate a Triage Decision (10 minutes)**
                  19. Open a new Claude.ai conversation and use this prompt: ``` I am triaging a potential browser RCE incident at a UK bank. Three trading floor workstations show Edge crash dumps and unusual child processes spawned from msedge.exe. The workstations have not been isolated yet. They have access to our order management system and internal network shares. Based on this information, give me a structured triage recommendation covering: (1) immediate containment actions, (2) evidence preservation steps I must take before isolation, (3) communications I need to send internally in the next 15 minutes, and (4) whether this meets the threshold for FCA notification.
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  18 May 2026
                  Auditing AI-Generated Code for Financial Services Security Flaws Before They Reach Production
                  Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 The Boring Stuff is Dangerous Now
                  SCENARIO

                  A developer at Meridian Bank's digital banking team has been using an AI coding assistant to accelerate delivery of a new open banking API integration. During a routine pull request review, a senior engineer flags that several code snippets appear to have been generated wholesale by an AI tool and merged without adequate security review. The CISO has raised an urgent concern: AI-generated code may contain subtle but exploitable vulnerabilities — insecure defaults, hardcoded secrets, missing input validation — that look syntactically correct but introduce serious risk. You have been tasked with auditing a sample of AI-generated code before it can be deployed to the production environment handling PSD2-regulated customer payment data.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  The engineer will learn to systematically identify, classify, and document security vulnerabilities commonly introduced by AI-generated code in a financial services context, and produce a structured pre-merge security review artefact. ---

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Open Claude.ai (free tier is sufficient).
                  2. Paste this exact prompt: > "Write me a Python REST API endpoint for a UK open banking service. It should accept a POST request with a customer account number and sort code, look up a balance from a backend database, log the request, and return the balance as JSON. Make it functional and ready to use."
                  3. Copy the full code response into a new file in your text editor. Save it as `ob_api_review.py`.
                  4. Do NOT ask Claude to make it secure yet — you want the raw first-pass output. --- **Step 2 — Threat model the code before reading it carefully (10 minutes)** Before line-by-line review, think like an attacker. In a second file (`threat_model.md`), write down at least **five** specific threats relevant to a payment data API in a UK financial institution. Consider: - What data is being processed? (Hint: sort codes and account numbers are sensitive under UK GDPR and PSD2) - Who might call this endpoint maliciously? - What could go wrong with logging in a regulated environment? - What happens if the database query is constructed from user input? - What authentication assumptions are being made? Write each threat in the format: `THREAT: [what] | ACTOR: [who] | IMPACT: [consequence]` --- **Step 3 — Manual code review (20 minutes)** Now read the code carefully. In a third file (`findings.md`), document every security issue you find. For each finding, record: ``` FINDING ID: F-001 LINE(S): [line numbers] CATEGORY: [e.g. Injection / Secrets Management / Logging / Auth / Input Validation / Error Handling] DESCRIPTION: [what the flaw is] EXPLOITABILITY: [High / Medium / Low — and why] PSD2/UK GDPR RELEVANCE: [yes/no and brief reason] ``` Look specifically for: - SQL or NoSQL injection (is user input sanitised before hitting the DB?) - Hardcoded credentials, connection strings, or API keys - Overly verbose logging (does it log account numbers or sort codes in plaintext? That's a UK GDPR breach risk) - Missing authentication or authorisation checks on the endpoint - No rate limiting (relevant for PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication requirements) - Generic exception handling that leaks stack traces or internal structure - HTTP rather than HTTPS assumptions - Any use of `eval()`, `exec()`, or dynamic code execution Aim to find at least **six distinct findings**. --- **Step 4 — Cross-reference against OWASP API Security Top 10 (10 minutes)**
                  5. Visit: https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x11-t10/
                  6. For each of your findings in `findings.md`, add a field: ``` OWASP API TOP 10 MAPPING: [e.g. API3:2023 Broken Object Property Level Authorisation] ```
                  7. Identify which OWASP category appears **most frequently** in your findings. Note this at the top of `findings.md` as a summary line. --- **Step 5 — Ask Claude to review its own code (10 minutes)**
                  8. Return to Claude.ai. Paste the generated code back in with this prompt: > "You are a security engineer at a UK financial institution. Review this code for security vulnerabilities relevant to PSD2, UK GDPR, and OWASP API Security Top 10. List every issue you find with severity ratings."
                  9. Compare Claude's self-review against your own `findings.md`.
                  10. In a new section of `findings.md` titled `## Gap Analysis`, note: - Findings you caught that Claude missed - Findings Claude caught that you missed - Any finding where Claude's severity rating differed from yours — and who you think was right and why --- **Step 6 — Classify findings by risk rating (15 minutes)** Using the scoring guidance at https://owasp.org/www-community/OWASP_Risk_Rating_Methodology, assign each finding a composite risk score (Likelihood × Impact = Risk). Add this to each finding record: ``` RISK SCORE: [Critical / High / Medium / Low] RATIONALE: [one sentence explaining your scoring] ``` Then produce a short prioritised remediation list at the bottom of `findings.md` — order findings from most to least urgent and state what a developer should do to fix each one. ---
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  Using your findings, draft a one-page **AI-Generated Code Security Review Policy** for Meridian Bank (`ai_code_policy.md`). It must include: 1. **Mandatory review gates** — define at what stage AI-generated code must be reviewed before it can touch any system handling payment, customer identity, or regulated data 2. **Required checks** — a minimum checklist a reviewer must complete (derive this from your actual findings — make it specific, not generic) 3. **Prohibited patterns** — a list of specific code patterns that must never appear in production (e.g. plaintext logging of account data, unauthenticated endpoints handling PSD2 data) 4. **Escalation criteria** — define which finding categories or

                  ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
                  NOT STARTED
                  15 May 2026
                  Mapping Broken Object-Level Authorisation in a Financial Services API Gateway
                  Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 CVE-2026-8629 (CVSS 8.1)
                  SCENARIO

                  Barclays-style retail banking platform has deployed an internal infrastructure orchestration tool to manage leased compute environments across its hybrid cloud estate. A routine third-party security review has flagged that the tool exposes privileged ticket-generation endpoints over its REST API. A junior DevSecOps engineer with read-only ("visibility-only") access to a shared UAT environment has reportedly been able to obtain agent tickets that should require elevated permissions — a textbook Broken Object-Level Authorisation (BOLA) vulnerability. Your SOC has been asked to assess the blast radius: if an attacker compromised a low-privilege service account, could they pivot through the API to impersonate trusted bridge agents and exfiltrate data from the payment processing estate?

                  OBJECTIVE

                  By completing this lab, the engineer will be able to identify, document, and communicate a Broken Object-Level Authorisation (BOLA) vulnerability in a REST API, map it to OWASP API Security Top 10 and PCI DSS controls, and produce a risk-rated finding suitable for a financial services vulnerability report. ---

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Open your text editor and create a new file called `bola_lab_notes.md`.
                  2. Navigate to https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa1-broken-object-level-authorization/ and read the full entry for API1:2023.
                  3. In your notes file, write in your own words (3–5 sentences) what BOLA is, how it differs from a missing authentication check, and why it is particularly dangerous in multi-tenant financial platforms.
                  4. Now read the CVE summary above carefully. In your notes, answer: which specific HTTP method and which three endpoint patterns are exploitable, and what permission level is required to exploit them? --- **Part 2 — Model the Attack Path (20 minutes)**
                  5. In your text editor, draw an ASCII attack chain. It must include these nodes, connected with arrows and short labels: ``` [Attacker: visibility-only account] | | POST /v1/leases/:id/code/ticket v [API Server: insufficient access control check] | | Returns bridge-agent ticket v [Attacker impersonates trusted lease-side bridge] | | Accesses Code / WebVNC / Egress agents v [Potential: lateral movement into payment estate] ```
                  6. Beneath the diagram, write two realistic attacker motivations specific to a UK financial institution (e.g. access to SWIFT messaging agents, screen-scraping VNC sessions of trading terminals).
                  7. Identify the three ticket types (Code, WebVNC, Egress) and write one sentence each explaining what a financial services attacker could do with each ticket type if obtained — be specific to FinServ systems (think: code execution on a reconciliation server, VNC access to a trader's workstation, egress tunnelling to exfiltrate CREST settlement data). --- **Part 3 — Simulate the API Request (20 minutes)**
                  8. You will not interact with any real system. Instead, open Claude.ai (free tier is sufficient).
                  9. Paste this prompt exactly into Claude: > "You are a security API documentation assistant. Write out what a malicious HTTP POST request to the endpoint `/v1/leases/abc123/egress/ticket` would look like if the attacker only had a visibility-only Bearer token. Show the raw HTTP request headers and body. Then explain what a correctly implemented access control check on the server side should verify before issuing the ticket. Do not execute anything — this is a documentation exercise."
                  10. Copy Claude's response into your `bola_lab_notes.md` file under a heading `## Simulated Request`.
                  11. Annotate the response: highlight (with a comment beginning `# NOTE:`) exactly where the server-side check is missing or insufficient based on the CVE description.
                  12. Now send Claude a second prompt: > "For a UK financial services firm governed by PCI DSS v4.0 and FCA operational resilience requirements, list the specific controls that, if implemented, would have prevented this BOLA vulnerability. Map each control to either a PCI DSS requirement number or an FCA SYSC rule."
                  13. Copy this response into your notes under `## Regulatory Control Mapping`. --- **Part 4 — Risk Rate the Finding (10 minutes)**
                  14. In your notes file, create a section called `## Vulnerability Finding Card`.
                  15. Complete the following fields manually (do not ask Claude — use your own judgement based on what you have learned): ``` Vulnerability: CVE Reference: CVSS Score: OWASP Category: Affected Component: Attack Prerequisite: Confidentiality Impact (with FinServ-specific example): Integrity Impact (with FinServ-specific example): Availability Impact: Internal Risk Rating (Critical / High / Medium / Low — justify in one sentence): ```
                  16. For Internal Risk Rating: consider that this system manages leased compute in a hybrid cloud estate that includes payment processing workloads. Apply the principle that a CVSS 8.1 from NVD may not reflect your organisation's specific context — adjust up or down and justify your reasoning. --- **Part 5 — Check for Exposure (10 minutes)**
                  17. Navigate to https://www.shodan.io (free, no account required for basic searches).
                  18. Search for: `crabbox` and separately for `"lease" "webvnc"` — note any results (there may be none, which is itself informative). Record what you find (or don't find) in your notes under `## Exposure Assessment`.
                  19. Search NVD directly: https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/search — search for `crabbox` and note whether any related CVEs appear alongside CVE-2026-8629. Document whether a patch version is referenced (v0.12.0 per the advisory).
                  20. Write two sentences on what an organisation should do in the next 24 hours if they are running a vulnerable version and cannot immediately patch. ---
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  Design a one-page **API Access Control Policy** for

                  14 May 2026
                  Detecting Linux Kernel Privilege Escalation Attempts on FinServ Infrastructure Using Log Analysis
                  Intermediate ⏱ 75 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 New Fragnesia Linux flaw lets attackers gain root privileges
                  SCENARIO

                  Your SOC has received an alert from the SIEM at Meridian Bank's core banking platform, which runs on Linux-based servers hosting payment processing workloads. A threat intelligence feed has flagged CVE-2026-46300 ("Fragnesia") — a high-severity Linux kernel privilege escalation vulnerability — as being actively exploited in the wild against financial sector targets. Your vulnerability management team has confirmed that several internal servers are running unpatched kernel versions. Before the patching window opens tomorrow night, your job is to understand what a successful exploitation attempt looks like in Linux audit logs, build a detection logic document, and design a compensating control. A junior analyst has already flagged some suspicious `sudo` and `suid` activity on one of the payment gateway hosts.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  By completing this lab, the engineer will be able to identify the log artefacts and behavioural indicators associated with Linux kernel privilege escalation exploitation, and produce a documented detection rule suitable for submission to the SOC's SIEM team. ---

                  CHALLENGE
                  1. Open your text editor and create a file called `fragnesia_lab_notes.md`.
                  2. Navigate to https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/syscalls.2.html — this is the Linux syscall reference. You don't need to read everything; scan for categories of syscalls related to memory management and process control.
                  3. In your notes file, write a two-sentence plain-English explanation of what "kernel privilege escalation" means in the context of a running process going from user-space to root. This is your baseline understanding — you'll refine it after Step 2. --- **Step 2 — Research exploitation indicators using Claude (15 minutes)**
                  4. Open Claude.ai (free tier is fine).
                  5. Paste the following prompt **exactly**: > "I am a security engineer at a UK bank. I need to understand what Linux kernel privilege escalation exploits — in general, not for any specific CVE — typically look like in Linux audit logs and /var/log/syslog. What process names, syscalls, log strings, or behavioural patterns should a SOC analyst look for? Please give me realistic example log lines I could use to build a SIEM detection rule."
                  6. Copy Claude's response into your `fragnesia_lab_notes.md` under a heading called `## Exploitation Indicators from Research`.
                  7. Now ask a follow-up prompt: > "What are the most common false positives that would produce similar log patterns in a financial services environment running payment processing workloads on Linux? Give me 5 specific examples."
                  8. Add this response under `## False Positives to Tune Out`. --- **Step 3 — Simulate and examine relevant log patterns (20 minutes)**
                  9. Navigate to https://github.com/failing/linux-audit-log-samples or, if unavailable, use this alternative: search GitHub (github.com) for "linux audit log samples privilege escalation" — pick any public repo with sample `.log` or `.txt` files.
                  10. Alternatively, use the following **synthetic log snippet** — paste this directly into your notes file under `## Sample Log Data`: ``` type=SYSCALL msg=audit(1718900123.456:1001): arch=c000003e syscall=59 success=yes exit=0 a0=7ffd1234 a1=0 a2=0 a3=0 items=2 ppid=1842 pid=1843 auid=1001 uid=1001 gid=1001 euid=0 egid=0 suid=0 sgid=0 fsuid=0 fsgid=0 tty=pts0 ses=5 comm="bash" exe="/bin/bash" key="privilege_esc" type=SYSCALL msg=audit(1718900124.789:1002): arch=c000003e syscall=105 success=yes exit=0 a0=0 a1=0 a2=0 items=0 ppid=1843 pid=1843 auid=1001 uid=0 gid=0 euid=0 egid=0 suid=0 fsuid=0 tty=pts0 ses=5 comm="bash" exe="/bin/bash" key="root_setuid" type=EXECVE msg=audit(1718900125.001:1003): argc=3 a0="chmod" a1="u+s" a2="/tmp/.hidden_shell" type=PATH msg=audit(1718900125.002:1004): item=0 name="/tmp/.hidden_shell" inode=99812 dev=fd:01 mode=0104755 ouid=0 ogid=0 rdev=00:00 nametype=NORMAL ```
                  11. In your notes file, under `## Log Analysis`, answer these questions in writing: - Which field in log line 1 tells you the process started with UID 1001 but gained effective UID 0? - What does syscall 105 correspond to? (Hint: look it up at https://filippo.io/linux-syscall-table/ ) - Why is `/tmp/.hidden_shell` with mode `0104755` (SUID bit set) a critical indicator? - What is `auid` and why does it matter for forensic attribution in a bank environment? --- **Step 4 — Write a SIEM detection rule in pseudo-code (15 minutes)**
                  12. In your notes file, create a section `## SIEM Detection Rule (Pseudo-code)`.
                  13. Write a detection rule in plain pseudo-code (not Splunk SPL or any specific language — just readable logic) that would fire an alert when: - A process transitions from non-root UID to euid=0 - AND the parent process is not a known privileged service (e.g. not `sshd`, `sudo`, `su`) - AND the event occurs on a host tagged as `payment-processing` or `core-banking` in your asset inventory - AND it happens outside your approved change window (weekdays 02:00–04
                  DEFENCE TASK

                  13 May 2026
                  Mapping Regulatory Obligation Gaps: Stress-Testing Your Firm's FCA Private Markets Compliance Posture
                  Beginner ⏱ 60 minutes
                  ▼
                  📄 Laying the foundation for confidence
                  SCENARIO

                  Your firm's Head of Compliance has flagged that the FCA is increasing scrutiny on private markets and private credit funds following recent stress events in the sector. A deputy CEO-level speech from the FCA has signalled that resilience planning, investor protection, and operational robustness in private markets are active supervisory priorities for 2026. Your security and compliance teams have been asked to jointly assess whether your firm's current controls documentation adequately maps to the FCA's stated expectations — before a potential supervisory visit. You have been tasked with identifying gaps between what the regulator expects and what your firm can currently evidence.

                  OBJECTIVE

                  Build a structured regulatory gap analysis matrix that maps FCA resilience and private markets supervision expectations to concrete security and operational controls, identifying at least three documented gaps a UK financial firm might realistically have. ---

                  CHALLENGE
                    DEFENCE TASK

                    Using your completed gap analysis matrix, draft a one-page **Remediation Brief** addressed to your fictional firm's Chief Risk Officer. The brief must include: 1. A plain-English summary of the top two Red-rated gaps you identified 2. For each gap, one specific, implementable control that could close it within 90 days — be concrete (e.g. "implement quarterly stress scenario sign-off at Board Risk Committee with documented minutes retained for five years") 3. A statement explaining how closing these gaps would reduce both regulatory risk *and* a security risk — demonstrate the intersection between compliance obligations and technical security controls such as access logging, data integrity, or system availability 4. A recommendation for one internal team (e.g. InfoSec, Legal, Operations) who should own each remediation action Write this as a professional document your CRO could actually act on. ---

                    REFLECTION QUESTIONS
                    1. Q1: The FCA speech references stress resilience as a shared responsibility across the financial system. In practical terms, what is the difference between a firm's *operational resilience* obligations under PS21/3 and its *financial resilience* obligations under ICARA — and why does a security engineer need to understand both when advising on controls?
                    2. Q2: When you used Claude to review your gap analysis, you were asked to flag unverified claims. What does this exercise reveal about the risks of using AI tools to interpret regulatory obligations in a supervised financial services firm — and what governance controls should your firm have in place before using AI in compliance workflows?
                    3. Q3: Your matrix likely identified gaps in documentation or evidence rather than gaps in actual controls. Why is the *evidence* of a control often as important as the control itself in an FCA supervisory context — and what does this mean for how your security team should
                    ⚡ Complete this lab by running python complete_lab.py in your terminal.
