                      FinServ context: A critical remote code execution flaw in a FortiSandbox deployed at a UK bank's network perimeter could allow an attacker to break out of the analysis environment and move laterally into core financial systems.
                      First seen: 13 May 2026
                      Fourth Retirement Pillar
                      A concept describing housing wealth as a potential additional source of retirement income alongside the state pension, workplace pensions, and private savings.
                      FinServ context: The FCA is encouraging lenders to treat later life lending products such as equity release as a mainstream retirement planning tool, which creates new consumer duty obligations around suitability assessment.
                      First seen: 19 June 2026
                      Fragnesia / Dirty Frag (Linux Kernel Privilege Escalation)
                      A high-severity flaw in the Linux kernel that allows a local attacker to gain full root-level administrative control over a system by exploiting the way the kernel handles memory fragments.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions running Linux-based servers — common in trading platforms, core banking, and cloud infrastructure — need to patch this urgently, as an attacker who gains an initial foothold could use it to take complete control of critical systems.
                      First seen: 14 May 2026
                      Frontier AI Cyber Capability
                      The ability of the most advanced AI models to perform sophisticated offensive security tasks, such as finding vulnerabilities or writing exploits, at a speed and scale beyond human capability.
                      FinServ context: UK regulators have warned that frontier AI models amplify the threat to financial firms' safety and soundness, meaning firms that underinvest in cyber controls face disproportionately greater risk as these models become more widely accessible.
                      First seen: 02 June 2026
                      Frontier LLM
                      A cutting-edge large language model at the leading edge of AI capability, typically developed by major AI research organisations and offering the most advanced performance currently available.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms evaluating AI adoption must assess the additional security risks posed by frontier LLMs, which may have greater capability to assist attackers in crafting sophisticated fraud or social engineering attacks.
                      First seen: 04 May 2026
                      G
                      Gateway-Side Tool Access
                      The ability to invoke tools or execute functions on the central gateway component of a networked system, rather than being restricted to actions on the local or paired node.
                      FinServ context: In CVE-2026-41378, nodes with the role of "node" were incorrectly granted unrestricted gateway-side tool access, meaning a compromised credential at a peripheral node could give an attacker full control of the central gateway in a financial services platform.
                      First seen: 29 April 2026
                      Generate Malicious Commands
                      An attack technique where an adversary uses a large language model to produce harmful system commands or scripts that can be executed on a target machine.
                      FinServ context: A threat actor could use an open-source LLM hosted on internal financial services infrastructure to generate tailored attack scripts targeting banking systems or payment platforms.
                      First seen: 11 May 2026
                      Generative AI Models
                      AI systems that can create new content like text, images, or code based on prompts and training data.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions use generative AI for customer service chatbots and document analysis, but attackers can also use these models to create sophisticated phishing emails or malicious code targeting banking systems.
                      First seen: 01 April 2026
                      Genetics Data Breach
                      An unauthorised disclosure of DNA or biological profile information collected by testing or healthcare services.
                      FinServ context: Unlike payment card data, genetic data cannot be reissued, making it permanently exploitable for identity fraud or insurance manipulation, a risk financial services firms underwriting life products must account for.
                      First seen: 15 June 2026
                      Ghost CMS
                      A content management system (an open-source platform for building websites and blogs) that, like WordPress, can run on a server and has been found to contain exploitable security flaws.
                      FinServ context: Financial firms using Ghost CMS for public-facing content or news portals may be exposed to SQL injection attacks that allow attackers to inject malicious JavaScript, potentially compromising customer-facing pages or redirecting users to fraud sites.
                      First seen: 25 May 2026
                      Gogs
                      A lightweight, self-hosted Git service that allows organisations to run their own private source code repository on their own servers rather than using cloud services like GitHub.
                      FinServ context: Financial firms using self-hosted Gogs to store proprietary trading algorithms or compliance tooling could be exposed to remote code execution if the service is internet-facing and unpatched.
                      First seen: 29 May 2026
                      Golang
                      A programming language developed by Google that is known for its simplicity, efficiency, and strong support for concurrent programming.
                      FinServ context: Financial malware like EKANS ransomware is increasingly written in Golang because it can easily target multiple operating systems and is harder for traditional security tools to analyse.
                      First seen: 25 March 2026
                      Groovy Evaluator
                      A component that executes Groovy programming language code dynamically at runtime, often used for scripting and automation.
                      FinServ context: Compromised Groovy evaluators in financial workflow systems could allow attackers to inject malicious scripts that alter payment processing rules or extract sensitive customer data.
                      First seen: 08 April 2026
                      GroovyEvaluator
                      A component in PowerJob that executes Groovy programming language code but lacks proper input validation, making it vulnerable to code injection.
                      FinServ context: In financial systems using PowerJob, attackers could exploit GroovyEvaluator to execute malicious code and potentially access sensitive financial data or trading systems.
                      First seen: 08 April 2026
                      Guard Bypass (Agentic Gateway)
                      A weakness where an AI agent's protective controls — such as sandbox policies, authentication settings, or network restrictions — can be circumvented by a prompt-injected model accessing privileged configuration endpoints.
                      FinServ context: If an AI agent used in a financial services workflow has its gateway guard bypassed, an attacker could silently disable SSRF protections or TLS requirements, exposing internal banking APIs to unauthorised access.
                      First seen: 12 May 2026
                      Guardrails (AI)
                      Built-in safety controls and behavioural boundaries applied to an AI model to prevent it from producing harmful, illegal, or policy-violating outputs.
                      FinServ context: A UK bank deploying a customer-facing AI assistant must configure guardrails to prevent the model from giving unauthorised financial advice or disclosing sensitive account information.
                      First seen: 01 May 2026
                      H
                      hard-coded kill-list
                      A pre-programmed list of specific software processes that malware will automatically terminate when it runs.
                      FinServ context: Ransomware targeting financial services often includes kill-lists to stop security software, backup systems, or trading platforms before encrypting critical data.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Hardcoded Credential
                      A password or secret that is permanently written into software code rather than being stored securely and changeable, meaning it cannot be easily updated if discovered.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, a hardcoded credential in a banking application or storage system could allow an attacker to bypass authentication entirely and access protected customer data or transaction records.
                      First seen: 23 June 2026
                      Header Block Terminator
                      A specific sequence of characters that signals the end of HTTP headers in web communications.
                      FinServ context: Manipulating these terminators could allow attackers to smuggle malicious requests past security controls protecting financial web services.
                      First seen: 28 March 2026
                      Heap-based Buffer Overflow
                      A vulnerability where a program writes more data to a memory buffer than it can hold, potentially allowing attackers to execute malicious code.
                      FinServ context: This could compromise financial document processing systems, allowing attackers to execute code when employees open malicious client files or financial reports.
                      First seen: 15 April 2026
                      High-Autonomy Agent
                      An AI agent that can take actions, make decisions, and interact with systems with minimal or no human approval at each step.
                      FinServ context: A high-autonomy agent deployed in a financial firm's operations — for example to execute trades or process payments — could cause significant harm if it is manipulated through prompt injection or misconfigured with excessive permissions.
                      First seen: 04 June 2026
                      Hook Token
                      A short-lived credential that authorises a specific automated trigger (a "hook") to call back into a system and perform a defined action.
                      FinServ context: In financial services platforms, a compromised hook token could allow an attacker to escalate privileges within an AI agent framework, as seen in CVE-2026-53814 where hook tokens granted unintended owner-level access to restricted tools.
                      First seen: 12 June 2026
                      Host Environment Sanitizer
                      A security control that checks and cleans environment variables passed to a system to prevent malicious values from influencing how programs run.
                      FinServ context: A bypass of the host environment sanitizer (CVE-2026-53864) in an AI agent platform used by a financial firm could allow an attacker with access to configuration files to manipulate child processes running sensitive workloads.
                      First seen: 17 June 2026
                      Hostile State Threat Actor
                      A government or state-sponsored group that conducts cyberattacks against foreign organisations or infrastructure as part of geopolitical, espionage or sabotage objectives.
                      FinServ context: UK financial institutions must account for hostile state threat actors in their threat models, as these groups have the resources and motivation to target payment systems, trading platforms and financial data at scale.
                      First seen: 18 June 2026
                      Housing Wealth (Retirement Planning Context)
                      The financial value stored in a person's property that can be unlocked in later life through products such as equity release or retirement interest-only mortgages.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's later life lending agenda means firms offering equity release or lifetime mortgages must apply Consumer Duty standards to ensure vulnerable older customers understand the risks of using housing wealth to fund retirement.
                      First seen: 18 June 2026
                      Human Vulnerability (Social Engineering Context)
                      An attack where malicious instructions are hidden inside external content that an AI agent reads, causing the agent to carry out the attacker's commands without the user's knowledge.
                      FinServ context: An AI agent used by a bank to summarise customer documents could be hijacked if a malicious prompt is embedded in a webpage or file the agent is instructed to process.
                      First seen: 15 June 2026
                      I
                      IAM (Identity and Access Management)
                      The framework of policies, processes, and technologies used to control who can access what within an organisation's systems.
                      FinServ context: As AI agents multiply across financial firms, each agent requires its own identity and access controls — stretching traditional IAM budgets and practices that were designed for human users.
                      First seen: 04 June 2026
                      IAS Zone Cluster (Zigbee)
                      A Zigbee smart device security feature that manages intrusion alarm system sensors, using a state table to track enrolled devices on a wireless network.
                      FinServ context: CVE-2026-47150 shows that even IoT security protocols used in physical access control at financial services premises can contain out-of-bounds write vulnerabilities triggerable by malformed messages from already-joined devices.
                      First seen: 26 June 2026
                      ICO (Information Commissioner's Office)
                      The UK's independent data protection regulator, responsible for enforcing laws governing how organisations collect, store, and use personal data, and empowered to issue fines for breaches.
                      FinServ context: UK financial services firms that suffer ransomware attacks resulting in customer data being published can face significant ICO fines on top of FCA regulatory action, as seen in the South Staffordshire Water case.
                      First seen: 12 May 2026
                      ICS Software Platforms
                      Industrial Control Systems software used to monitor and control manufacturing and operational technology environments.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms with trading floors or data centers may run ICS software that could be targeted by ransomware with specific kill-lists.
                      First seen: 27 March 2026
                      Identity Abuse
                      The misuse of stolen, forged, or manipulated user identity information to gain unauthorised access to systems or perform actions on behalf of a legitimate user.
                      FinServ context: Identity abuse is a growing attack vector in financial services, where compromising a privileged identity can give an attacker access to payment systems, customer data, or regulatory reporting tools.
                      First seen: 20 May 2026
                      Identity and Access Management (IAM)
                      The set of policies, processes, and technologies used to ensure that the right people and systems have the right level of access to the right resources at the right time.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, IAM controls which staff or automated systems can access core banking platforms, trading systems, or customer data, making it a foundational control for regulatory compliance and fraud prevention.
                      First seen: 25 May 2026
                      Identity Control Plane
                      A centralised security layer that treats identity — who or what is making a request — as the primary mechanism for enforcing access decisions across all systems.
                      FinServ context: In agentic AI deployments at banks, making identity the control plane means every AI agent, service account, and API call must be verified before accessing payment systems or customer records, replacing traditional network-perimeter controls.
                      First seen: 29 June 2026
                      Impersonate
                      To fraudulently assume another user's identity and permissions within a computer system.
                      FinServ context: Attackers could impersonate bank employees or customers to access sensitive financial data or authorize unauthorized transactions.
                      First seen: 30 March 2026
                      improper access control
                      A security flaw where systems fail to properly restrict what files, data, or functions users can access based on their permission levels.
                      FinServ context: Banking applications with improper access control might allow junior staff to access senior executive financial reports or customer data beyond their authorization level.
                      First seen: 08 April 2026
                      Improper Neutralization
                      A security weakness where an application fails to properly sanitize or validate user input before using it in commands, queries, or other operations.
                      FinServ context: A common cause of vulnerabilities in fintech applications where customer input data isn't properly cleaned before being processed by payment systems or database queries.
                      First seen: 03 April 2026
                      Indirect Prompt Injection
                      An attack where malicious instructions are hidden inside external content (such as a webpage or document) that an AI agent is asked to read, causing the agent to follow the attacker's instructions rather than the user's.
                      FinServ context: An AI assistant used by a wealth management firm to summarise client emails could be hijacked via indirect prompt injection embedded in a malicious attachment, causing it to silently exfiltrate account details.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      Industry-Wide Redress Scheme
                      A regulatory mechanism where a single structured framework is applied across an entire sector to compensate consumers who have been harmed, rather than handling cases individually.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's motor finance redress scheme is designed to ensure consistent and efficient compensation to consumers across all affected lenders, reducing legal uncertainty for firms and their investors.
                      First seen: 07 May 2026
                      Inference Engines
                      Software components that process AI models to generate predictions or responses from input data.
                      FinServ context: Compromised inference engines in financial AI systems could manipulate credit scoring, fraud detection, or algorithmic trading decisions.
                      First seen: 28 March 2026
                      Information Disclosure Vulnerability
                      A limit placed on how many API calls an AI agent or application is permitted to make within a given time period or cost threshold, used to control resource consumption and costs.
                      FinServ context: An adversary could use prompt injection to instruct an AI agent deployed at a financial firm to exhaust its API budget through unnecessary queries, disrupting legitimate fraud detection or customer service operations.
                      First seen: 23 June 2026
                      Information Repositories
                      Systems designed to store and share information between users, such as document libraries, wikis, or collaboration platforms.
                      FinServ context: Attackers may target financial firms' information repositories like SharePoint or Confluence to steal sensitive documents containing regulatory reports or customer data.
                      First seen: 23 March 2026
                      Infostealer
                      A type of malicious software designed specifically to silently collect and send sensitive information — such as passwords, session cookies, and financial credentials — from a victim's device to an attacker.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, infostealers targeting bank staff or customers can harvest authentication tokens and account credentials, enabling account takeover and fraudulent transactions.
                      First seen: 21 May 2026
                      Initial Access
                      The first step an attacker takes to gain a foothold inside a target organisation's systems, such as exploiting a vulnerability, using stolen credentials, or tricking a user.
                      FinServ context: Verizon's 2026 DBIR reports that exploits now account for 31% of initial access events in breaches, meaning financial firms with unpatched systems are increasingly likely to be the entry point for a full attack chain.
                      First seen: 21 May 2026
                      Insecure Default File Permissions
                      A security weakness where software creates files with access controls that are too permissive, allowing unintended users or processes to read or modify them.
                      FinServ context: An AI SDK storing memory or session data with insecure file permissions in a financial services environment could expose sensitive customer interaction logs to other users on the same system.
                      First seen: 16 April 2026
                      Insecure Deserialization
                      A vulnerability where applications unsafely convert serialized data back into objects, potentially allowing attackers to execute malicious code.
                      FinServ context: Trading platforms that serialize market data or user sessions are vulnerable to attackers injecting malicious payloads that could manipulate trades or steal credentials.
                      First seen: 30 March 2026
                      Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
                      A vulnerability where an application exposes internal object references (like database keys or file names) that attackers can manipulate to access unauthorized data.
                      FinServ context: In payment systems, IDOR vulnerabilities could allow attackers to access other customers' transaction records or complete purchases they haven't paid for by manipulating order IDs.
                      First seen: 30 March 2026
                      Insider Trading Controls (Crypto Market Integrity)
                      Rules and technical controls designed to prevent individuals from trading cryptoassets based on non-
                      Installation Credentials
                      Authentication information used to set up or configure software applications and their connections to other systems.
                      FinServ context: Compromised installation credentials for banking software could allow attackers to reconfigure fraud detection systems or payment gateways.
                      First seen: 30 March 2026
                      Integer Overflow
                      A programming error that occurs when an arithmetic operation produces a result too large to be stored in the allocated memory space.
                      FinServ context: Integer overflow vulnerabilities in network protocols used by financial systems can be exploited to crash trading platforms or payment processing services.
                      First seen: 02 April 2026
                      Integer Underflow
                      A programming error that occurs when a calculation produces a number smaller than the minimum value a variable can hold, causing it to wrap around to an unexpectedly large number and potentially corrupt memory.
                      FinServ context: An integer underflow in a payment processing library could cause a transaction amount or buffer size to be miscalculated, opening the door to memory corruption or denial-of-service attacks against banking infrastructure.
                      First seen: 01 May 2026
                      Integrated Application (Third-Party Integration)
                      An external software application granted permission to connect to and interact with a core platform such as a CRM or data system, sharing data between the two services.
                      FinServ context: The Salesforce breaches via integrated apps like Klue demonstrate that in financial services, granting third-party applications access to client data systems creates additional attack surface that must be independently assessed and monitored.
                      First seen: 25 June 2026
                      Internal-Address Blocklist
                      A security control that prevents a service from fetching or connecting to private, internal network addresses that should not be reachable from the internet.
                      FinServ context: A poorly implemented internal-address blocklist in a bank's web-crawling or data-fetching service could be bypassed to expose internal APIs, cloud metadata endpoints, or core banking system details.
                      First seen: 23 June 2026
                      Internet Key Exchange version 2 (IKEv2)
                      A protocol used to establish secure VPN connections by negotiating encryption keys and authentication methods between network devices.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions rely on IKEv2 for secure site-to-site connections between branches and data centres, making vulnerabilities in this protocol a critical concern for maintaining secure banking networks.
                      First seen: 25 March 2026
                      Investment Fraud (Organised Criminal Context)
                      A coordinated scheme, often run by professional criminal networks operating across multiple countries, that deceives victims into handing over money through fake or manipulated investment opportunities.
                      FinServ context: The FCA has highlighted that organised criminal groups are running investment fraud at scale against UK consumers, with average losses exceeding £25,000, making fraud detection and customer protection a core regulatory expectation for financial firms.
                      First seen: 18 May 2026
                      IOCTL
                      A pattern where a single request triggers multiple simultaneous database or API queries, often exponentially increasing system load.
                      FinServ context: Attackers could exploit AI-powered financial advisory systems to generate excessive database queries, overwhelming trading platforms or customer data systems.
                      First seen: 07 April 2026
                      IPv6 Extension Header
                      Optional data fields that can be added to an IPv6 network packet to carry extra routing or processing instructions beyond what the main packet header contains.
                      FinServ context: Attackers can craft malicious IPv6 packets with mismatched extension header sizes to trigger memory vulnerabilities in security software protecting financial network infrastructure.
                      First seen: 08 June 2026
                      IPv6-Mapped IPv4 Address
                      A way of representing an older IPv4 network address using the newer IPv6 format, which some security controls fail to recognise as the same address and therefore do not block correctly.
                      FinServ context: An attacker could use IPv6-mapped IPv4 addresses to bypass a financial firm's internal network blocklist and reach sensitive cloud infrastructure or internal payment systems that should never be publicly accessible.
                      First seen: 23 June 2026
                      ISA (Individual Savings Account)
                      A UK government-approved savings or investment account that allows individuals to grow money free of UK income tax and capital gains tax.
                      FinServ context: Financial advisers and robo-advice platforms must ensure AI-driven recommendation tools correctly distinguish between ISA types when assessing retirement planning suitability for customers.
                      First seen: 19 June 2026
                      Ivanti Sentry
                      A secure mobile gateway product made by Ivanti that controls and protects mobile devices accessing an organisation's internal systems and email.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms using Ivanti Sentry to give traders or advisers mobile access to internal platforms must treat root-level RCE vulnerabilities as critical and patch immediately.
                      First seen: 10 June 2026
                      J
                      Joint Regulatory Taskforce
                      A coordinated group formed by multiple UK regulators working together to identify and act on a specific harm, such as misleading financial promotions, rather than each regulator acting independently.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's joint regulatory taskforce on car finance advertising demonstrates how regulators are increasingly coordinating to tackle harms that span claims management, legal services, and financial promotion rules simultaneously.
                      First seen: 16 June 2026
                      JPEG driver
                      A situation where a single query triggers multiple additional queries to be executed, often exponentially increasing the computational load.
                      FinServ context: Attackers may exploit AI agents in financial systems to perform excessive query fan-outs, driving up cloud costs and potentially causing service disruptions during peak trading hours.
                      First seen: 08 April 2026
                      jsonpickle
                      A Python library that converts Python objects to and from JSON format, including the ability to reconstruct complex objects — which can be dangerous if the input is not trusted.
                      FinServ context: In financial services platforms using Python-based analytics or SIEM tools, unsafe use of jsonpickle can allow attackers to craft JSON payloads that execute arbitrary code on the server.
                      First seen: 11 June 2026
                      Juniper Networks Apstra
                      A network attack where an adversary intercepts and potentially alters communications between two parties who believe they are directly communicating with each other.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, these attacks could intercept trading communications or payment instructions between banks and their partners.
                      First seen: 10 April 2026
                      Jupyter Notebooks
                      Interactive coding documents widely used by data scientists to write, run, and share code, often containing configuration details and credentials.
                      FinServ context: Financial services AI teams using Jupyter notebooks for model development may inadvertently expose API keys or model configurations if those notebooks are stored insecurely or published publicly.
                      First seen: 15 April 2026
                      Just-in-Time Access
                      A security mechanism that grants users elevated permissions only when needed and for a limited time period, rather than permanent elevated access.
                      FinServ context: Banks implement just-in-time access so that employees only get administrative privileges to payment systems temporarily when required for specific tasks.
                      First seen: 22 March 2026
                      JWT (JSON Web Token)
                      A compact, digitally signed token used to verify a user's identity and pass claims between systems without needing to check a database on every request.
                      FinServ context: In financial services APIs, a JWT confirms who is making a request, but as the Mem0 vulnerability shows, verifying identity alone is not enough — the system must also check what role or permissions that identity holds.
                      First seen: 10 June 2026
                      K
                      Kaggle-MCP
                      A specific, pre-defined sequence of network packets that a backdoor listens for before activating, so it stays hidden until the attacker deliberately triggers it.
                      FinServ context: The cd00r backdoor uses a secret knock technique, meaning it could sit undetected on a bank's UNIX server for months, only revealing itself when the attacker sends the correct traffic sequence.
                      First seen: 28 April 2026
                      Kali365
                      A criminal phishing-as-a-service platform offered via Telegram that enables attackers to steal Microsoft 365 authentication tokens without needing to know how to build the attack infrastructure themselves.
                      FinServ context: UK financial firms relying on Microsoft 365 for daily operations are at risk from Kali365 attacks, where stolen OAuth tokens could give criminals persistent access to employee email and internal systems.
                      First seen: 26 May 2026
                      Key Exchange without Entity Authentication
                      A vulnerability where cryptographic key exchange occurs without properly verifying the identity of the communicating parties.
                      FinServ context: This flaw allows attackers to intercept SSH connections between financial network management systems and devices, potentially capturing banking credentials or transaction data.
                      First seen: 10 April 2026
                      Kill-list
                      A pre-configured list of specific processes or services that malware automatically terminates when it executes on a target system.
                      FinServ context: Banking trojans may use kill-lists to shut down security software or trading applications before deploying their payload.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Kirki Plugin (WordPress Privilege Escalation)
                      A popular WordPress customisation plugin that was found to contain a critical flaw allowing attackers to elevate their access rights and take over administrator accounts.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms that use WordPress for public-facing content or marketing portals must apply plugin patches promptly, as compromised admin accounts could be used to deface sites or harvest visitor data.
                      First seen: 03 June 2026
                      L
                      LangChain
                      An open-source framework used to build applications that connect large language models to external data sources, tools, and workflows.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms using LangChain to build AI-powered document analysis or customer service tools must patch promptly, as vulnerabilities in its prompt templates or file-loading functions can expose sensitive data.
                      First seen: 16 April 2026
                      Langflow
                      A visual programming framework that allows users to build AI applications and workflows through a drag-and-drop interface without writing code.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms using Langflow to create customer service chatbots or document processing workflows face code injection risks that could compromise sensitive customer data.
                      First seen: 25 March 2026
                      Large Language Models (LLMs)
                      AI systems trained on vast amounts of text data that can understand and generate human-like text responses.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions using LLMs for customer service chatbots face risks from prompt injection attacks that could manipulate the AI to reveal sensitive customer account information.
                      First seen: 25 March 2026
                      Later Life Lending
                      A category of mortgage and lending products designed specifically for older borrowers who wish to release equity from their home or borrow into retirement.
                      FinServ context: The FCA is focusing on later life lending as a regulated product area where AI-driven affordability assessments must be carefully governed to avoid unfair outcomes for vulnerable older customers.
                      First seen: 16 June 2026
                      Lateral Movement
                      A technique used by attackers to progressively move through a network or set of connected systems after initial access, seeking higher privileges or more valuable data.
                      FinServ context: Stolen OAuth tokens are particularly dangerous in financial services cloud environments because they enable lateral movement between connected systems such as data platforms, AI tools and customer databases without triggering password-based alerts.
                      First seen: 21 April 2026
                      Lazarus Group
                      A highly sophisticated, North Korea state-sponsored threat actor known for conducting financially motivated cyberattacks, including bank heists and cryptocurrency theft, as well as espionage operations.
                      FinServ context: Lazarus has a long history of targeting financial institutions and cryptocurrency platforms, making it a direct threat to UK financial services firms and their supply chains.
                      First seen: 25 April 2026
                      LD_PRELOAD
                      A Linux environment variable that forces the operating system to load a specified shared library before all others when launching a program, which attackers can abuse to inject malicious code into a process.
                      FinServ context: An attacker exploiting an environment variable injection flaw in an AI agent's MCP server could use LD_PRELOAD to intercept cryptographic calls or credential lookups within a financial services application.
                      First seen: 12 May 2026
                      Learning Management System (LMS)
                      A software platform used by organisations to deliver, track, and manage training and educational content for staff or customers.
                      FinServ context: Financial firms use LMS platforms to deliver mandatory regulatory training, making them an attractive target for attackers who can exploit vulnerabilities to gain a foothold in corporate networks.
                      First seen: 27 May 2026
                      Lifecycle Policies
                      Automated rules that manage how long data is stored in cloud storage before being moved, archived, or deleted.
                      FinServ context: Attackers with access to a bank's cloud storage could modify lifecycle policies to immediately delete all customer records and transaction histories.
                      First seen: 22 March 2026
                      Lifecycle-Triggered Deletion
                      A telecommunications fraud where attackers use a victim's messaging system to send large volumes of SMS messages to premium numbers they control to generate revenue.
                      FinServ context: Attackers could exploit a bank's customer verification system that sends SMS codes, flooding premium rate numbers to generate fraudulent telecom revenue while disrupting legitimate customer authentication.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Listing Authority Advisory Panel (LAAP)
                      A statutory advisory body that provides independent input to the FCA in its role as the UK's official listing authority, representing the interests of issuers and others involved in capital markets.
                      FinServ context: For junior engineers at firms involved in capital markets or listings, understanding the LAAP helps contextualise regulatory policy changes that may drive new compliance or technical reporting requirements.
                      First seen: 01 July 2026
                      LiteLLM
                      An open-source software library that acts as a unified interface, allowing developers to connect applications to many different AI language model providers using a single, consistent set of commands.
                      FinServ context: A financial services firm using LiteLLM as a proxy to manage access to multiple AI models must ensure role-based access controls are properly enforced, as misconfigurations can allow internal users to escalate their own privileges to full administrator level.
                      First seen: 22 May 2026
                      LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin
                      A software plugin used within the cPanel web hosting control panel to improve website performance, which has been found to contain a security vulnerability actively being exploited by attackers.
                      FinServ context: Financial firms or their third-party hosting providers using cPanel with the LiteSpeed plugin must prioritise patching this flaw immediately, as CISA has given US government agencies only three days to remediate it.
                      First seen: 16 June 2026
                      LLM Jailbreak
                      A technique where an attacker tricks an AI language model into ignoring its safety rules and producing content or taking actions it was designed to prevent.
                      FinServ context: A jailbroken LLM deployed in a financial services chatbot could be manipulated into revealing customer data, generating fraudulent documents, or bypassing compliance guardrails.
                      First seen: 01 May 2026
                      LLM Trace Provider
                      An external service that receives and records the inputs, outputs, and reasoning steps of a large language model for monitoring or debugging purposes.
                      FinServ context: An attacker redirecting a bank's AI application traffic to an attacker-controlled LLM trace provider could silently harvest customer queries and sensitive financial data.
                      First seen: 19 May 2026
                      Local Privilege Escalation
                      An attack where a user who already has limited access to a system exploits a vulnerability to gain administrator or root-level control without authorisation.
                      FinServ context: An attacker who has compromised a low-privilege service account on a financial firm's Linux server could use a local privilege escalation flaw to access sensitive trading data or cryptographic key stores.
                      First seen: 01 May 2026
                      Loopback MCP Owner Context
                      Files or data stores that an AI agent uses to retain information about past interactions, context, or instructions across sessions.
                      FinServ context: In financial services AI deployments, compromised memory files could allow an attacker to persistently alter an agent's behaviour — for example, manipulating a customer-facing advisory agent to provide false information or leak sensitive data.
                      First seen: 07 May 2026
                      LuCI
                      A web-based configuration interface used on embedded networking devices running the OpenWrt operating system.
                      FinServ context: Command injection vulnerabilities in network device management interfaces like LuCI are relevant to financial firms managing branch office or ATM network infrastructure.
                      First seen: 30 June 2026
                      M
                      Machine-in-the-Middle Attack
                      Bootstrap Protocol, an older network protocol used by devices to automatically obtain their IP address and other network configuration when they start up.
                      FinServ context: BOOTP vulnerabilities in financial network infrastructure could allow attackers to disrupt trading floor systems or branch office connectivity by causing network configuration chaos.
                      First seen: 25 March 2026
                      Macros (Malicious)
                      Small automated scripts embedded in documents like Word or Excel files that run code automatically when the file is opened.
                      FinServ context: Threat groups have used malicious macros in email attachments to deliver backdoors targeting financial sector employees, making macro-enabled documents a key concern for FinServ security teams.
                      First seen: 15 April 2026
                      Malicious Pull Request
                      A contribution submitted to a shared code repository that appears legitimate but contains hidden malicious code designed to compromise the software build or development pipeline.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms that use open-source components or shared developer workflows are at risk if malicious pull requests enter their software supply chain, potentially introducing vulnerabilities into production trading or payment systems.
                      First seen: 25 June 2026
                      Malicious Repository
                      A source code repository — such as one hosted on GitHub — that has been deliberately crafted or compromised to contain harmful instructions or code that executes when accessed by a developer tool.
                      FinServ context: A developer at a financial services firm cloning what appears to be a legitimate open source repository could unknowingly trigger code execution in their AI-assisted coding tool, exposing internal credentials or source code.
                      First seen: 13 May 2026
                      Managed File Transfer (MFT)
                      A software platform that provides secure, auditable transfer of files between internal systems or external partners, with controls beyond basic FTP or email.
                      FinServ context: UK financial services firms rely on MFT platforms to exchange settlement files, regulatory reports, and client statements, so vulnerabilities in these platforms can directly expose regulated data.
                      First seen: 05 May 2026
                      Market Manipulation (Crypto Context)
                      The deliberate act of artificially influencing the price or trading activity of an asset in order to deceive other market participants.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's new crypto regulatory framework explicitly introduces market integrity rules covering insider trading and market manipulation, meaning crypto firms must now implement surveillance controls similar to those required of traditional securities firms.
                      First seen: 30 June 2026
                      Market Transparency Rules
                      Regulatory requirements obliging financial market participants to publicly report trade prices and volumes within a set timeframe, so all investors have access to fair and consistent market information.
                      FinServ context: The UK's updated bond market transparency rules, which came into force in December 2025, increased the share of corporate bond trades reported in real time from under 5% to over 75%, directly improving price fairness for investors.
                      First seen: 22 June 2026
                      matlab-mcp-server
                      An open-source Model Context Protocol server that exposes MATLAB code generation and execution capabilities as tools that AI agents can call remotely.
                      FinServ context: CVE-2026-7272 demonstrated that a path traversal flaw in this MCP server's script execution function could be exploited remotely, posing a risk to any financial firm using AI-assisted data analysis workflows built on MATLAB.
                      First seen: 29 April 2026
                      MCP API Call Endpoint
                      A network-accessible interface that allows an AI model or agent to invoke external tools and services using the Model Context Protocol.
                      FinServ context: If an MCP endpoint lacks proper input validation, an attacker could craft a malicious server URL to trigger server-side request forgery, potentially accessing internal financial systems from within the firm's own infrastructure.
                      First seen: 02 June 2026
                      MCP Server (Model Context Protocol Server)
                      A server that provides tools and data sources to an AI agent, extending what the agent can do by giving it the ability to call external services or read external content.
                      FinServ context: If a financial services AI assistant connects to an MCP server that scrapes external content, an attacker could embed malicious instructions in that content to hijack the agent's actions and exfiltrate credentials.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      MCP Server (Oracle Context)
                      A server component implementing the Model Context Protocol that allows AI tools to interact with external data sources or services, in this case an Oracle helper tool that processes SQL.
                      FinServ context: A CVSS 8.7 flaw in Oracle's MCP Server Helper Tool allows an unauthenticated attacker to cause the server to execute malicious SQL, posing a direct data integrity risk to financial institutions running Oracle banking or analytics platforms.
                      First seen: 05 May 2026
                      MD5 Hash
                      A mathematical function that converts data into a fixed-length string of characters, historically used to verify data integrity, but now considered cryptographically weak because it can be reverse-engineered.
                      FinServ context: Relying on MD5 hashes for password reset or authentication logic in financial services applications is dangerous, as attackers can exploit predictable hash outputs to bypass security controls, as demonstrated in the Ultimate Member CVE.
                      First seen: 24 June 2026
                      Medusa Ransomware
                      A ransomware-as-a-service strain operated by a financially motivated cybercrime group that encrypts victim data and demands payment, often targeting organisations at high speed using known vulnerabilities.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms are prime targets for Medusa campaigns because of their ability to pay ransoms and the operational impact of encrypted transaction or customer data systems.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      MegaCortex
                      A ransomware variant that uses hardcoded lists of processes to terminate before encrypting systems.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions need to understand ransomware variants like MegaCortex that may specifically target trading or banking applications.
                      First seen: 27 March 2026
                      Mem0
                      An open-source memory layer for AI applications that stores and retrieves context across conversations, allowing AI agents to remember information between sessions.
                      FinServ context: A missing authorisation flaw in Mem0 could allow any authenticated user to redirect an AI agent's LLM traffic to an attacker-controlled server, a serious risk for financial firms using AI agents to handle customer or trading data.
                      First seen: 10 June 2026
                      Memory Corruption
                      A programming error where a program incorrectly modifies memory locations, potentially allowing attackers to control program execution.
                      FinServ context: Memory corruption in high-frequency trading systems could be exploited to manipulate trade orders or leak proprietary trading strategies to competitors.
                      First seen: 07 April 2026
                      memory exhaustion
                      An attack technique that forces a system to use up all available memory, causing applications to crash or become unresponsive.
                      FinServ context: Memory exhaustion attacks against financial trading systems could disrupt market operations or prevent customers from accessing their accounts.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Memory Leak
                      A software bug where a program gradually uses up more and more memory over time without properly releasing it back to the system.
                      FinServ context: Memory leaks in financial trading systems or payment processors can cause critical services to slow down or crash during peak transaction periods, potentially resulting in significant financial losses.
                      First seen: 25 March 2026
                      Mesh Router
                      A type of networking device that uses multiple interconnected nodes to extend wireless coverage across a large area.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions operating across large office campuses may use mesh routers for network connectivity; unpatched zero-day vulnerabilities in such devices could allow attackers to gain an initial foothold on corporate networks.
                      First seen: 04 June 2026
                      MetaGPT
                      An AI framework that assigns different roles to GPT models to collaborate on complex tasks like software development and project management.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions might use MetaGPT-like tools for automated report generation or regulatory compliance documentation, making command injection vulnerabilities particularly dangerous.
                      First seen: 10 April 2026
                      MFA Fatigue
                      An attack technique where a criminal repeatedly triggers multi-factor authentication requests to a victim's device until the victim accidentally or frustratedly approves one.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, MFA fatigue attacks have been used to compromise bank employee accounts, bypassing strong authentication controls that protect access to core banking systems.
                      First seen: 18 June 2026
                      Microsoft Copilot
                      An AI assistant integrated into Microsoft 365 products that can read, summarise, and act on documents, emails, and data within an organisation's environment.
                      FinServ context: At a financial services firm, a compromised or manipulated Copilot instance could be used to silently exfiltrate trade data, customer records, or internal reports through prompt injection attacks.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      Microsoft Defender Privilege Escalation
                      A class of vulnerability in Microsoft's built-in antivirus and endpoint protection tool that allows a low-privileged attacker to gain higher system rights than they should have.
                      FinServ context: Ransomware gangs exploiting Defender privilege escalation vulnerabilities can elevate from a standard user account to SYSTEM-level access on a bank's endpoint, disabling defences before deploying ransomware.
                      First seen: 30 June 2026
                      Mirai Botnet
                      A type of malware that infects internet-connected devices, particularly routers and IoT devices, and recruits them into a network used to launch large-scale attacks.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms with internet-exposed network devices must ensure end-of-life hardware is replaced promptly, as Mirai campaigns actively target unpatched routers to build attack infrastructure that can be directed at financial sector targets.
                      First seen: 23 April 2026
                      Misleading Financial Promotion
                      An advertisement or communication about a financial product or service that creates a false or misleading impression, for example by appearing to be independent advice when it is actually a paid promotion.
                      FinServ context: The FCA has warned consumers about CMC adverts on social media that mimic impartial money tips content but are actually paid promotions, breaching financial promotion rules that apply to all FCA-regulated firms.
                      First seen: 09 June 2026
                      Missing Authorization
                      A vulnerability where an application fails to check whether the user making a request actually has permission to perform that action.
                      FinServ context: A missing authorization flaw in a financial firm's customer portal could allow a low-privileged user to access or modify account data belonging to other customers, breaching FCA data protection obligations.
                      First seen: 20 May 2026
                      MITM Attacker
                      An adversary who positions themselves between two communicating parties to intercept, monitor, or alter their communications.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, MITM attackers can intercept payment instructions or manipulate trading data flowing between banking applications and servers.
                      First seen: 10 April 2026
                      Model Context Protocol
                      A standardized way for AI agents to access and interact with external tools and data sources.
                      FinServ context: Financial AI systems using Model Context Protocol could be compromised to give attackers access to trading systems, customer databases, or risk management tools.
                      First seen: 25 March 2026
                      Model Context Protocol (MCP) Tool Poisoning
                      An attack where a malicious instruction is hidden inside content that an AI coding assistant retrieves via an MCP server, causing the assistant to execute harmful commands on the user's machine without the user realising.
                      FinServ context: A developer at a financial firm using an AI coding assistant like Cursor could inadvertently expose internal API keys or agent configuration files if the assistant is tricked into scraping an attacker-controlled webpage.
                      First seen: 09 June 2026
                      Model Weights
                      The internal numerical parameters of a machine learning model that are produced during training and define how the model processes inputs and generates outputs.
                      FinServ context: An attacker who gains access to a financial firm's proprietary AI model weights could reverse-engineer the model's decision logic, exposing credit scoring algorithms or fraud detection strategies to manipulation.
                      First seen: 01 May 2026
                      Modoboa
                      An open-source email hosting platform used to manage email domains and user accounts.
                      FinServ context: A vulnerability in Modoboa's password reset endpoint allowed domain administrators to take over superadmin accounts, illustrating how admin-level privilege misuse can cascade to full system compromise in platforms used by financial services firms.
                      First seen: 30 June 2026
                      Motor Finance Redress Scheme
                      A formal industry-wide compensation programme mandated by a regulator to reimburse customers who suffered financial harm due to undisclosed discretionary commission arrangements on car finance products.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's motor finance redress scheme requires affected firms to implement robust customer identification and payment processes, creating significant operational and data management challenges for compliance
                      MOVEit Automation
                      An enterprise software product that automates the secure transfer of files between systems and organisations.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms use managed file transfer tools like MOVEit to exchange sensitive customer or payment data with counterparties, making authentication bypass vulnerabilities in these products a critical risk.
                      First seen: 05 May 2026
                      MuddyWater
                      An Iranian state-sponsored advanced persistent threat group linked to Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security, known for using commercially available tools to conduct espionage and disruptive attacks.
                      FinServ context: State-sponsored groups like MuddyWater increasingly use ransomware as a smokescreen to disguise intelligence-gathering operations that could target financial institutions holding sensitive economic data.
                      First seen: 08 May 2026
                      Multi-Tenant System
                      A software architecture where a single application serves multiple separate customer organisations, with each organisation's data kept logically isolated from the others.
                      FinServ context: Core banking and RegTech platforms are commonly multi-tenant, meaning a privilege escalation or authorisation bypass could expose one firm's data to another tenant.
                      First seen: 19 May 2026
                      Multipart Upload Handler
                      A server-side component that receives files sent in multiple separate chunks over HTTP, reassembling them into a complete file on the server.
                      FinServ context: Poorly validated multipart upload handlers in financial portals (for example, document submission for mortgage applications) are a common entry point for path traversal and remote code execution attacks.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      N
                      n-day Vulnerability
                      A security flaw that has already been publicly disclosed and has a patch available, but which attackers continue to exploit against systems that have not yet applied the fix.
                      FinServ context: Storm-1175's ransomware campaigns exploiting n-day vulnerabilities highlight that UK financial institutions with slow patching cycles remain exposed even after a vulnerability is publicly known and fixed.
                      First seen: 18 April 2026
                      Name Constraints (X.509/TLS)
                      Rules embedded in a digital certificate that restrict which domain names or IP addresses a Certificate Authority is allowed to vouch for.
                      FinServ context: A flaw that bypasses name constraints could allow an attacker to present a fraudulent certificate for a bank's domain, enabling man-in-the-middle interception of customer transactions.
                      First seen: 08 May 2026
                      NGINX
                      A widely used open-source web server and reverse proxy software that handles incoming web requests and forwards them to backend applications.
                      FinServ context: Many financial services firms use NGINX to front their customer-facing APIs and online banking portals, meaning a denial-of-service vulnerability in NGINX could disrupt customer access to critical services.
                      First seen: 15 May 2026
                      NIST NVD (National Vulnerability Database)
                      A US government database maintained by NIST that catalogues known software vulnerabilities, their severity scores, and remediation guidance, built on top of the CVE list.
                      FinServ context: Security engineers at UK financial firms use the NVD alongside CVE data to assess patch priority, particularly when NIST changes its scoring methodology to focus on the highest-impact vulnerabilities.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      NODE_OPTIONS
                      An environment variable used by the Node.js runtime that allows command-line flags to be passed automatically to every Node.js process, which attackers can abuse to load malicious scripts or enable dangerous runtime behaviours.
                      FinServ context: Malicious workspace configurations targeting AI coding tools used by financial services developers could abuse NODE_OPTIONS to silently load attacker-controlled modules during development or CI/CD pipeline execution.
                      First seen: 12 May 2026
                      Non-Human Identity (NHI)
                      A digital identity assigned to a machine, application, service account, or AI agent rather than to a human user, used to authenticate and authorise automated processes.
                      FinServ context: As financial services firms deploy AI agents to process transactions or access customer data, securing non-human identities becomes critical to prevent attackers from impersonating automated systems to move funds or exfiltrate data.
                      First seen: 29 June 2026
                      Non-Recursive String Replace
                      A sanitization method that only removes dangerous characters once, allowing attackers to bypass security by using nested malicious patterns.
                      FinServ context: Financial web applications using non-recursive sanitization could allow attackers to access sensitive customer data or payment information through crafted input sequences.
                      First seen: 14 April 2026
                      Nonce Validation
                      A security mechanism that checks whether a randomly generated number used once (nonce) is valid to prevent replay attacks.
                      FinServ context: Missing nonce validation in financial web applications can allow attackers to forge requests that trigger unauthorized transactions or modify payment settings.
                      First seen: 08 April 2026
                      Nopriv AJAX Handler
                      An attack where a malicious contributor submits a code change to an open source project that, when reviewed or merged, executes harmful code within the project's automated build pipeline.
                      FinServ context: The Cordyceps attack technique affecting projects like Google's AI Agent Development Kit highlights supply chain risk for financial firms that consume or contribute to open source software in their AI and data infrastructure.
                      First seen: 03 July 2026
                      npm Package Ecosystem
                      A large public registry of reusable JavaScript and Node.js software packages that developers download and include in their applications.
                      FinServ context: Financial services development teams that rely on npm packages for building customer-facing applications face supply chain risk if malicious code is injected into widely used packages such as those in the TanStack ecosystem.
                      First seen: 13 May 2026
                      Nudification Tools (AI)
                      AI systems that generate non-consensual intimate images by digitally removing clothing from photographs of real people.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms using AI-generated content or operating social platforms must ensure third-party AI tools they integrate cannot be repurposed for prohibited uses such as nudification, which is now subject to regulatory bans.
                      First seen: 08 May 2026
                      NVD Backlog
                      The growing queue of publicly known security vulnerabilities that have been reported but not yet analysed, scored, or published in the National Vulnerability Database.
                      FinServ context: A backlog of over 27,000 unprocessed vulnerabilities means security teams at financial firms cannot rely on NVD data alone to prioritise patching, increasing the risk of exploitable gaps remaining unaddressed.
                      First seen: 02 June 2026
                      O
                      OAuth Sprawl
                      The accumulation of too many third-party applications connected to your systems via OAuth tokens, creating excessive and hard-to-monitor access points that increase your attack surface.
                      FinServ context: A financial services firm using dozens of SaaS integrations may grant OAuth access to each one, and if any single integration is compromised, attackers can pivot into core banking or data systems.
                      First seen: 30 April 2026
                      OAuth Token
                      A small digital key issued by an authentication system that proves a user or application has been granted permission to access a resource, without needing to share a password.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, stolen OAuth tokens can allow attackers to move laterally between cloud services and AI development tools, as seen in the Vercel breach where an employee's AI tool access was compromised.
                      First seen: 21 April 2026
                      OAuth Token Theft
                      The act of stealing the short-lived digital credentials that applications use to access each other's data on a user's behalf, without needing the user's password.
                      FinServ context: In the LastPass/Klue incident, stolen OAuth tokens gave attackers access to a Salesforce environment, a scenario directly relevant to financial services firms that integrate CRM, data, and identity platforms using OAuth.
                      First seen: 24 June 2026
                      Object-Level Access Control
                      A security check that verifies whether a specific user is allowed to interact with a specific individual record or object, not just a general resource type.
                      FinServ context: Without object-level access controls on password reset APIs, a domain admin at a financial firm could reset a superadmin's credentials, bypassing the segregation of duties required under operational resilience frameworks.
                      First seen: 30 June 2026
                      Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation (OFSI)
                      The UK government body responsible for implementing and enforcing financial sanctions, including freezing assets belonging to designated individuals or entities.
                      FinServ context: Financial firms supervised by the FCA must maintain adequate systems and controls to comply with OFSI requirements, including screening customers and transactions against sanctions lists.
                      First seen: 05 June 2026
                      Office of Trade Sanctions Implementation (OTSI)
                      A UK government body responsible for implementing and enforcing trade sanctions, which restrict the movement of goods, services, and technology to or from designated countries or individuals.
                      FinServ context: Financial firms must maintain controls that satisfy both OTSI trade sanctions and OFSI financial sanctions requirements, as the FCA actively reviews these systems across the sector.
                      First seen: 08 June 2026
                      Ollama
                      An open-source framework that allows users to run large language models locally on their own infrastructure.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms may need to monitor for unauthorized local AI model deployments that could bypass corporate AI governance policies.
                      First seen: 27 March 2026
                      One-Time Password (OTP)
                      Integrated business management software that combines functions like finance, HR, supply chain, and operations into a single platform used across an organisation.
                      FinServ context: The Oracle ERP zero-day exploited by ShinyHunters shows that vulnerabilities in financial and administrative platforms used across large institutions can expose sensitive data at scale, making patch management for ERP systems a critical control.
                      First seen: 25 June 2026
                      Open Finance
                      A system that allows consumers and businesses to securely share their financial data with authorised third-party providers across a wide range of financial products.
                      FinServ context: UK financial services firms must prepare for FCA-regulated open finance frameworks that will require secure data-sharing APIs covering mortgages, pensions, savings, and investments.
                      First seen: 18 April 2026
                      Open Source AI Model Hosting
                      The practice of downloading publicly available AI models and running them on privately controlled infrastructure rather than using a commercial provider's API.
                      FinServ context: A threat actor could host an uncensored open source model locally using frameworks like Ollama to generate phishing content or malicious code targeting a UK bank's systems, with no provider safeguards in place.
                      First seen: 28 May 2026
                      Open Source Enterprise Resiliency Alliance (OSERA)
                      A vendor-neutral industry coalition formed to identify, fund, and secure the open source software components that financial institutions depend on in their technology infrastructure.
                      FinServ context: Because banks and fintechs share many of the same open source libraries, a single compromised component could affect the entire sector simultaneously, making collective resilience efforts like OSERA a financial-industry-specific supply chain defence.
                      First seen: 29 June 2026
                      Open Source Intelligence (OSINT)
                      The practice of collecting and analyzing publicly available information from sources like websites, social media, and databases for security purposes.
                      FinServ context: Banks use OSINT to identify threat actors researching their systems or to discover if their AI models or customer data have been exposed online.
                      First seen: 30 March 2026
                      Open Source Software Clearinghouse
                      A centralised repository or vetting platform where open source software components are assessed, catalogued, and cleared for safe use by enterprises before they are adopted.
                      FinServ context: IBM and Red Hat's planned clearinghouse, with major banks as early adopters, aims to reduce supply chain risk by giving financial institutions a trusted source for vetted open source dependencies.
                      First seen: 17 June 2026
                      Open-Source Package Compromise
                      An attack where a malicious actor inserts harmful code into a legitimate, publicly available software package used by developers.
                      FinServ context: Development teams at banks and fintechs who install open-source packages without verifying their integrity risk introducing malware directly into production systems handling sensitive financial data.
                      First seen: 04 June 2026
                      OpenBullet2
                      An open-source automation and credential-testing tool that has been abused by attackers to run credential stuffing campaigns, and which itself contains a remote code execution vulnerability in versions up to 0.3.2.
                      FinServ context: Fraud and threat intelligence teams at banks should monitor for OpenBullet2-based credential stuffing campaigns targeting customer login portals, while also ensuring internal deployments of the tool are patched.
                      First seen: 09 June 2026
                      OpenClaw
                      A cloud-based platform or application framework that manages agents, plugins, and sandboxed environments with scope-based access controls.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms using OpenClaw for automated trading agents or compliance monitoring would need to patch privilege escalation vulnerabilities that could allow unauthorized access to sensitive financial data.
                      First seen: 11 April 2026
                      Operational Resilience (Cloud Context)
                      A firm's ability to continue delivering critical services even when technology systems, including cloud infrastructure, experience disruption or attack.
                      FinServ context: UK financial institutions migrating to cloud must demonstrate to the PRA and FCA that their operational resilience controls can withstand cloud provider outages or cyber incidents affecting shared infrastructure.
                      First seen: 01 June 2026
                      Operational Resilience (Cyber Context)
                      A firm's ability to continue delivering its critical services to customers and markets even when it is suffering a cyber attack or technology failure.
                      FinServ context: The FCA, Bank of England, and Treasury jointly warned that frontier AI models amplify cyber threats in ways that could undermine a firm's operational resilience, making it a key regulatory focus for UK financial institutions.
                      First seen: 18 May 2026
                      OPSEC (Operational Security) Failure
                      A mistake made by an attacker or criminal group that accidentally reveals information about their identity, infrastructure, or methods to investigators or defenders.
                      FinServ context: When a ransomware gang targeting financial institutions makes an OPSEC failure — such as leaking internal chat logs or reusing identifiable infrastructure — it can expose the group's membership, tactics, and victim list to law enforcement and threat intelligence teams.
                      First seen: 14 May 2026
                      OPSEC Failure (Ransomware Context)
                      An attack approach where threat actors use AI tools to rapidly generate custom hacking tools or malicious code on demand during a live campaign, rather than preparing tools in advance.
