                      FinServ context: Vibe hacking lowers the technical barrier for attackers targeting financial services firms in emerging markets, enabling less skilled criminals to produce tailored attack tools against specific banking platforms or payment systems at speed.
                      First seen: 15 May 2026
                      Oracle Financial Services Analytical Applications Infrastructure (OFSAAI)
                      A software platform made by Oracle that financial services companies use to store, analyse, and report on large volumes of financial data across multiple business functions.
                      FinServ context: Three separate CVEs (CVSS 7.5) were disclosed against OFSAAI, meaning unauthenticated attackers could remotely access all data held within the platform — a critical risk for any bank or insurer running it.
                      First seen: 22 April 2026
                      Oracle Financial Services Customer Screening
                      An Oracle software product used by financial institutions to screen customers against watchlists, sanctions lists, and other regulatory checks during onboarding or transactions.
                      FinServ context: A CVSS 7.5 vulnerability in the User Interface component allowed unauthenticated remote attackers to read all customer screening data, which could expose personally identifiable information and sanctions-related records.
                      First seen: 22 April 2026
                      Oracle Financial Services Transaction Filtering
                      An Oracle software product that automatically checks financial transactions against regulatory rules, sanctions lists, and internal policies to block prohibited payments.
                      FinServ context: A CVSS 7.5 flaw in the User Interface allowed unauthenticated network access via HTTP, meaning an attacker could read transaction filtering data without any credentials — a serious compliance and data-protection risk.
                      First seen: 22 April 2026
                      Oracle Platform (Blockchain Context)
                      A service that acts as a trusted bridge between a blockchain network and real-world external data, feeding verified off-chain information into smart contracts that cannot access it directly.
                      FinServ context: In financial services blockchain applications, oracle platforms like Chainlink can be used to feed verified identity and compliance data into automated processes, enabling privacy-preserving customer verification without exposing raw personal data on-chain.
                      First seen: 22 May 2026
                      Oracle WebLogic Server
                      A widely-used enterprise Java application server made by Oracle, commonly used to host business-critical web applications and services.
                      FinServ context: Many UK financial institutions run Oracle WebLogic Server to host core banking and regulatory reporting applications, making unpatched vulnerabilities in it a high-priority risk for the CISO function.
                      First seen: 03 June 2026
                      Org Membership Check
                      An authorisation control that verifies whether a user belongs to the specific organisation whose data they are attempting to access or modify.
                      FinServ context: Financial services platforms hosting multiple client firms must enforce org membership checks to prevent one firm's employees from viewing or altering another firm's transaction or compliance data.
                      First seen: 22 June 2026
                      OS Command Injection
                      A vulnerability where an attacker can execute operating system commands on a server by manipulating input that gets passed to system command functions.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, OS command injection could allow attackers to execute commands on trading systems or payment processors, potentially accessing sensitive financial data or disrupting critical operations.
                      First seen: 07 April 2026
                      Out-of-Band (OOB) Security Update
                      An emergency software patch released outside a vendor's normal scheduled update cycle to fix a critical vulnerability that cannot wait for the next planned release.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms running ASP.NET Core applications must apply OOB patches immediately, as delayed patching of privilege escalation flaws could breach FCA operational resilience requirements.
                      First seen: 23 April 2026
                      Out-of-Band Security Update
                      Large integrated software platforms that manage core business processes such as finance, HR, and procurement across an organisation.
                      FinServ context: A zero-day vulnerability in Oracle's ERP software was exploited to steal significant volumes of data, demonstrating that core back-office financial systems are high-value targets for attackers.
                      First seen: 19 June 2026
                      Out-of-Bounds Read
                      A memory access vulnerability where a program reads data from memory locations beyond the allocated buffer boundaries.
                      FinServ context: Out-of-bounds read attacks against financial network infrastructure can lead to service disruption and potential disclosure of sensitive transaction data.
                      First seen: 02 April 2026
                      Out-of-Bounds Write
                      A software vulnerability where a program writes data outside the allocated memory region, which can corrupt data or allow an attacker to execute their own code.
                      FinServ context: An out-of-bounds write in a file-parsing component used by financial analysts could be exploited via a crafted document to compromise a trading workstation or analyst endpoint.
                      First seen: 27 May 2026
                      Out-of-Memory (OOM) Conditions
                      A system state where available memory is exhausted, typically causing applications to crash or become unresponsive.
                      FinServ context: High-frequency trading platforms could be targeted with large payload attacks designed to trigger OOM conditions and disrupt market operations.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Outlook Web Access (OWA)
                      A browser-based interface that allows users to access Microsoft Exchange email and calendar from any device without installing software.
                      FinServ context: OWA is widely used across UK financial services firms and is a high-value target because a successful XSS attack against it can compromise staff credentials or intercept sensitive client communications.
                      First seen: 11 June 2026
                      Output Escaping
                      When a single request triggers multiple additional database or API queries, potentially creating a cascading effect.
                      FinServ context: AI-powered trading systems can be manipulated to perform excessive query fan-outs that overwhelm market data APIs and exhaust trading platform budgets.
                      First seen: 17 April 2026
                      Overage Event
                      A recorded system event triggered when a user or organisation exceeds a pre-set usage or spending limit, typically used to trigger additional billing charges.
                      FinServ context: An attacker exploiting a misconfigured billing function in a financial services SaaS platform could insert fraudulent overage events to drain a competitor organisation's credit balance or generate illegitimate charges.
                      First seen: 22 June 2026
                      Owner-Scoped Authority
                      A method of calculating whether a borrower can repay a loan by considering income that fluctuates — such as freelance earnings or commission — rather than relying solely on a fixed salary.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's proposed mortgage rule changes aim to give lenders more flexibility to apply variable income affordability assessments, helping self-employed borrowers who are currently excluded by rigid automated underwriting models.
                      First seen: 12 June 2026
                      P
                      Packet Sequence Authentication
                      The practice of injecting malicious content into systems that accept public input, with the intent of affecting downstream AI processing.
                      FinServ context: Criminals might insert malicious prompts into loan application forms that later poison the AI models used for credit risk assessment at banks.
                      First seen: 10 April 2026
                      packet-capturing program
                      Software that intercepts and records network traffic passing through a system for monitoring or analysis purposes.
                      FinServ context: Attackers use packet-capturing backdoors in financial networks to secretly monitor sensitive communications like transaction data or customer information.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Paired Node Credentials
                      Authentication credentials issued to a specific networked node that has been formally registered and trusted by a gateway or central system, allowing it to send authorised requests.
                      FinServ context: CVE-2026-41378 showed that attackers holding paired node credentials in OpenClaw could abuse unrestricted agent request dispatch to escalate privileges and achieve remote code execution on the gateway — a serious risk in any financial platform using distributed agent architectures.
                      First seen: 29 April 2026
                      Pairing Requirements
                      Authentication mechanisms that require two systems or components to establish a secure connection through a mutual verification process.
                      FinServ context: Payment processing systems use pairing requirements to ensure only authorized terminals can connect to the backend, preventing rogue devices from processing fraudulent transactions.
                      First seen: 11 April 2026
                      PAN-OS
                      The operating system that runs on Palo Alto Networks firewall and network security appliances, which organisations use to control and inspect network traffic.
                      FinServ context: Many UK financial institutions rely on Palo Alto firewalls as a core perimeter control, so an unpatched RCE vulnerability in PAN-OS requires urgent prioritisation in vulnerability management processes.
                      First seen: 06 May 2026
                      PAN-OS User-ID Authentication Portal
                      A web-based component of Palo Alto Networks firewall software that handles user identity authentication and is exposed to network traffic.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions running Palo Alto firewalls must treat unpatched vulnerabilities in the User-ID portal as critical priorities, since exploitation can give attackers remote code execution on perimeter security devices.
                      First seen: 07 May 2026
                      Passkeys
                      A login method that replaces passwords by using a cryptographic key pair stored on your device, so you authenticate using biometrics or a PIN instead of a typed password.
                      FinServ context: UK financial services firms are increasingly adopting passkeys as a consumer authentication option to reduce account takeover risk and meet FCA expectations around robust customer identity verification.
                      First seen: 23 April 2026
                      Password Reset Token Verification
                      A security check that confirms a user possesses a unique, time-limited secret sent to their registered contact before allowing a password to be changed.
                      FinServ context: Absence of token verification in a financial portal's password reset flow could allow an attacker to take over customer or staff accounts without any interaction from the legitimate user.
                      First seen: 29 May 2026
                      Patch Tuesday
                      Microsoft's monthly scheduled release of security updates, issued on the second Tuesday of each month, fixing vulnerabilities across Windows and related products.
                      FinServ context: UK financial services firms must incorporate Patch Tuesday releases into their vulnerability management cycle, prioritising critical patches for systems that process payments, customer data, or trading activity.
                      First seen: 13 May 2026
                      Path Containment Check
                      A security control that verifies a requested file path stays within an approved directory, preventing attackers from navigating up the folder structure to reach sensitive files.
                      FinServ context: A flawed path containment check using `str.startswith()` without a trailing separator could allow an attacker at a bank to read configuration files containing database credentials or API keys.
                      First seen: 28 May 2026
                      Path Traversal (Storage Directory Context)
                      An attack technique where an attacker manipulates a file path parameter to access files outside the intended directory on a server.
                      FinServ context: CVE-2025-71324 in Flowise allowed unauthenticated attackers to use path traversal in the chatId parameter to read sensitive database files, which in a financial services deployment could expose customer and transaction data.
                      First seen: 26 June 2026
                      Path Traversal Sanitization
                      The process of cleaning and validating file paths to prevent attackers from accessing files outside of intended directories using sequences like "../".
                      FinServ context: Banking applications must implement proper path traversal sanitization to prevent attackers from reading sensitive customer data files or configuration files containing database credentials.
                      First seen: 17 April 2026
                      Path Traversal Sequences
                      Special character combinations like "../" that allow attackers to navigate outside intended directories to access restricted files on a server.
                      FinServ context: Payment processing systems with inadequate input validation could expose sensitive transaction logs or configuration files through path traversal attacks.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Payment Amount Bypass
                      A vulnerability where attackers can manipulate payment values by controlling parameters that the system uses to validate transaction amounts.
                      FinServ context: This could allow fraudsters to process transactions for amounts lower than intended in banking applications or payment processing systems.
                      First seen: 28 March 2026
                      Payment Callback Forgery
                      A technique where an attacker sends fake payment confirmation messages to a system, tricking it into believing a transaction was successful without money actually changing hands.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, a forged payment callback could allow a fraudster to complete a checkout and receive goods or services without making a real payment, directly impacting revenue and fraud loss metrics.
                      First seen: 16 April 2026
                      PayPal Transaction Token
                      A unique identifier generated by PayPal to track and validate specific payment transactions between merchants and customers.
                      FinServ context: Financial applications must properly validate these tokens to prevent attackers from using a low-value payment token to complete high-value transactions fraudulently.
                      First seen: 30 March 2026
                      Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Crypto Trading
                      The direct buying and selling of cryptocurrency between individuals without using a regulated centralised exchange as an intermediary.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's first enforcement operation against illegal P2P crypto trading highlights that operating unregistered P2P crypto services in the UK is a criminal offence under the Money Laundering Regulations.
                      First seen: 23 April 2026
                      PeopleSoft
                      Oracle's suite of enterprise software applications used by large organisations to manage HR, finance, and supply chain processes.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions using PeopleSoft for HR or finance workflows were at direct risk from CVE-2026-35273, which allowed unauthenticated remote code execution against unpatched deployments.
                      First seen: 12 June 2026
                      Permissive Cross-Domain Policy
                      A misconfiguration in a web application that allows scripts from any external website to make requests to that application, potentially exposing sensitive data to unauthorised parties.
                      FinServ context: In financial services API environments, a permissive cross-domain policy on a token endpoint could allow an attacker's website to silently harvest authentication tokens from a customer's browser session.
                      First seen: 21 April 2026
                      Persistence (in AI Agent Attacks)
                      Techniques used by an attacker to maintain ongoing access to a compromised system or AI agent even after the initial intrusion is discovered or the system is restarted.
                      FinServ context: Vulnerabilities in AI agent frameworks like OpenClaw could allow an attacker to embed persistent access within an automated financial workflow, silently exfiltrating data across multiple sessions.
                      First seen: 20 May 2026
                      Personal Information Protection Commission (PIPC)
                      South Korea's national data protection regulator, responsible for investigating breaches and enforcing privacy law against organisations that handle Korean citizens' data.
                      FinServ context: UK financial firms with Korean customers or operations should be aware that the PIPC can impose record fines — as seen in the $409 million Coupang case — similar in intent to the UK ICO's enforcement powers under UK GDPR.
                      First seen: 12 June 2026
                      Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS)
                      A criminal business model where attackers sell ready-made phishing toolkits, infrastructure, and support to other criminals who lack the technical skills to build their own campaigns.
                      FinServ context: UK financial institutions and their customers are prime targets for PhaaS platforms like Kali365, which lower the barrier for criminals to harvest Microsoft 365 credentials used to access banking and trading systems.
                      First seen: 25 May 2026
                      Pig Butchering
                      A type of fraud where scammers build long-term romantic or investment relationships with victims before convincing them to make increasingly large financial investments in fake schemes.
                      FinServ context: Financial firms must monitor for customers being manipulated into transferring large sums to cryptocurrency platforms or investment accounts as part of pig butchering schemes.
                      First seen: 22 March 2026
                      Pikabot
                      A backdoor malware that provides attackers with persistent remote access to infected systems and is used to deploy additional malicious tools.
                      FinServ context: Pikabot is frequently used as an initial access method in financial services attacks, allowing criminals to establish a foothold before deploying tools like Cobalt Strike for lateral movement through banking networks.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      PinTheft (Linux Privilege Escalation)
                      A specific patched vulnerability in Arch Linux that allows a local attacker with limited permissions to gain full root (administrator) access to the system.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms running Linux on internal servers or trading infrastructure must patch privilege escalation flaws promptly, as an attacker with any foothold could gain complete system control.
                      First seen: 21 May 2026
                      Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)
                      Encryption algorithms designed to remain secure against attacks from quantum computers, which can break most encryption methods in widespread use today.
                      FinServ context: UK financial institutions processing long-lived data such as pension records or mortgage contracts need to begin migrating to post-quantum cryptography now, because data harvested today by adversaries could be decrypted once quantum computers mature — a threat sometimes called "harvest now, decrypt later."
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      PowerDuke
                      A backdoor malware that is typically delivered through malicious Microsoft Office documents containing embedded macros.
                      FinServ context: PowerDuke represents a common attack vector against financial services employees who regularly receive and open Office documents from clients, suppliers, and business partners.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Principal Firm Responsibility
                      The legal and regulatory obligation a directly authorised firm holds for ensuring its appointed representatives comply with FCA rules, including client money protections.
                      FinServ context: The Sapia case demonstrates that a principal firm can face FCA censure and significant financial liability if it fails to implement adequate oversight and segregation of duties over the client money activities of its appointed representatives.
                      First seen: 05 May 2026
                      Principal-Appointed Representative Relationship
                      A regulatory arrangement where a regulated firm (the principal) takes legal responsibility for the activities of another firm (the appointed representative) that acts on its behalf.
                      FinServ context: In the Sapia/WealthTek case, Sapia's responsibility for client money extended to WealthTek's activities because it had appointed WealthTek as its representative, meaning failures by the representative exposed the principal to regulatory censure.
                      First seen: 04 May 2026
                      Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs)
                      A group of tools and methods that allow organisations to process or share data while protecting personal information, for example by anonymising or encrypting it so individuals cannot be identified.
                      FinServ context: Ant International's Alipay+ upgrade uses PETs to protect customer payment data during transactions, a model increasingly relevant to UK financial firms handling sensitive customer data under GDPR.
                      First seen: 25 April 2026
                      Privacy-Preserving Identity Verification
                      A method of confirming who someone is without exposing or transmitting their raw personal data, using cryptographic techniques so only a pass or fail result is shared rather than the underlying identity documents.
                      FinServ context: UK financial firms subject to Know Your Customer (KYC) regulations can use privacy-preserving identity verification on blockchain platforms to meet anti-money laundering obligations without storing sensitive customer data in multiple locations.
                      First seen: 22 May 2026
                      Private Credit
                      Lending provided by non-bank institutions — such as private funds or asset managers — directly to companies, outside of public debt markets or traditional bank loans.
                      FinServ context: UK financial regulators are increasingly scrutinising private credit markets for systemic risk, as stress in this sector could affect the broader financial system and the firms that invest in or originate these instruments.
                      First seen: 12 May 2026
                      Private Markets / Private Credit
                      Investment in assets such as loans or company equity that are
                      Privilege Escalation
                      A security vulnerability that allows a user to gain higher access rights or permissions than they should normally have.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, privilege escalation could allow a regular banking application user to gain administrative access to customer accounts or sensitive financial data.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Privilege Escalation (Zero-Day Exploitation)
                      An attack where an adversary exploits an unknown, unpatched software flaw to gain higher-level system permissions than they are supposed to have before the vendor has issued
                      Professional Indemnity Insurance (PII)
                      A regulatory requirement for certain financial advice firms to hold insurance that compensates clients if they suffer loss due to negligent or improper professional advice.
                      FinServ context: A firm's compliance system should flag or block regulated advice activities — such as DB pension transfer recommendations — if the adviser's professional indemnity insurance record has lapsed or is absent.
                      First seen: 13 May 2026
                      Promiscuous Mode
                      A network interface configuration that allows a device to capture and inspect all network traffic, not just traffic intended for that device.
                      FinServ context: Attackers may use promiscuous mode on compromised systems in financial networks to intercept sensitive data like trading information or customer credentials passing over the network.
                      First seen: 23 March 2026
                      Prompt Infiltration
                      A technique where malicious instructions are inserted into data sources with the intent that they will later be processed by an AI system.
                      FinServ context: Attackers could plant malicious prompts in customer service databases that later cause AI chatbots to leak sensitive financial information or perform unauthorized transactions.
                      First seen: 26 March 2026
                      Prompt Injection
                      An attack where malicious instructions are inserted into AI system prompts to manipulate the AI's behavior or extract sensitive information.
                      FinServ context: Attackers could use prompt injection against a bank's AI customer service bot to trick it into revealing other customers' account details or bypassing security protocols.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Prompt Injection (in Agentic AI)
                      An attack where malicious instructions hidden in content that an AI agent reads are interpreted as legitimate commands, causing the agent to take harmful or unintended actions.
                      FinServ context: Prompt injection vulnerabilities in tools like Salesforce Agentforce and Microsoft Copilot could allow attackers to leak sensitive customer financial data processed by those AI systems.
                      First seen: 27 April 2026
                      Prompt Template
                      A pre-defined text structure used to format instructions or inputs that are passed into an AI language model.
                      FinServ context: In a financial services AI assistant, a poorly validated prompt template could be manipulated by an attacker to extract confidential customer data or bypass intended model behaviour.
                      First seen: 16 April 2026
                      Proof-of-Concept (PoC) Exploit
                      A working demonstration, usually created by a researcher, that shows a vulnerability is real and exploitable, without necessarily causing harm, in order to prove the risk exists.
                      FinServ context: The Backslash team published a PoC showing how an MCP server could be weaponised for indirect prompt injection against Cursor, giving financial security teams concrete evidence of the attack path to assess in their own AI development environments.
                      First seen: 29 April 2026
                      Proxy Admin (LLM Proxy Context)
                      The highest-privilege administrator role within an LLM proxy service, granting unrestricted access to all users, API keys, models, teams, and logged data within that system.
                      FinServ context: If a financial services AI deployment uses a proxy layer to manage LLM access, an attacker who escalates to proxy admin could read all prompt history, potentially exposing sensitive customer information submitted to the model.
                      First seen: 22 May 2026
                      Proxy Delivery Mode
                      A file serving method where a server acts as an intermediary, loading requested content into memory before forwarding it to the client.
                      FinServ context: Financial document management systems using proxy delivery could be vulnerable to memory exhaustion attacks if attackers request large files with unbounded range headers.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA)
                      The UK regulatory body, part of the Bank of England, responsible for the prudential supervision of banks, insurers, and major investment firms to ensure their financial safety and soundness.
                      FinServ context: The PRA worked alongside the FCA to reform the SM&CR, aiming to reduce compliance costs for regulated financial services firms while maintaining senior manager accountability.
                      First seen: 29 April 2026
                      Public Nonce (Broken Permission Check)
                      A randomly generated token embedded in a webpage that is intended to verify a legitimate request, but when made publicly accessible it fails to restrict who can call sensitive API endpoints.
                      FinServ context: A REST endpoint at a financial services firm's customer portal accepting a public nonce for permission checks could allow unauthenticated attackers to perform bulk data operations without any login.
                      First seen: 28 May 2026
                      public-facing application
                      Any software system that accepts input directly from internet users without requiring them to authenticate first.
                      FinServ context: Bank websites, mobile apps, and customer portals are public-facing applications that could be targeted with malicious prompts intended to poison AI systems that later process the data.
                      First seen: 31 March 2026
                      Public-facing application infiltration
                      The practice of injecting malicious prompts into publicly accessible applications with the intent that AI systems will process them later.
                      FinServ context: Attackers might inject malicious prompts into a bank's customer feedback system, which could later be processed by AI tools used by support staff, potentially compromising internal systems.
                      Pull Request Poisoning
                      An attack technique where a malicious contributor submits a code change to an open source project that appears legitimate but contains hidden harmful code or exploits CI/CD pipeline permissions.
                      FinServ context: A financial services DevOps team consuming open source libraries from major projects such as those used by Apache or Google could inadvertently incorporate compromised code if pull request review processes are not rigorous.
                      First seen: 24 June 2026
                      PyInterpreter
                      A software component that executes Python code dynamically at runtime, often used in applications that need to run user-provided scripts.
                      FinServ context: Vulnerabilities in PyInterpreter components within financial AI systems could allow attackers to execute malicious code and manipulate trading algorithms or customer data processing.
                      First seen: 06 April 2026
                      PyPI (Python Package Index)
                      The official online repository where developers publish and download Python software packages for use in their projects.
                      FinServ context: Attackers targeted PyPI packages linked to AI companies as part of a supply chain campaign, meaning a financial firm's Python-based ML pipelines could inadvertently install compromised code.
                      First seen: 18 May 2026
                      PyTorch / TensorFlow / JAX
                      Open-source software libraries (frameworks) used by developers to build and train machine learning models, providing pre-built mathematical building blocks so engineers do not have to write everything from scratch.
                      FinServ context: A bank using these frameworks to build fraud-detection models must patch them promptly, since a compromised deep learning framework could corrupt model outputs or exfiltrate training data containing customer transactions.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      Q
                      Qilin Ransomware
                      A ransomware-as-a-service group that encrypts victims' files and threatens to publish stolen data unless a ransom is paid, recently linked to the exploitation of a Check Point VPN zero-day vulnerability.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms are high-value targets for Qilin because the sensitivity of financial data increases the pressure to pay a ransom quickly to avoid regulatory and reputational consequences.
                      First seen: 09 June 2026
                      Qualifying Stablecoin
                      A type of cryptocurrency whose value is pegged to a stable asset (such as a fiat currency) and which falls within a specific legal definition that brings it under regulatory oversight.
                      FinServ context: From October 2027, UK firms issuing or dealing in qualifying stablecoins will need FCA authorisation, creating new compliance and security obligations around cryptoasset custody and issuance.
                      First seen: 18 April 2026
                      Quantum-Resilience-as-a-Service (QRaaS)
                      A cloud-delivered security service that upgrades a financial institution's encryption to withstand attacks from quantum computers without requiring a full replacement of existing systems.
                      FinServ context: UK banks and payment networks face a future threat from quantum computing that could break current encryption standards, making QRaaS a practical option for strengthening cryptographic defences incrementally.
                      First seen: 18 April 2026
                      Query Fan-out
                      When a single request triggers multiple additional queries or API calls to different services or databases.
                      FinServ context: In banking systems, attackers might craft prompts that cause an AI agent to perform excessive database lookups across multiple account systems, overwhelming the infrastructure.
                      First seen: 01 April 2026
                      R
                      Race Condition
                      A security flaw that occurs when multiple processes access shared resources simultaneously without proper coordination, creating unpredictable results.
                      FinServ context: In banking systems, race conditions could allow attackers to manipulate transaction processing timing to transfer funds multiple times or bypass account balance checks.
                      First seen: 15 April 2026
                      Range header
                      An HTTP request component that specifies which portion of a file or data the client wants to download.
                      FinServ context: Malicious Range headers can be used to exhaust memory in financial applications that serve large files like transaction reports or audit logs.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Ransomware Affiliate
                      A time-limited collaborative event where regulators, firms, and technologists work together to build prototypes that explore how technology can solve regulatory or market challenges.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's Smart Data Accelerator TechSprints used a digital testing environment to let firms demonstrate open finance solutions that could inform real policy, giving junior engineers a direct route to shaping regulatory technical standards.
                      First seen: 06 May 2026
                      Ransomware Affiliate Model
                      A business arrangement where a ransomware group provides its attack tools and infrastructure to third-party criminal partners in exchange for a share of any ransom payments collected.
                      FinServ context: The affiliate model lowers the technical barrier for attacking financial services firms, meaning a highly capable ransomware toolkit can be wielded by less sophisticated criminals who simply recruit themselves to an established gang.
                      First seen: 14 May 2026
                      Ransomware Affiliate Model (Organisational Structure)
                      A business-like arrangement where a ransomware group recruits outside criminals to carry out attacks in exchange for a share of the ransom proceeds, with the core group providing the malware and infrastructure.
                      FinServ context: Understanding the affiliate model helps financial services security teams appreciate that ransomware groups targeting banks or insurers may be loosely connected networks rather than tight-knit teams, making attribution and takedown more difficult.
                      First seen: 15 May 2026
                      Ransomware Affiliate Revenue Share
                      A financial arrangement in ransomware-as-a-service operations where hackers who carry out attacks receive a percentage of the ransom paid by victims, with the remainder going to the group's operators.
                      FinServ context: Groups offering high affiliate payouts — such as 90% splits — can rapidly recruit skilled attackers, increasing the volume and sophistication of ransomware threats faced by financial institutions.
                      First seen: 11 June 2026
                      Ransomware Negotiator
                      A supervised programme where AI systems are tested in real-world conditions with live data and users, under regulatory oversight, before or during broader deployment.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's AI Live Testing initiative allows firms such as Barclays and Lloyds to identify safety and bias risks in production AI systems, helping demonstrate to the regulator that AI deployment meets consumer protection standards.
                      First seen: 23 April 2026
                      Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS)
                      A business model where ransomware developers rent or licence their malicious software to other criminals, who carry out attacks and share the ransom proceeds with the developers.
                      FinServ context: The RaaS model lowers the technical barrier to attacking financial institutions, meaning even non-technical criminals can deploy sophisticated ransomware like ALPHV against banks and payment processors.
                      First seen: 21 April 2026
                      Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) Organisational Structure
                      A form of cyberattack where threat actors use AI agents to automatically generate custom hacking tools and techniques on demand during a live campaign, rather than relying on pre-built malware.
                      FinServ context: Vibe hacking lowers the skill barrier for attacking financial institutions, because criminals targeting banks in Latin America were observed using AI to create bespoke attack tools in real time without needing deep technical expertise.
                      First seen: 18 May 2026
                      Rate Limiting
                      A control that restricts how many requests or connections a system will accept from a single source within a given time window, preventing it from being overwhelmed.
                      FinServ context: Financial services APIs handling payment requests or authentication attempts should enforce rate limiting to prevent attackers from flooding systems and causing outages that affect customers.
                      First seen: 06 May 2026
                      RCE (Remote Code Execution)
                      A vulnerability that allows an attacker to run arbitrary code on a target system from a remote location without physical access.
                      FinServ context: Represents a critical threat to financial institutions as it can lead to complete system compromise, allowing attackers to manipulate transactions or steal sensitive financial data.
                      First seen: 03 April 2026
                      Real-Time Bond Consolidated Tape
                      A security flaw in enterprise resource planning software — large business management systems handling finance, HR, and operations — that attackers can exploit to access or steal an organisation's core data.
                      FinServ context: The Oracle ERP zero-day exploited by ShinyHunters demonstrates that financial services firms running Oracle platforms for financial reporting or customer screening face significant data theft risk when patches are not applied promptly.
                      First seen: 29 June 2026
                      Real-Time Scam Detection (Agentic)
                      A configuration where an AI agent is granted access to far more systems, data, or actions than it strictly needs to complete its assigned tasks.
                      FinServ context: In financial services, an AI agent with broad permissions that is compromised via prompt injection could access customer account data, initiate transactions, or exfiltrate credentials across multiple internal systems.
                      First seen: 11 June 2026
                      Redirect-Following Without Revalidation
                      A behaviour where software automatically follows HTTP redirects to new addresses without checking whether each new destination is permitted by security rules.
                      FinServ context: In financial reporting platforms, this flaw can allow an attacker to chain redirects from a trusted URL to an internal banking system, potentially exposing sensitive data.
                      First seen: 11 June 2026
                      Redress Scheme
                      A formal regulatory programme requiring firms to identify affected customers, calculate fair compensation, and make payments for a specific category of past consumer harm.
                      FinServ context: Engineers at financial services firms must understand redress schemes because they often require building or auditing data pipelines that identify eligible customers and calculate compensation at scale.
                      First seen: 18 April 2026
                      Ref Parameter
                      A Python code execution component that allows applications to run Python scripts dynamically within larger systems.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions using AI tools with PyInterpreter functionality face code injection risks where malicious Python code could access sensitive customer data or trading systems.
                      First seen: 06 April 2026
                      Referral Parser
                      A software component that processes and analyzes information about where web traffic originated from.
                      FinServ context: Financial services websites use referral parsers to understand customer acquisition channels, but poorly designed parsers can become injection points for malicious code.
                      First seen: 17 April 2026
                      Regex-Based Validation
                      A security control that uses regular expressions to check if user input matches expected patterns before processing.
                      FinServ context: Poorly implemented regex validation in financial APIs can be bypassed by attackers to execute commands that manipulate payment processing or account data.
                      First seen: 02 April 2026
                      Regulatory Filing
                      A formal document that a company is legally required to submit to a financial regulator or stock exchange disclosing material events, financial results, or significant incidents.
                      FinServ context: When Bajaj Auto disclosed its ransomware incident via a regulatory filing, it illustrates how cyber incidents in financial and listed firms trigger mandatory disclosure obligations, including requirements that apply to UK-listed firms under FCA rules.
                      First seen: 25 June 2026
                      Remote Access Trojan (RAT)
                      A category of malicious software that secretly gives an attacker full remote control of an infected device, including the ability to view files, execute commands, and monitor activity.
                      FinServ context: RATs targeting financial services employees could allow attackers to silently monitor trading terminals, extract client data, or initiate fraudulent transactions.
                      First seen: 27 April 2026
                      Request Smuggling
                      An attack technique where differences in how web servers and proxies parse HTTP requests allow attackers to bypass security controls.
                      FinServ context: This could enable attackers to bypass fraud detection systems or access controls protecting financial APIs and services.
                      First seen: 28 March 2026
                      Residential Proxy
                      A service that routes internet traffic through real consumer devices, making malicious or automated traffic appear to come from legitimate home internet connections.
                      FinServ context: Fraudsters use residential proxy networks to conduct account takeover attempts against banking portals, as the traffic appears to originate from genuine customers rather than data centres.
                      First seen: 19 June 2026
                      Resource Hijacking
                      An attack where a threat actor secretly uses a victim organisation's computing infrastructure — such as cloud servers or GPUs — for the attacker's own purposes, typically cryptocurrency mining or running AI workloads.
                      FinServ context: A bank running large AI inference workloads in the cloud is an attractive target for resource hijacking, as the high baseline of compute usage can mask unauthorised usage and inflate cloud bills before detection.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      Resource-Intensive Queries
                      A specific sequence of network traffic or data packets that triggers a backdoor or hidden functionality when detected.
                      FinServ context: Malicious actors could embed secret knock sequences in financial transaction data to activate dormant backdoors in payment processing systems without triggering standard security monitoring.
                      First seen: 06 April 2026
                      REST API Endpoint
                      A specific URL path in a web service that accepts HTTP requests and returns data in a standardized format.
                      FinServ context: Unsecured REST API endpoints in financial management systems can expose customer account information or transaction histories to unauthorized users.
                      First seen: 02 April 2026
                      Retirement Interest-Only Mortgage
                      A mortgage product designed for older borrowers where only the interest is repaid monthly, with the loan repaid when the property is sold, typically upon death or entry into care.
                      FinServ context: A junior engineer building affordability assessment tooling for a lender must understand that retirement interest-only mortgages have distinct regulatory treatment under FCA mortgage rules compared to standard repayment products.
                      First seen: 18 June 2026
                      Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
                      An AI technique that combines a large language model with a searchable database to provide more accurate and up-to-date responses.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions use RAG systems to help customer service chatbots access current account information and regulatory documents when responding to client queries.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Retrieval Content Crafting
                      A Chinese state-sponsored advanced persistent threat group known for conducting both espionage and financially motivated attacks, targeting cloud infrastructure and software supply chains globally.
                      FinServ context: APT41's targeting of major cloud platforms such as AWS and Azure using typosquatting to hide command-and-control traffic poses a direct risk to financial firms relying on cloud infrastructure for core banking and data services.
                      First seen: 25 April 2026
                      RLIKE-Based Boolean SQL Injection
                      A specific SQL injection technique that uses the RLIKE operator to create true/false conditions for extracting database information character by character.
                      FinServ context: This attack method could be used against financial applications to slowly extract customer account details, transaction histories, or authentication credentials from banking databases.
                      First seen: 06 April 2026
                      RMM Tool (Remote Monitoring and Management)
                      Software used by IT teams or managed service providers to remotely monitor, manage, and control systems across an organisation's infrastructure.
                      FinServ context: A critical RCE vulnerability in Bomgar RMM illustrates how attackers can exploit trusted IT management tools already present in financial services environments to spread ransomware across connected systems.
                      First seen: 04 May 2026
                      RoguePlanet
                      The highest level of access on a Windows computer, allowing a process or attacker to read, modify, or delete any file, change security settings, and install software without restriction.
                      FinServ context: If an attacker gains SYSTEM privileges on a workstation inside a financial firm's network, they can harvest credentials, disable security tooling, and move laterally to core banking or trading systems.
                      First seen: 10 June 2026
                      Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
                      A security method that restricts what actions users can perform in a system based on their assigned job role, so a standard user cannot do things only an administrator is permitted to do.
                      FinServ context: In financial services AI platforms, weak RBAC implementations — such as the LiteLLM vulnerability where users could modify their own role field — can allow a low-privileged employee to gain access to all customer data, model configurations, and prompt history.
                      First seen: 22 May 2026
                      Rolling Release (Software)
                      A software development model where updates are continuously published without fixed version numbers, making it harder to pinpoint exactly which version contains a vulnerability.
                      FinServ context: Several MCP server CVEs affecting financial AI tooling were published with no specific affected version listed because the projects use rolling releases, complicating patch management for security teams.
                      First seen: 28 April 2026
                      Root Privilege Escalation
                      The process by which an attacker gains the highest possible level of system access, allowing them to read, modify, or delete anything on the affected machine.
                      FinServ context: A root privilege escalation vulnerability in network management software used by a bank could give an attacker complete control over the infrastructure managing inter-branch communications.
                      First seen: 05 June 2026
                      ROP Chain
                      Return-Oriented Programming chain is an exploitation technique that chains together small code snippets (gadgets) already present in memory to execute malicious operations.
                      FinServ context: ROP chains can bypass modern security protections in banking applications to execute unauthorized transactions or access sensitive customer data.
                      First seen: 13 April 2026
                      Roundcube Webmail Exploitation
                      A technique where attackers exploit security flaws in the open-source Roundcube email platform to run malicious code on a victim's system simply by having them open an email.
                      FinServ context: Financial services staff using webmail platforms face similar zero-interaction risks, where opening a legitimate-looking email could trigger code execution without any further user action.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      Row Level Security (RLS)
                      A database feature that restricts which rows a user can read or modify based on rules tied to their identity, so users only see data they are permitted to access.
                      FinServ context: A UK bank or payments firm using a multi-tenant database to store customer records would rely on RLS to ensure one customer cannot query another's transaction history.
                      First seen: 22 June 2026
                      RSSReaderComponent / SearXNG Component (Langflow)
                      Built-in components within the Langflow AI platform that fetch content from external URLs, originally designed for reading RSS feeds or performing web searches within automated AI workflows.
                      FinServ context: Legacy or unpatched tool components in AI workflow platforms used by financial services firms can expose internal networks to SSRF attacks, particularly dangerous where those networks hold sensitive customer or transaction data.
                      First seen: 01 July 2026
                      rxgk Module
                      A kernel-level authentication and encryption module in Linux used to secure network communications within distributed file systems.
                      FinServ context: A privilege escalation flaw in the rxgk module could allow an attacker who has already reached a Linux server — such as one hosting a bank's internal data platform — to gain full root control of that system.
                      First seen: 18 May 2026
                      S
                      S/4HANA
                      SAP's flagship cloud-based enterprise resource planning (ERP) platform used by large organisations to manage finance, procurement, and business operations in a single integrated system.
                      FinServ context: Critical vulnerabilities in S/4HANA are of particular concern to financial services firms that use it to manage core accounting and regulatory reporting, where exploitation could lead to data manipulation or unauthorised transactions.
                      First seen: 13 May 2026
                      Safety Alignment
                      The process of training an AI model so that its outputs and behaviour remain consistent with intended human values, policies, and ethical boundaries.
                      FinServ context: Poor safety alignment in a financial services AI model could allow it to recommend unsuitable investment products or circumvent regulatory obligations such as FCA conduct rules.
                      First seen: 01 May 2026
                      Safety Mechanism (AI Model Context)
                      A built-in control within an AI model designed to prevent it from producing harmful, dangerous, or policy-violating outputs.
                      FinServ context: Attackers targeting financial services AI systems may attempt to bypass safety mechanisms in LLMs used for customer service or fraud detection, causing the model to leak sensitive data or generate misleading advice.
                      First seen: 01 June 2026
                      Salesforce Agentforce
                      A platform from Salesforce that allows organisations to build and deploy AI agents capable of performing tasks and interacting with business data on behalf of users.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms deploying Agentforce to handle customer queries or process data must ensure prompt injection vulnerabilities are patched to prevent attackers leaking sensitive client information.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      SAML (Security Assertion Markup Language)
                      An XML-based standard that allows identity information to be shared between an identity provider and a service provider to enable single sign-on.
                      FinServ context: Banks and insurers commonly use SAML to federate identity across internal and third-party platforms, so a malformed SAML payload causing server thread exhaustion could take down authentication services firm-wide.
                      First seen: 20 May 2026
                      Sanctions Systems and Controls
                      The internal policies, processes, and technology a financial firm uses to identify, screen, and block transactions or relationships involving sanctioned parties.
                      FinServ context: The FCA found repeated gaps in firms' sanctions systems and controls during reviews, meaning some institutions were at risk of inadvertently processing transactions that should have been blocked.
                      First seen: 05 June 2026
                      Sandbox Escape
                      A security breach where malicious code breaks out of a restricted execution environment designed to isolate it from the host system.
                      FinServ context: Sandbox escape vulnerabilities in financial trading platforms could allow attackers to access real market data or execute unauthorized trades.
                      First seen: 02 April 2026
                      Sandbox Escape (Node.js VM Context)
                      A technique where an attacker breaks out of a supposedly isolated code execution environment to access and control the underlying host system.
                      FinServ context: If a UK bank's developer tooling or automation pipeline uses Node.js VM sandboxes to evaluate customer-supplied logic, a sandbox escape could give attackers full control of the host server running that service.
                      First seen: 03 June 2026
                      Sandbox Roots
                      A programming error where data written to a memory buffer exceeds the buffer's boundaries and overwrites adjacent memory locations on the program's call stack.
                      FinServ context: Stack buffer overflows in financial trading applications could allow attackers to manipulate transaction data or bypass authentication controls by overwriting return addresses.
                      First seen: 13 April 2026
                      Sanitization Issue
                      A software vulnerability where input or output data is not properly cleaned or validated, allowing an attacker to sneak in malicious content that the application then processes as legitimate instructions.
                      FinServ context: A sanitization issue in an AI-based filesystem tool allowed prompt injection to escape the sandbox, illustrating how poor input validation in AI products can lead to full system compromise in a financial services environment.
                      First seen: 25 April 2026
                      SAP Commerce Cloud
                      A cloud-based e-commerce platform made by SAP that manages online sales, customer accounts, and transactions.
                      FinServ context: Financial services firms using SAP Commerce Cloud for customer-facing portals or product sales must apply critical patches promptly to protect customer data and payment flows.
                      First seen: 10 June 2026
                      SAP NetWeaver
                      A technology platform made by SAP that acts as the foundation for running SAP business applications and integrating different systems together.
                      FinServ context: Critical vulnerabilities in SAP NetWeaver are high-priority for financial services firms, as many banks and insurers use SAP to run core finance and compliance processes.
                      First seen: 10 June 2026
                      Satellite data files
                      Data files containing information transmitted from or to satellite communication systems.
                      FinServ context: Financial firms using satellite communications for remote trading offices or disaster recovery sites may process satellite data files that could contain malicious payloads.
                      First seen: 07 April 2026
                      Scattered Spider
                      A loosely organised cybercriminal collective known for using social engineering and SIM-swapping techniques to compromise large organisations and steal cryptocurrency.
                      FinServ context: Scattered Spider's tactics of impersonating IT helpdesk staff pose a direct threat to financial services firms, where employees may be manipulated into resetting credentials or bypassing security controls.
                      First seen: 21 April 2026
                      Scope Boundary Bypass
                      A security flaw where an application incorrectly grants higher-level permissions than what was originally authorized to a user or process.
                      FinServ context: In banking APIs, scope boundary bypass could allow a read-only trading application to execute unauthorized transactions by gaining admin-level API access.
                      First seen: 11 April 2026
                      Scope Validation
                      The security process of checking whether a user or system has the appropriate permissions to access specific resources or perform certain actions.
                      FinServ context: In financial systems, insufficient scope validation could allow junior staff to approve high-value transactions or access sensitive customer data beyond their authorization level.
                      First seen: 01 April 2026
                      Scope-Upgrade Requests
                      Requests to expand the permissions or access level of an authenticated session or device beyond its initially granted privileges.
                      FinServ context: In banking systems, unauthorized scope upgrades could allow attackers to escalate from read-only access to administrative control over payment processing systems.
                      First seen: 10 April 2026
                      Scraping-Based Prompt Injection
                      An attack where a malicious prompt is hidden inside a webpage that an AI agent is instructed to scrape, causing the agent to execute harmful commands on behalf of the attacker.
                      FinServ context: An AI assistant used by a financial analyst to gather market data from the web could be hijacked via a scraping-based prompt injection, potentially exfiltrating internal research or credentials.
                      First seen: 03 June 2026
                      SearchLeak
                      A chain of vulnerabilities that, when combined, allows an attacker to steal data from a victim's cloud storage or email by sending them a specially crafted URL.
                      FinServ context: In a financial services firm using Microsoft 365 Copilot, SearchLeak could allow an attacker to silently extract sensitive customer data from SharePoint or OneDrive with a single malicious link sent to an employee.
                      First seen: 16 June 2026
                      Secondary Markets
                      Financial markets where investors buy and sell previously issued securities such as shares, bonds, and derivatives, rather than buying directly from the issuer.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's advisory committee on secondary markets helps shape regulation of equities, derivatives, and fixed income trading to protect market integrity in UK financial services.
                      First seen: 04 May 2026
                      Secret Knock
                      A specific sequence of network packets or traffic patterns that triggers dormant malware to activate and execute its payload.
                      FinServ context: Advanced persistent threats in financial networks may use secret knocks to remain undetected until receiving activation signals from command and control servers.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Security Definer Function
                      A database function that runs with the permissions of the account that created it, rather than the account calling it, meaning it can access resources the caller normally cannot.
                      FinServ context: In financial services databases, a misconfigured SECURITY DEFINER function handling billing or account operations could allow an authenticated user to manipulate payment or credit data belonging to other organisations.
                      First seen: 22 June 2026
                      Security Metrics
                      Measurable values used to assess how well an organisation's security controls, processes, and teams are performing over time.
                      FinServ context: Poorly chosen security metrics in a financial services SOC can create a false sense of security, masking genuine exposure to fraud, data breaches, or regulatory breaches.
                      First seen: 27 April 2026
                      Security Metrics (SOC)
                      Quantitative measures used to assess the effectiveness and performance of a security operations centre, such as mean time to detect or number of incidents resolved.
                      FinServ context: Choosing the wrong SOC metrics — for example, measuring ticket volume rather than detection quality — can give a false sense of security in a financial firm and mask genuine gaps in threat coverage.
                      First seen: 28 April 2026
                      Segregation of Duties
                      A control principle that requires different people to be responsible for different parts of a sensitive process, so no single person can carry out and conceal fraud or errors alone.
                      FinServ context: The FCA's censure of Sapia highlights that allowing the same staff to authorise and audit client money payments violates segregation of duties, a fundamental control required under FCA CASS rules.
                      First seen: 24 April 2026
                      Segregation of Duties (Client Money Context)
                      The practice of separating financial responsibilities so that no single person can both authorise and verify the same transaction or account activity.
                      FinServ context: The FCA found Sapia failed to segregate duties properly — staff who could move money from client accounts were also the ones checking those accounts, increasing fraud risk.
                      First seen: 28 April 2026
                      Self-Employed Affordability Assessment
                      A self-propagating malicious program that uses artificial intelligence to adapt its behaviour, identify new targets, and spread between systems without human intervention.
                      FinServ context: An AI worm targeting a large financial institution's internal AI agent infrastructure could autonomously move between systems, exfiltrating customer data or disrupting payment processing before defenders can respond.
                      First seen: 16 June 2026
                      Self-Hosted Git Service
                      A version of Git repository software that an organisation installs and operates on its own infrastructure rather than relying on a third-party provider, giving full control over source code storage.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions may prefer self-hosted Git services to keep proprietary risk models and source code within their own regulatory boundary, but must ensure these services are patched and not directly exposed to the internet.
                      First seen: 29 May 2026
                      Self-Propagating Worm (npm Ecosystem)
                      A type of malicious code that automatically copies and spreads itself across software packages or systems without human intervention.
                      FinServ context: A self-propagating worm infecting open source npm packages used in a bank's internal development toolchain could steal developer credentials and compromise the firm's software supply chain before the infection is detected.
                      First seen: 13 May 2026
                      Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SM&CR)
                      A UK regulatory framework that assigns clear personal accountability to senior individuals at financial services firms for the areas of the business they manage.
                      FinServ context: UK financial firms must ensure that senior managers are formally approved and that their responsibilities are documented, meaning a security incident could result in personal regulatory liability for named individuals.
                      First seen: 25 April 2026
                      Sensitive Information Removal Before Storage
                      A secure development practice requiring that credentials, passwords, or personal data are stripped out of logs, error messages, and stored records before they are saved or transmitted.
                      FinServ context: Failure to remove sensitive information before storage — as described in CVE-2024-43384 — could expose root credentials in a financial firm's device management logs, enabling an attacker to take full control of critical infrastructure.
                      First seen: 08 May 2026
                      Serialized Payload
                      Malicious data that has been converted into a format for storage or transmission but contains hidden code that executes when processed.
                      FinServ context: Banking APIs receiving serialized transaction data could be exploited if the payload contains code designed to manipulate account balances or transfer funds.
                      First seen: 30 March 2026
                      Series C Extension
                      An additional round of investment added on top of an existing Series C funding round, used to raise more capital without starting a completely new funding round.
                      FinServ context: A $25 million Series C extension in an AI fraud and compliance platform signals growing investor confidence in automated financial crime detection tools used by banks and fintechs.
                      First seen: 01 June 2026
                      Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
                      A vulnerability that tricks a server into making HTTP requests to internal systems or external URLs that the attacker specifies, rather than the server choosing what to connect to itself.
                      FinServ context: In a financial services API or picture-upload service, an SSRF flaw could allow an attacker to reach internal payment systems or cloud metadata endpoints that should never be publicly accessible.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      Serverless Computing
                      A cloud computing model where code runs in managed containers without the user needing to provision or manage servers.
                      FinServ context: Financial institutions using serverless functions for transaction processing must secure these environments as attackers can abuse them to run cryptocurrency mining or data theft operations.
                      First seen: 22 March 2026
                      Serverless Execution
                      A technique where attackers abuse cloud services that run code without managing servers (like AWS Lambda) to execute malicious commands.
                      FinServ context: An attacker could deploy malicious code through Azure Functions to process payment data or cryptocurrency mining operations within a bank's cloud infrastructure without maintaining persistent servers.
                      First seen: 24 March 2026
                      Service Principals
                      Non-human identities in cloud environments that applications and services use to authenticate and access resources automatically.
                      FinServ context: Attackers may create rogue service principals in a bank's Azure environment to maintain persistent access to customer data systems without using stolen employee accounts.
                      First seen: 22 March 2026
                      Session and Authorisation Token Exposure
                      A vulnerability where active login tokens or access credentials are written to logs or indexes in plain readable text rather than being masked or encrypted.
                      FinServ context: In a financial services SIEM environment, session tokens visible in Splunk internal indexes could allow an insider threat or compromised admin account to hijack other users' authenticated sessions.
                      First seen: 16 April 2026
                      Session Fixation
                      An attack where an attacker forces a user's web session to use a known session identifier, so the attacker can take over the session after the victim logs in.
                      FinServ context: A session fixation flaw in an identity provider like Keycloak could allow an attacker to hijack a bank employee's authenticated session, gaining access to internal financial systems without needing their password.
                      First seen: 20 May 2026
                      Session Hijacking
                      A technique where an attacker steals or forges a valid session token to impersonate an authenticated user without needing their password.
                      FinServ context: A junior engineer at a UK bank should understand that session hijacking can allow attackers to take over a customer's active online banking session, bypassing login controls entirely.
                      First seen: 18 June 2026
                      Settlement Fund (Data Breach Context)
                      A method lenders use to calculate how much someone can afford to borrow when their earnings fluctuate rather than being a fixed monthly salary.
                      FinServ context: The FCA is proposing that mortgage lenders adopt more flexible variable income affordability assessments to avoid unfairly excluding self-employed borrowers whose finances are sound but irregular.
                      First seen: 15 June 2026
                      Shadow AI
                      The use of AI tools and services by employees without the knowledge or approval of the IT or security team, similar to the broader concept of shadow IT.
                      FinServ context: A junior analyst at a bank using an unapproved AI coding assistant to process customer data could inadvertently expose sensitive financial records outside the firm's governed environment.
                      First seen: 30 April 2026
                      Shell Access
                      The ability to interact directly with an operating system's command-line interface, allowing a user to run any program or system command on that machine.
                      FinServ context: The ThreatSonar vulnerability (CVE-2026-5967) shows that if an authenticated attacker gains shell access to an anti-ransomware appliance deployed in a financial institution, they can escalate to root and disable the very controls designed to protect the environment.
                      First seen: 20 April 2026
                      Shell Metacharacters
                      Special characters (such as semicolons, pipes, or backticks) that a command-line interpreter treats as instructions rather than plain text, allowing attackers to inject additional commands into a system.
                      FinServ context: If a financial services application passes user-supplied input directly into a system command without sanitisation, an attacker could use shell metacharacters to execute unauthorised commands on the underlying server.
                      First seen: 24 June 2026
                      Shell Substitution
                      A feature of Unix/Linux shells where a sequence like `$()` inside a command is replaced by the output of whatever command runs inside it, before the main command executes.
                      FinServ context: If a financial services platform passes unvalidated user input into a shell command, an attacker can embed shell substitutions to run arbitrary code on the underlying server.
                      First seen: 30 June 2026
                      ShinyHunters
                      A well-known cybercriminal extortion group that steals large volumes of data from organisations and threatens to publish it publicly unless a ransom is paid.
